content_sections.III Results and Discussion: We first examine the transition amplitudes TJ=0superscript𝑇𝐽0TJ=0 in the complex plane to see how the poles appear. To analyze the coupled-channel effects, we strictly follow our cutoff scheme Λ=mex+600Λsubscript𝑚ex600Lambda=mex+600 MeV for a while. Note that the kernel amplitudes do not contain any singularities since we do not include s𝑠s channel diagrams. By solving the two-body integral equation with a single channel, we can identify which channels give rise to resonant states. Each T𝑇T-matrix element is depicted in the left panel of Fig. . We see that the strong attraction in the u𝑢u-channel exchange generates two poles in the D0​K+superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾D0K+ and D+​K0superscript𝐷superscript𝐾0D+K0 channels. Though the kernel amplitudes for the Ds+​π0→D​K→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜋0𝐷𝐾Ds+pi0to DK and and Ds+​η→D​K→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠𝜂𝐷𝐾Ds+etato DK transitions also incorporate the u𝑢u-channel diagrams, no poles appear in the corresponding T𝑇T-matrix. Nevertheless, the Ds+​π0→D0​K+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜋0superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾Ds+pi0to D0K+ and Ds+​π0→D+​K0→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜋0superscript𝐷superscript𝐾0Ds+pi0to D+K0 contributions show a significant enhacement at the Ds+​π0superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜋0Ds+pi0 threshold energy. No pole structures were found in other T𝑇T matrix elements. We multiply the coupling strength gD​D∗​Msubscript𝑔𝐷superscript𝐷𝑀gDD*M by a control parameter b𝑏b to see the effects. In the right panel of Fig. , we depict the u𝑢u-channel dependence of the TD0​K+→D0​K+J=0superscriptsubscript𝑇→superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾𝐽0TD0K+to D0K+J=0 with b𝑏b varied from b=0.7𝑏0.7b=0.7 to b=1.2𝑏1.2b=1.2. Note that the pole, which arises from the strong attraction in the D0​K+superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾D0K+ kernel amplitude, appears only if the value of b𝑏b is approximately greater than 0.7. As anticipated, the energy corresponding to this pole increases as b𝑏b becomes larger. We now consider the S-wave transition amplitude for the D0​K+→D0​K+→superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾D0K+to D0K+ process. When the D0​K+superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾D0K+ channel is coupled to the D+​K0superscript𝐷superscript𝐾0D+K0 channel, the two poles still remain below the D0​K+superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾D0K+ threshold, as depicted in Fig. . However, once we couple it to one of the non-diagonal amplitudes, only the first pole starts to undergo changes. For example, let us couple the Ds+​π0superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜋0Ds+pi0 amplitude to the D0​K+→D+​K0→superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾superscript𝐷superscript𝐾0D0K+to D+K0 transition. We can scrutinize the coupled-channel effects by varying the coupling strength of the two channels, which is controlled by parameter b𝑏b. As b𝑏b increases, the width of the first pole is rapidly broadened, as shown in Fig. . By setting b=1𝑏1b=1, which corresponds to our final results, the first pole completely disappears. Consequently, we observe only the second pole that remains largely unaffected by the Ds+​π0superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜋0Ds+pi0 channel. Next, we discuss how the Ds+​ηsuperscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠𝜂Ds+eta channel comes into play. If we couple Ds+​π0superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜋0Ds+pi0, D​K𝐷𝐾DK, and Ds+​ηsuperscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠𝜂Ds+eta channels together, the second pole starts to move to the lower energy, which is demonstrated in Fig. . When we introduce all possible coupled channels, the pole is finally positioned at approximately sR=(2261.4−i​0.072)subscript𝑠𝑅2261.4𝑖0.072sqrtsR=(2261.4-i0.072) MeV. The pole position on the real axis Re​sR=2261.4Resubscript𝑠𝑅2261.4Re,sqrtsR=2261.4 MeV is still deviated from the experimental data for the Ds​0∗​superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠02317Ds0* mass. Thus, we need to fit the model parameters, keeping their uncertainties as low as possible. We can itemize how we can proceed to fit the Ds​0∗​superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠02317Ds0* mass: • We can slightly change the cutoff masses listed in Table. • We still have one more free parameter, i. e. , gη​η​σsubscript𝑔𝜂𝜂𝜎getaetasigma. Fortunately, the effect of gη​η​σsubscript𝑔𝜂𝜂𝜎getaetasigma is almost negligible. • We can also consider the effects of the explicit flavor SU symmetry breaking by about 101010, and find that they are also negligible. So, we concentrate on the fitting of the cutoff masses in such a way that the corresponding values are not much deviated from our recipe Λ≃mex+600similar-to-or-equalsΛsubscript𝑚ex600Lambdasimeq mex+600 MeV. As listed in Table, the values of the cutoff masses for the exchanged light mesons are kept to be Λ=mex+600Λsubscript𝑚ex600Lambda=mex+600 MeV, whereas we choose slightly smaller values for the exchanged heavy mesons Λ≈mex+540Λsubscript𝑚ex540Lambdaapprox mex+540 MeV. Finally, we arrive at the physical pole position in the second Riemann sheet: sR=(2317.90−i​0.0593)subscript𝑠𝑅2317.90𝑖0.0593sqrtsR=(2317.90-i0.0593) MeV as shown in Fig. , where we plot the pole corresponding to the Ds​0∗​superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠02317Ds0* meson in the complex plane. The pole for the Ds​0∗​superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠02317Ds0* meson is located approximately 40 MeV below the D​K𝐷𝐾DK thresholds, which indicates that the Ds​0∗​superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠02317Ds0* may be identified as a D​K𝐷𝐾DK molecular statecitebib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib20. To explore the effects of the coupled channels in more detail, we analyze the channel couplings for the channels involved. The transition amplitudes in the vicinity of the pole position sRsubscript𝑠𝑅sqrtsR can be parameterized as Ta​b​(s)=4​π​ga​gbs−sR. subscript𝑇𝑎𝑏𝑠4𝜋subscript𝑔𝑎subscript𝑔𝑏𝑠subscript𝑠𝑅displaystyle Tab(sqrts)=4pifracgagbs-sR. The residue can be extracted by taking the limit s→sR→𝑠subscript𝑠𝑅sto sR as follows: ℛa​b=lims→sR(sR−s)​Ta​b/(4​π). subscriptℛ𝑎𝑏subscript→𝑠subscript𝑠𝑅subscript𝑠𝑅𝑠subscript𝑇𝑎𝑏4𝜋displaystyleRab=limsto sR(sR-s)Tab/(4pi). The channel coupling is defined by ga=ℛa​a,subscript𝑔𝑎subscriptℛ𝑎𝑎displaystyle ga=sqrtRaa, which characterizes the coupling strength of the corresponding resonance with a given channel a𝑎a. We predict the four different channel couplings as follows: gDs+​π0=0.00967+i​0.05294​GeV,gDs+​η=−85.25−i​0.0610​GeV,formulae-sequencesubscript𝑔superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜋00.00967𝑖0.05294GeVsubscript𝑔superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠𝜂85.25𝑖0.0610GeVdisplaystyle gDs+pi0=0.00967+i0.05294,GeV,;;;gDs+eta=-85.25-i0.0610,GeV, gD0​K+=−77.59+i​0.730​GeV,gD+​K0=−80.16−i​0.905​GeV. formulae-sequencesubscript𝑔superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾77.59𝑖0.730GeVsubscript𝑔superscript𝐷superscript𝐾080.16𝑖0.905GeVdisplaystyle gD0K+=-77.59+i0.730,GeV,;;;gD+K0=-80.16-i0.905,GeV. We can also define the corresponding coupling strengths: g1=|gDs+​π0|=5.381×10−2​GeV,subscript𝑔1subscript𝑔superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜋05.381superscript102GeVdisplaystyle g1=|gDs+pi0|=5.381times 10-2,GeV, g2=|gD0​K+|=77.59​GeV,subscript𝑔2subscript𝑔superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾77.59GeVdisplaystyle g2=|gD0K+|=77.59,GeV, g3=|gD+​K0|=80.17​GeV,subscript𝑔3subscript𝑔superscript𝐷superscript𝐾080.17GeVdisplaystyle g3=|gD+K0|=80.17,GeV, g4=|gDs+​η|=85.25​GeV. subscript𝑔4subscript𝑔superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠𝜂85.25GeVdisplaystyle g4=|gDs+eta|=85.25,GeV. Note that the channels corresponding to gisubscript𝑔𝑖gi (i=2, 3, 4𝑖234i=2,,3,,4) are not experimentally reachable by kinematics. The value of g1subscript𝑔1g1 is the predicted one, since all the parameters are fixed by reproducing the mass of the Ds​0∗​superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠02317Ds0* meson. Using the predicted value of g1subscript𝑔1g1, we determine the Ds​0∗→Ds+​π0→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠0superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜋0Ds0*to Ds+pi0 decay width: ΓDs​0∗→Ds+​π0=g12mDs​0∗​ρDs+​π0​(mDs​0∗2)=g12mDs​0∗2​pcm4​π=13.86​keV,subscriptΓ→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠0superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜋0superscriptsubscript𝑔12subscript𝑚superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠0subscript𝜌superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜋0superscriptsubscript𝑚superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠02superscriptsubscript𝑔12superscriptsubscript𝑚superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠02subscript𝑝cm4𝜋13.86keVdisplaystyleGammaDs0*to Ds+pi0=fracg12mDs0*rhoDs+pi0(mDs0*2)=fracg12mDs0*2fracpcm4pi=13.86,keV, where ρR​(s)subscript𝜌𝑅𝑠rhoR(s) stands for the two-body phase space factor and pcmsubscript𝑝cmpcm denotes the center of momentum for the final states. The predicted value ΓDs​0∗→Ds+​π0=13.86​keVsubscriptΓ→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠0superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜋013.86keVGammaDs0*to Ds+pi0=13.86,keV lies within the experimental upper limit of 3.83.83.8 MeV. The coupling strengths convey information on which channel is the strongest one. We conclude from the results in Eq. that the D​K𝐷𝐾DK channel is the most dominant one, and the Ds+​ηsuperscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠𝜂Ds+eta is the second most significant one. In Table, we compare the current results for the channel couplings with those from other works. Interestingly, The values of the D​K𝐷𝐾DK and Ds+​ηsuperscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠𝜂Ds+eta coupling strengths from the present work are much larger than those from other works. Within the same theoretical framework, we extend our analysis to the scalar bottom-strange meson. Specifically, we apply the coupled-channel formalism to investigate the B¯​ℳ¯𝐵ℳbarBM interaction with positive strangeness +11+1. It is of great importance to note that there is a distinction compared to the charm sector, as the u𝑢u-channel process in the B−​K+superscript𝐵superscript𝐾B-K+ channel is not allowed due to the absence of the charged Bs∗superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠Bs* meson. The cutoff parameters, ΛB∗=630subscriptΛsuperscript𝐵630LambdaB*=630 MeV and ΛBs∗=390subscriptΛsuperscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠390LambdaBs*=390 MeV, are used for the u𝑢u-channel diagrams. The pole for the Bs​0∗superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠0Bs0* emerges again in the second Riemann sheet, located at sR=(5756.32−i​0.0228)subscript𝑠𝑅5756.32𝑖0.0228sqrtsR=(5756.32-i0.0228) MeV, as depicted in Fig. . Notably, the total width for the Bs​0∗superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠0Bs0* resonance, ΓB¯s​0∗subscriptΓsuperscriptsubscript¯𝐵𝑠0GammabarBs0*, exhibits a comparable value to that of the Ds​0∗superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠0Ds0* resonance. This observation is understandable when considering that the two-body phase space factor, ρB¯s0​π0​(mB¯s​0∗2)subscript𝜌superscriptsubscript¯𝐵𝑠0superscript𝜋0superscriptsubscript𝑚superscriptsubscript¯𝐵𝑠02rhobarBs0pi0(mbarBs0*2) for the B¯s​0∗superscriptsubscript¯𝐵𝑠0barBs0* resonance is approximately two times smaller than that of the Ds​0∗superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠0Ds0*. We exmaine the channel couplings for the bottom-strange meson as follows: gB¯s0​π0=0.104−i​0.139​GeV,gB¯s0​η=−302.0−i​1.491​GeV,formulae-sequencesubscript𝑔superscriptsubscript¯𝐵𝑠0superscript𝜋00.104𝑖0.139GeVsubscript𝑔superscriptsubscript¯𝐵𝑠0𝜂302.0𝑖1.491GeVdisplaystyle gbarBs0pi0=0.104-i0.139,GeV,;;;gbarBs0eta=-302.0-i1.491,GeV, gB−​K+=−396.8+i​0.318​GeV,gB¯0​K0=−134.07+i​5.340​GeV. formulae-sequencesubscript𝑔superscript𝐵superscript𝐾396.8𝑖0.318GeVsubscript𝑔superscript¯𝐵0superscript𝐾0134.07𝑖5.340GeVdisplaystyle gB-K+=-396.8+i0.318,GeV,;;;gbarB0K034.07+i5.340,GeV. The corresponding coupling strengths are given by g1=|gB¯s0​π0|=0.174​GeV,subscript𝑔1subscript𝑔superscriptsubscript¯𝐵𝑠0superscript𝜋00.174GeVdisplaystyle g1=|gbarBs0pi0|=0.174,GeV, g2=|gB−​K+|=302.03​GeV,subscript𝑔2subscript𝑔superscript𝐵superscript𝐾302.03GeVdisplaystyle g2=|gB-K+|=302.03,GeV, g3=|gB¯0​K0|=396.75​GeV,subscript𝑔3subscript𝑔superscript¯𝐵0superscript𝐾0396.75GeVdisplaystyle g3=|gbarB0K0|=396.75,GeV, g4=|gB¯s0​η|=134.18​GeV. subscript𝑔4subscript𝑔superscriptsubscript¯𝐵𝑠0𝜂134.18GeVdisplaystyle g4=|gbarBs0eta|=134.18,GeV. In the bottom sector, the B​K𝐵𝐾BK channel is the most dominant one.
content_sections.IV Summary and conclusions: In the current work, we aimed to investigate the Ds​0∗​superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠02317Ds0*, studying Ds+​π0superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜋0Ds+pi0 scattering within the off-shell coupled channel formalism. We introduced sixteen different coupled channels in the charge basis: Ds+​π0superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜋0Ds+pi0, D0​K+superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾D0K+, D+​K0superscript𝐷superscript𝐾0D+K0 and Ds+​ηsuperscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠𝜂Ds+eta with positive strangeness +11+1. We first constructed the invariant kernel amplitudes based on the effective Lagrangians derived from heavy-quark effective theory and hidden local symmetry. Having inserted the kernel amplitudes into the Blankenbecler-Sugar-type coupled integral equations, we evaluated the transition amplitudes. Since the Ds​0∗​superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠02317Ds0* is the scalar meson, we carried out the partial-wave expansion to pick out the S-wave transition amplitude. We then scrutinized the D​K𝐷𝐾DK channels how the pole appears dynamically. When we only considered the D​K𝐷𝐾DK channels, two poles emerged. Adding different channels one by one, the width of the first pole became broadened. Once we included all the coupled channels, the first pole disappeared, and at the same time the position of the second pole started to move to lower energies. Having fitted the cutoff masses, the pole is positioned at the physical mass of the Ds​0∗​superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠02317Ds0*. The Ds​0∗→Ds+​π0→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠0superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜋0Ds0*to Ds+pi0 decay width was predicted to be 13.8613.8613.86 keV, which lies within the experimental upper limit. The D​K𝐷𝐾DK channels were found to be the most dominant one. In addition, we predicted the mass and width of the Bs​0∗superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠0Bs0* meson. Both the Ds​0∗superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠0Ds0* and Bs​0∗superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠0Bs0* are positioned below the D​K𝐷𝐾DK and B¯​K¯𝐵𝐾barBK thresholds, respectively. It indicates that the Ds​0∗superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠0Ds0* and Bs​0∗superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠0Bs0* mesons can be interpreted as the D​K𝐷𝐾DK and B¯​K¯𝐵𝐾barBK molecular states. Acknowledgements. We are very grateful to Samson Clymton for useful comments and discussion. The present work was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea funded by the Korean government (Ministry of Education, Science and Technology, MEST), Grant-No. 2021R1A2C2093368 and 2018R1A5A1025563.

sorted_papers.title:
Estimates of the isospin-violating $\Lambda_b\rightarrow \Sigma^0 \phi,
  \Sigma^0 J/\psi$ decays and the $\Sigma-\Lambda$ mixing
sorted_papers.abstract:
We analyse the two purely isospin-violating decays $\Lambda_b\rightarrow
\Sigma^0 \phi$ and $\Lambda_b\rightarrow \Sigma^0 J/\psi$, proceed merely via
the exchange topologies, in the framework of perturbative QCD approach.
Assuming $\Sigma^0$ baryon belongs to the idealized isospin triplet with quark
components of $usd$, the branching ratios of the two decay modes are predicted
to be tiny, of the order $10^{-8}-10^{-9}$, leading to a difficulty in
observing them. We then extend our study to include the $\Sigma-\Lambda$
mixing.It is found that the mixing has significant effect on the
$\Lambda_b\rightarrow \Sigma$ decays, especially it can greatly increase the
rate of the $J/\psi$ process, by as much as two orders of magnitude, yield
$10^{-7}$, which should be searchable in the future. We also estimate a set of
asymmetry observables with and without the mixing effect, which will be tested
in coming experiments.

sections.3: Fleischer, Search for the angle gamma in the electroweak penguin dominated decay Bs→π0​Φ→subscript𝐵𝑠superscript𝜋0ΦB_{s}\to\pi^{0}\Phi, Phys

qualitative: Isospin symmetry is an approximate symmetry in the Standard Model, used to relate amplitudes of decays involving up and down quarks; it is violated by the mass difference between down and up quarks and electromagnetic corrections.

The Λb→Σ⁰ϕ and Λb→Σ⁰J/ψ decays are purely isospin-violating because the Λb is an isospin singlet while the final states have total isospin I=1, leading to no contribution from isospin-conserving QCD penguin operators.

These decays proceed exclusively via tree-level and electroweak penguin operators, which are isospin-violating, making them sensitive probes of electroweak penguin contributions and potential new physics.
The Σ⁰ baryon is assumed to belong to an idealized isospin triplet with quark content usd, but in reality, it mixes with the Λ⁰ due to strong isospin symmetry breaking, known as Σ−Λ mixing.

The Σ−Λ mixing significantly enhances the decay rate of Λb→Σ⁰J/ψ by up to two orders of magnitude, increasing the branching ratio to around 10⁻⁷, which is potentially observable in future experiments.

The perturbative QCD (PQCD) approach is suitable for analyzing these decays because it can consistently include non-factorizable exchange topologies, unlike factorization approaches or diquark approximations which fail due to orthogonality of Λb and Σ⁰ spin wave functions.
The PQCD framework uses light-cone distribution amplitudes (LCDAs) and Sudakov suppression to handle large logarithms and provide convergent, predictive results for exclusive baryonic decays.
Asymmetry observables are estimated both with and without Σ−Λ mixing, providing testable predictions for future experimental measurements at LHCb and future B factories.
quantitative: Branching ratios for Λb→Σ⁰ϕ and Λb→Σ⁰J/ψ are predicted to be of order 10⁻⁸–10⁻⁹ under the idealized isospin triplet assumption (no Σ−Λ mixing).
Inclusion of Σ−Λ mixing increases the branching ratio of Λb→Σ⁰J/ψ by up to two orders of magnitude, reaching approximately 10⁻⁷.

The LHCb observation of Bs⁰→ϕρ⁰ has a branching fraction of (2.7±0.8)×10⁻⁷, which is consistent with SM expectations and leaves little room for new physics, motivating the study of other isospin-violating decays.
The Σ−Λ mixing effect is significant in the Λb→Σ⁰J/ψ decay, but less so in the Λb→Σ⁰ϕ decay, indicating a stronger sensitivity of the J/ψ channel to mixing.
The PQCD approach allows for a self-consistent evaluation of all Feynman diagrams, including exchange topologies, without relying on factorization assumptions.

authors: Zhou Rui, Jia-Ming Li, Chao-Qi Zhang

content_sections.I Introduction: Isospin symmetry is an approximate symmetry in the Standard model (SM) which has been widely used in the phenomenological analysis of heavy quark decays. For example, the amplitudes for the family of B→K​π→𝐵𝐾𝜋Brightarrow Kpi decays are expected to obey a quadrilateral relation imposed by isospin symmetry citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4, which allow us to constrain the SM parameters, and to look for signs of new physics (NP). Based on isospin arguments, the difference between the direct C​P𝐶𝑃CP asymmetries for the modes B+→K+​π0→superscript𝐵superscript𝐾superscript𝜋0B+rightarrow K+pi0 and B0→K+​π−→superscript𝐵0superscript𝐾superscript𝜋B0rightarrow K+pi- are expected to be zero at the leading order in the SMcitebib.bib5, as the two decays differ only by the spectator quark. Nevertheless, a nonzero C​P𝐶𝑃CP asymmetry difference with a significance of more than six standard deviations (σ𝜎sigma) was observed in the latest measurements of LHCbcitebib.bib6, suggests a violation of the strong isospin symmetry may beyond the SM expectation, which was referred to as the longstanding “K​π𝐾𝜋Kpi puzzle”citebib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11. To resolve above puzzle, a sizeable electroweak (EW) penguin contribution, offering an attractive avenue for new particles to enter, will be requestedcitebib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16. In the beauty-hadron decays aspect, the EW penguin contributions are usually overshadowed by the larger QCD penguins. For this reason, it is of particular interest to consider those purely isospin-violating decays, in which the isospin-conserving QCD penguin amplitudes do not contribute at all. The representative examples in hadronic B𝐵B meson decays are the B¯s→(η,η′,ϕ)​(π,ρ)→subscript¯𝐵𝑠𝜂superscript𝜂′italic-ϕ𝜋𝜌barBsrightarrow(eta,etaprime,phi)(pi,rho) modescitebib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, whose branching ratios were predicted to be relatively small in the SM, of order 10−8−10−7superscript108superscript10710-8-10-7 citebib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib26. The inclusion of NP effectscitebib.bib32, bib.bib31, bib.bib33, bib.bib34 in EW penguin greatly increases their rates by an order of magnitude with respect to the SM predictions, making these decays are interesting for LHCb and future B𝐵B factories searches. Up to now, only the 4​σ4𝜎4sigma evidence for Bs0→ϕ​ρ0→subscriptsuperscript𝐵0𝑠italic-ϕsuperscript𝜌0B0srightarrowphirho0 was seen by LHCb with a branching fraction of (2.7±0.8)×10−7plus-or-minus2.70.8superscript107(2.7pm 0.8)times 10-7citebib.bib35, which is compatible with its SM expectation albeit still with a significant uncertainty. This consistency leaves little room for NP contributions and motivates us to explore further information from other hadronic decays which are also sensitive to EW penguin contributions. In the b𝑏b-baryon sector, the similar promising processes are the Λb→Σ0​ϕ,Σ0​J/ψ→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΣ0italic-ϕsuperscriptΣ0𝐽𝜓LambdabrightarrowSigma0phi,Sigma0J/psi decays, which received less attention in the literature. Since ΛbsubscriptΛ𝑏Lambdab is an isospin singlet while the final state have total isospin I=1𝐼1I=1, these processes thus also fully break isospin symmetry. Their amplitudes receive contributions from the tree and EW penguin operators, which are isospin-violating. By contrast for the QCD penguin operators, which preserve isospin symmetry, do not affect these processes. In the SM, isospin violation arises from the quark mass difference md−musubscript𝑚𝑑subscript𝑚𝑢md-mu and from electromagnetic correctionscitebib.bib36. In this case, the QCD-penguin operators can contribute through the isospin-conserving amplitudes if the Σ−ΛΣΛSigma-Lambda mixing under strong isospin-symmetry breaking is permittedcitebib.bib37. The factorization approach (FA) and the diquark approximation failed in describing such purely isospin-violating decays because the Λb→Σ0→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΣ0LambdabrightarrowSigma0 transition is forbidden due to the orthogonality of the ΛbsubscriptΛ𝑏Lambdab and Σ0superscriptΣ0Sigma0 spin functions. They can only proceed via the exchange diagrams, thus the observations of them would provide valuable information on the role of exchange topologies in b𝑏b-baryon weak decays. This poses serious challenges to precise theoretical calculations based on factorization. However, FA is not necessary in the perturbative QCD (PQCD) approachcitebib.bib38. Except for the factorizable diagrams, one can valuate all relevant Feynman diagrams including the exchange topologies in a self-consistent manner. In PQCD factorizationcitebib.bib39, the decay amplitude for a exclusive process is obtained as a convolution of a perturbative kernel and the nonperturbative hadronic light-cone distribution amplitudes (LCDAs). After summing the large logarithms associated with parton transverse momenta, the PQCD formalism with Sudakov suppression can give converging results and have predictive power. Therefore, it can serve as a useful tool for investigating the heavy bottom decayscitebib.bib40, bib.bib41, bib.bib22, bib.bib42, bib.bib43, bib.bib44. Inspired by the success of the PQCD approach in various weak decays of ΛbsubscriptΛ𝑏Lambdab baryon citebib.bib45, bib.bib46, bib.bib47, bib.bib48, bib.bib49, bib.bib50, bib.bib51, bib.bib52, bib.bib53, bib.bib54, bib.bib55, in this work we shall perform a phenomenological analysis of the above two purely isospin-violating decays and explore the exchange topological contribution inside. The Σ−ΛΣΛSigma-Lambda mixing effects in the two reactions are also investigated. The rest of the paper is organized as follows. We briefly review the LCDAs, kinematics, and the Σ−ΛΣΛSigma-Lambda mixing phenomenon in Sec. . The numerical analysis including the results for the decay amplitudes, branching ratios and various asymmetry parameters are presented in Sec. . Section is reserved for a summary and conclusions. Finally, we give the explicit expressions of the factorization formulas in the Appendix.
content_sections.II Theoretical framework II.1 Light-cone: The LCDAs are primary nonperturbative quantities for calculating the heavy baryon decays based on the PQCD approach, which can be constructed via the nonlocal matrix elements. We now summarize the definitions of the LCDAs for the initial and final states. Heavy baryons containing a b𝑏b-quark can be regarded as a system of an effective heavy quark and a pair of light quarks with aligned helicities, called diquark in the heavy quark limit. The heavy quarks are nonrelativistic particles which decouple from the diquark in the leading order of the heavy quark mass expansion. The investigation of heavy-baryon distribution amplitudes has made brilliant progresscitebib.bib57, bib.bib58, bib.bib60, bib.bib61, bib.bib59, bib.bib56 since the complete classification and renormalization group equations (RGE) that govern the scale-dependence are presentedcitebib.bib57. The above definition indicates the daughter baryon momentum is along the n𝑛n direction in the massless limit. Several asymptotic models for the various twist LCDAs, in which the twist is indicated by the subscript numbers, have been proposed in Refs. citebib.bib57, bib.bib58, bib.bib56 and summarized in Ref. citebib.bib51. We adopt the exponential model, whose explicit expressions can be found in the previous workcitebib.bib51, bib.bib53, bib.bib54 and shall not be repeated here. The twist 2 and twist 4 LCDAs are symmetric under exchange of x2subscript𝑥2x2 and x3subscript𝑥3x3, while the two twist 3 ones do not have any definite symmetry but satisfy Ψ3+−​(x2,x3)=Ψ3−+​(x3,x2)superscriptsubscriptΨ3absentsubscript𝑥2subscript𝑥3superscriptsubscriptΨ3absentsubscript𝑥3subscript𝑥2Psi3+-(x2,x3)=Psi3-+(x3,x2) because of the isospin zero condition for the diquark. ΦVsuperscriptΦ𝑉PhiV, ΦAsuperscriptΦ𝐴PhiA and ΦTsuperscriptΦ𝑇PhiT are the vector, axial-vector and tensor structure LCDAs, respectively. Their explicit expressions have been studied using QCD sum rulescitebib.bib62, bib.bib63, bib.bib64, bib.bib65 and lattice QCDcitebib.bib66, bib.bib67, bib.bib68. Quite recently, the one-loop perturbative contributions to LCDA of a light baryon has been derived in large-momentum effective theorycitebib.bib69, which provides a first step to obtaining the LCDA from first principle lattice QCD calculations in the future. It can be seen that the LCDAs at the scale μ=1𝜇1mu=1 GeV differ greatly from their asymptotic forms. It is easy to observe that ΦVsuperscriptΦ𝑉PhiV and ΦTsuperscriptΦ𝑇PhiT are symmetric under permutation of u𝑢u and d𝑑d quarks, but ΦAsuperscriptΦ𝐴PhiA is antisymmetric under the same operation. These symmetry properties are completely opposite to those of the Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0 baryon. content_sections.II Theoretical framework II.3 Σ−ΛΣΛ\\Si: Isospin symmetry can be broken either by electroweak effects or by the strong interaction through the up and down quark mass difference, which might lead to the quark flavor mixing. In other words, the observed physical states with definite mass are actually mixtures of the idealized isospin states. In the SM, Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0 and Σ0superscriptΣ0Sigma0 baryons are part of the baryon octet and share the same valence quarks (u​d​s)𝑢𝑑𝑠(uds). Their different isospin separates the former as a singlet and the latter as part of the triplet with two partners Σ±superscriptΣplus-or-minusSigmapm. The physical baryons are made of the mixing of isospin triplet and singlet states 1We use Σ0,Λ0superscriptΣ0superscriptΛ0Sigma0,Lambda0 to stand for the unmixed isospin states and Σ,ΛΣΛSigma,Lambda to denote the physical particle states. (ΛΣ)=(cos⁡θ−sin⁡θsin⁡θcos⁡θ)​(Λ0Σ0),ΛΣ𝜃𝜃𝜃𝜃superscriptΛ0superscriptΣ0displaystyleleft(beginarraycLambda Sigmaendarrayright)=left(beginarraycccostheta-sintheta sinthetacosthetaendarrayright)left(beginarraycLambda0 Sigma0endarrayright), with θ𝜃theta being the mixing angle. Since the mixing phenomenon in the Λ−ΣΛΣLambda-Sigma system was first proposed in the late 1970s, many fruitful theoretical works on the mixing angle have been made so far citebib.bib83, bib.bib84, bib.bib85, bib.bib86, bib.bib87, bib.bib88, bib.bib89, bib.bib91, bib.bib90, bib.bib92. Although the mixing angle has not yet been given experimentally, its value has been estimated to be in the range (0.55−1.2)∘superscript0.551.2(0.55-1.2)circcitebib.bib90, bib.bib91, bib.bib92, bib.bib93, bib.bib94. The smallness of the mixing angle indicates the degree of isospin-symmetry breaking is weak. In general, the initial states of ΛbsubscriptΛ𝑏Lambdab and ΣbsubscriptΣ𝑏Sigmab, corresponding to I=0𝐼0I=0 and I=1𝐼1I=1, are also mixed. However, the mixing receive an additional suppression from the bottom quark masscitebib.bib95. As a consequence, it is ignored in following analysis. Considering above mixing effect, the physical decay amplitude for the concerned processes can be written ascitebib.bib37 ⟨Σ​V|ℋe​f​f|Λb⟩≈θ​⟨Λ0​V|ℋ0|Λb⟩+⟨Σ0​V|ℋ1|Λb⟩,quantum-operator-productΣ𝑉subscriptℋ𝑒𝑓𝑓subscriptΛ𝑏𝜃quantum-operator-productsuperscriptΛ0𝑉subscriptℋ0subscriptΛ𝑏quantum-operator-productsuperscriptΣ0𝑉subscriptℋ1subscriptΛ𝑏displaystyleSigma V|Heff|LambdabapproxthetaLambda0V|H0|Lambdab+Sigma0V|H1|Lambdab, where ℋ0subscriptℋ0H0 and ℋ1subscriptℋ1H1 refer to isospin conserving and breaking Hamiltonian, respectively. It can be seen the isospin breaking effect affect not only the mixing of the states but also the amplitude. The first term on the right-hand side (rhs) of Eq. conserves isospin and hence receives contributions from both electroweak and QCD-penguin, but the second term belongs to an isospin-violating transition to which the QCD-penguin component does not participate. The tree operators, mediated by the b→u→𝑏𝑢brightarrow u transition, contribute in both terms but suffer from CKM suppression. Though the mixing is small, there is lack of quantitative estimate of its effect. It is then worthwhile to examine whether the large isospin conserving amplitudes are able to compensate for the tiny mixing, and to give a sizable impact on the Λb→Σ→subscriptΛ𝑏ΣLambdabrightarrowSigma decays.
content_sections.III Numerical results: In the numerical calculations, we use the masses (GeV) as followscitebib.bib96: M𝑀displaystyle M =displaystyle= 5.6196,mΣ=1.193,mb=4.8,mϕ=1.019,mJ/ψ=3.097. formulae-sequence5.6196subscript𝑚Σ1.193formulae-sequencesubscript𝑚𝑏4.8formulae-sequencesubscript𝑚italic-ϕ1.019subscript𝑚𝐽𝜓3.097displaystyle 5.6196,quad mSigma=1.193,quad mb=4.8,quad mphi=1.019,quad mJ/psi=3.097. The CKM matrix elements in the Wolfenstein parametrization read (Vu​b,Vu​s,Vt​b,Vt​s)=(A​λ3​(ρ−i​η),λ,1,−A​λ2),subscript𝑉𝑢𝑏subscript𝑉𝑢𝑠subscript𝑉𝑡𝑏subscript𝑉𝑡𝑠𝐴superscript𝜆3𝜌𝑖𝜂𝜆1𝐴superscript𝜆2displaystyle(Vub,Vus,Vtb,Vts)=(Alambda3(rho-ieta),lambda,1,-Alambda2), withcitebib.bib96 λ=0.22650,A=0.790,ρ¯=0.141,η¯=0.357. formulae-sequence𝜆0.22650formulae-sequence𝐴0.790formulae-sequence¯𝜌0.141¯𝜂0.357displaystylelambda=0.22650,quad A=0.790,quadbarrho=0.141,quadbareta=0.357. The lifetime of the ΛbsubscriptΛ𝑏Lambdab is taken to be 1.464 ps from the particle data group (PDG)citebib.bib96. Other nonperturbative parameters appearing in the hadron LCDAs have been specified in the preceding section. There are three crucial differences between Λb→Σ0→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΣ0LambdabrightarrowSigma0 and Λb→Λ0→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΛ0LambdabrightarrowLambda0 decays: (i) the former can occur only via the exchange topologies in the SM, which are power suppressed with respect to the emission ones. (ii) As the QCD penguin operators do not give contributions to the pure Δ​I=1Δ𝐼1Delta I=1 decays, the (S−P)​(S+P)𝑆𝑃𝑆𝑃(S-P)(S+P) amplitudes, stem from the Fierz transformation of the O7,8subscript𝑂78O7,8 operators, are strongly suppressed by the corresponding Wilson coefficients. Our numerical results manifest that their contributions are vanishingly small compared to the (V−A)​(V−A)𝑉𝐴𝑉𝐴(V-A)(V-A) ones. After taken account of the above two aspects, it is then expected that the Σ0superscriptΣ0Sigma0 modes have much small decay amplitudes as shown in Table. (iii) The symmetry properties of the LCDAs of Σ0superscriptΣ0Sigma0 under the exchange of the up and down quarks are always opposite to the corresponding terms for Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0, resulting in different relative contributions from ΦVsuperscriptΦ𝑉PhiV, ΦAsuperscriptΦ𝐴PhiA and ΦTsuperscriptΦ𝑇PhiT. This distinction will cause some observed quantities to behave differently, as detailed more below. In Table, we compare contributions from three components of LCDAs of Σ0superscriptΣ0Sigma0 baryon to the invariant amplitudes: ΦVsuperscriptΦ𝑉PhiV,ΦAsuperscriptΦ𝐴PhiA,and ΦTsuperscriptΦ𝑇PhiT. It is apparent that the ΦAsuperscriptΦ𝐴PhiA component for the concerned decays have the smallest contributions. This observation differs from that in Λb→Λ​ϕ→subscriptΛ𝑏Λitalic-ϕLambdabrightarrowLambdaphi decay, where a dominant contribution from the ΦAsuperscriptΦ𝐴PhiA component was claimedcitebib.bib54. The reason is that the ΦAsuperscriptΦ𝐴PhiA is antisymmetric under the interchange of the u𝑢u and d𝑑d quarks for Σ0superscriptΣ0Sigma0 but symmetric for Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0. It is also observed that the relative contributions from ΦVsuperscriptΦ𝑉PhiV and ΦTsuperscriptΦ𝑇PhiT components are different between Λb→Σ0​ϕ→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΣ0italic-ϕLambdabrightarrowSigma0phi and Λb→Σ0​J/ψ→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΣ0𝐽𝜓LambdabrightarrowSigma0J/psi modes, which can be understood easily as follows. The Λb→Σ0​ϕ→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΣ0italic-ϕLambdabrightarrowSigma0phi decay receives both the E𝐸E and B𝐵B-type topological contributions. The numerical analysis shows that the four E𝐸E-type diagrams Ea​6subscript𝐸𝑎6Ea6, Ec​1subscript𝐸𝑐1Ec1, Ed​2subscript𝐸𝑑2Ed2, and Ed​4subscript𝐸𝑑4Ed4 play the most significant role in the decay amplitude for the Λb→Σ0​ϕ→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΣ0italic-ϕLambdabrightarrowSigma0phi transition, while the contributions from B𝐵B-type exchange diagrams are predicted to be vanishingly small. As shown in Table, for the E𝐸E-type amplitudes, the ΦVsuperscriptΦ𝑉PhiV and ΦAsuperscriptΦ𝐴PhiA components are down by the power of rΣsubscript𝑟ΣrSigma or rVsubscript𝑟𝑉rV, then the ΦTsuperscriptΦ𝑇PhiT one dominates this process. Things are different for the Λb→Σ0​J/ψ→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΣ0𝐽𝜓LambdabrightarrowSigma0J/psi decay, which involves only the B𝐵B-type amplitudes dominated by Ba​3subscript𝐵𝑎3Ba3 diagram. From Table, we note that the leading twist term of HBa​3L​Lsuperscriptsubscript𝐻subscript𝐵𝑎3𝐿𝐿HBa3LL is proportional to the factor rΣ​rV​(1−x3−y)​x1​(ΦV−ΦA)​Ψ2subscript𝑟Σsubscript𝑟𝑉1subscript𝑥3𝑦subscript𝑥1superscriptΦ𝑉superscriptΦ𝐴subscriptΨ2rSigmarV(1-x3-y)x1(PhiV-PhiA)Psi2. Furthermore, the combination of the two virtual quark propagators in Ba​3subscript𝐵𝑎3Ba3 diagram is proportional to 1(1−x3−y)​x3′​1x1​x3′11subscript𝑥3𝑦subscriptsuperscript𝑥′31subscript𝑥1subscriptsuperscript𝑥′3frac1(1-x3-y)xprime3frac1x1xprime3 if removing the parton transverse momenta (see Table). Therefore the amplitude of Ba​3subscript𝐵𝑎3Ba3 diagram behaves like 1x3′1superscriptsubscript𝑥3′frac1x3prime2Note that the LCDAs of Σ0superscriptΣ0Sigma0 baryon also contain one x3′superscriptsubscript𝑥3′x3prime. and induce enhancement in the endpoint region x3′→0→superscriptsubscript𝑥3′0x3primerightarrow 0. Likewise, the Bb​3subscript𝐵𝑏3Bb3 diagram also exhibits the similar endpoint behavior. Our results indicate its contribution is smaller than the Ba​3subscript𝐵𝑎3Ba3 one because an additional subleading term associated with rcsubscript𝑟𝑐rc yields a significant destructive correction. On the contrary, this correction becomes constructive in HBa​3L​Lsuperscriptsubscript𝐻subscript𝐵𝑎3𝐿𝐿HBa3LL due to the rcsubscript𝑟𝑐rc term flipping sign [see the argument below Eq. ]. The substantial endpoint enhancement was also observed in the previous PQCD calculations for the Λb→p→subscriptΛ𝑏𝑝Lambdabrightarrow p transition form factorcitebib.bib51, which warns us to be much cautious when dropping those power suppression terms especially in the endpoint region. Numerically, such endpoint enhancement can overcomes the power suppression from rΣ​rVsubscript𝑟Σsubscript𝑟𝑉rSigmarV, so that the Ba​3subscript𝐵𝑎3Ba3 amplitude dominates the decay. Viewing Table, it is obvious that the ΦTsuperscriptΦ𝑇PhiT component is always accompanied by the subleading-twist LCDAs of ΛbsubscriptΛ𝑏Lambdab baryon, while the ΦVsuperscriptΦ𝑉PhiV one is associated with the leading-twist one. The above combined effect can explain why this decay is governed by the ΦVsuperscriptΦ𝑉PhiV component as indicated in Table. Although the B𝐵B-type amplitudes are of 𝒪​(rΣ​rV)𝒪subscript𝑟Σsubscript𝑟𝑉O(rSigmarV), we should note that, for the case of Λb→Σ0​J/ψ→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΣ0𝐽𝜓LambdabrightarrowSigma0J/psi decay, rV∼0.5similar-tosubscript𝑟𝑉0.5rVsim 0.5 is not a serious suppression factor. Moreover, the additional terms connected with the charm quark mass are numerically significant in the Λb→Σ0​J/ψ→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΣ0𝐽𝜓LambdabrightarrowSigma0J/psi amplitude. It is therefore not surprising that |𝒜​(Λb→Σ0​J/ψ)|𝒜→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΣ0𝐽𝜓|A(LambdabrightarrowSigma0J/psi)| only fall short by a factor of 4 compared to |𝒜​(Λb→Σ0​ϕ)|𝒜→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΣ0italic-ϕ|A(LambdabrightarrowSigma0phi)| as shown in the Table. The corresponding branching ratios of the two decay modes are estimated to be small, of the order 10−8−10−9superscript108superscript10910-8-10-9, which are difficult to measure experimentally at present. The numerical results of the helicity amplitudes are displayed in the Table. As mentioned above the ΦTsuperscriptΦ𝑇PhiT contribution dominates over other two components in the ϕitalic-ϕphi mode. From Eq. , one can see that the ΦTsuperscriptΦ𝑇PhiT term in A1L,Tsuperscriptsubscript𝐴1𝐿𝑇A1L,T and B1L,Tsuperscriptsubscript𝐵1𝐿𝑇B1L,T has opposite signs but is the same in A2Lsuperscriptsubscript𝐴2𝐿A2L and B2Lsuperscriptsubscript𝐵2𝐿B2L. This pattern will result in the constructive (destructive) combination of the four terms in H−12​0subscript𝐻120H-frac120 (H12​0subscript𝐻120Hfrac120) [see Eq. ]. Similarly, we have H−12−1≫H12​1much-greater-thansubscript𝐻121subscript𝐻121H-frac12-1gg Hfrac121 since the interference between A1Tsuperscriptsubscript𝐴1𝑇A1T and B1Tsuperscriptsubscript𝐵1𝑇B1T is constructive in H−12−1subscript𝐻121H-frac12-1 but destructive in H12​1subscript𝐻121Hfrac121. Note that the values of A​(B)2L𝐴superscriptsubscript𝐵2𝐿A(B)2L are typically large, so that the longitudinal polarization contribution exceeds the transverse one in Λb→Σ0​ϕ→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΣ0italic-ϕLambdabrightarrowSigma0phi decay. One can observe from Table that the helicity amplitudes of the ϕitalic-ϕphi mode are dominated by H−12​0subscript𝐻120H-frac120 which occupies about 73.6%percent73.673.6% of the full contribution. The observation is different for the Λb→Σ0​J/ψ→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΣ0𝐽𝜓LambdabrightarrowSigma0J/psi decay, in which the ΦVsuperscriptΦ𝑉PhiV component contributes largest amounts to the invariant amplitudes. In this case, A1L,Tsuperscriptsubscript𝐴1𝐿𝑇A1L,T and B1L,Tsuperscriptsubscript𝐵1𝐿𝑇B1L,T have the same signs whereas A2Lsuperscriptsubscript𝐴2𝐿A2L and B2Lsuperscriptsubscript𝐵2𝐿B2L have opposite signs (see also Eq. ). Thus we have H12​1≫H−12−1much-greater-thansubscript𝐻121subscript𝐻121Hfrac121gg H-frac12-1 and H12​0≫H−12​0much-greater-thansubscript𝐻120subscript𝐻120Hfrac120gg H-frac120 as shown in Table. This pattern indicates transversely polarization contributions will dominate over the longitudinal one in this channel. As mentioned earlier, the decay amplitudes at hand receive contributions from tree and EW penguin operators. For the b→s→𝑏𝑠brightarrow s transition, the product of CKM matrix elements in the tree contributions is suppressed compared to that in the EW penguin ones, and the suppression factor is |Vu​s​Vu​b|/|Vt​s​Vt​b|∼0.02similar-tosubscript𝑉𝑢𝑠subscript𝑉𝑢𝑏subscript𝑉𝑡𝑠subscript𝑉𝑡𝑏0.02|VusVub|/|VtsVtb|sim 0.02. On the other hand, the Wilson coefficient of the dominant EW penguin operator O9subscript𝑂9O9 around the mbsubscript𝑚𝑏mb scale is also of the order 10−2superscript10210-2citebib.bib97. Therefore, the tree and EW penguin operator contributions are in fact similar in magnitude as presented in Table. Thus one expects that the comparable tree and EW contributions can enhance the direct C​P𝐶𝑃CP asymmetry. From Table, one see the AC​P​(Λb→Σ0​ϕ)=5.0%subscript𝐴𝐶𝑃→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΣ0italic-ϕpercent5.0ACP(LambdabrightarrowSigma0phi)=5.0% is larger than AC​P​(Λb→Λ0​ϕ)=−1.0%subscript𝐴𝐶𝑃→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΛ0italic-ϕpercent1.0ACP(LambdabrightarrowLambda0phi).0%citebib.bib54. For the Λb→Σ0​J/ψ→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΣ0𝐽𝜓LambdabrightarrowSigma0J/psi mode, as indicated in Table of Appendix, all contributing Feynman diagrams have the same factor aσsuperscript𝑎𝜎asigma containing weak phase information, implies that the sum of the remnant hard kernels yields an overall strong phase. In this case the strong phase difference only come from the interference between (V−A)​(V−A)𝑉𝐴𝑉𝐴(V-A)(V-A) and (S−P)​(S+P)𝑆𝑃𝑆𝑃(S-P)(S+P) amplitudes. As noted previously, the latter is highly suppressed relative to the former and the resultant strong phase difference is rather tiny, such that the direct C​P𝐶𝑃CP asymmetry AC​P​(Λb→Σ0​J/ψ)subscript𝐴𝐶𝑃→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΣ0𝐽𝜓ACP(LambdabrightarrowSigma0J/psi) is estimated to be less than 𝒪​(1%)𝒪percent1O(1%) in magnitude. We therefore conclude that any measurement of a sizeable direct C​P𝐶𝑃CP asymmetry in this decay is an unequivocal signal of NP. We provide the numerical results for the various asymmetry observables defined by Eq. in Table, where the first and second uncertainties arise from the shape parameter ω0=0.40±0.04subscript𝜔0plus-or-minus0.400.04omega0=0.40pm 0.04 GeV in the ΛbsubscriptΛ𝑏Lambdab baryon LCDAs and hard scale t𝑡t varying from 0.8​t0.8𝑡0.8t to 1.2​t1.2𝑡1.2t, respectively. It is observed that the predicted asymmetries for the two modes seem to be distinctly different. The reason is, again, the helicity amplitude H−12​0subscript𝐻120H-frac120 dominate the ϕitalic-ϕphi mode while the H12​1subscript𝐻121Hfrac121 one is more preferred in the J/ψ𝐽𝜓J/psi channel as explained above. The pattern of the Λb→Σ0​ϕ→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΣ0italic-ϕLambdabrightarrowSigma0phi mode coincides with its Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0 counterpart, so the asymmetries between the two modes are close to each other. However, the PQCD predictions for the asymmetries in Λb→Σ0​J/ψ→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΣ0𝐽𝜓LambdabrightarrowSigma0J/psi are at variance with those in Λb→Λ0​J/ψ→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΛ0𝐽𝜓LambdabrightarrowLambda0J/psicitebib.bib53. Some quantities even possess opposite signs, such as the longitudinal polarization of the daughter baryon. Our findings can be compared in future. We now turn to the results by including the Σ−ΛΣΛSigma-Lambda mixing effect. According to Eq. , the physical (mixed) amplitude are modified into 𝒜​(Λb→Σ​V)=θ​𝒜​(Λb→Λ0​V)+𝒜​(Λb→Σ0​V),𝒜→subscriptΛ𝑏Σ𝑉𝜃𝒜→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΛ0𝑉𝒜→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΣ0𝑉displaystyleA(LambdabrightarrowSigma V)=thetaA(LambdabrightarrowLambda0V)+A(LambdabrightarrowSigma0V), where the first and second terms correspond to the isospin conserving and violating amplitudes, respectively. The determination of the mixing angle has been extensive discussed in the literature, whose value still varies in a finite range. We take the value θ=(2.07±0.03)×10−2𝜃plus-or-minus2.070.03superscript102theta=(2.07pm 0.03)times 10-2 (in radians), which was also considered incitebib.bib93. The central values of the isospin conserving amplitudes of Λb→Λ0​ϕ→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΛ0italic-ϕLambdabrightarrowLambda0phi and Λb→Λ0​J/ψ→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΛ0𝐽𝜓LambdabrightarrowLambda0J/psi are quoted from our previous workcitebib.bib53, bib.bib54, read 3These values are not explicitly given incitebib.bib53, bib.bib54, but one can infer them from the corresponding branching ratios. |𝒜​(Λb→Λ0​ϕ)|𝒜→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΛ0italic-ϕdisplaystyle|A(LambdabrightarrowLambda0phi)| =displaystyle= 3.1×10−8,3.1superscript108displaystyle 3.1times 10-8, |𝒜​(Λb→Λ0​J/ψ)|𝒜→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΛ0𝐽𝜓displaystyle|A(LambdabrightarrowLambda0J/psi)| =displaystyle= 4.0×10−7.4.0superscript107displaystyle 4.0times 10-7. As can be seen, the isospin conserving amplitudes are at least an order of magnitude larger than the isospin violating ones and can more or less compensate for the suppression from the mixing angle, implying that the isospin conserving amplitudes may provide potentially non-negligible contributions to the decays of ΛbsubscriptΛ𝑏Lambdab to the physical final state Σ​VΣ𝑉Sigma V. Comparing Tables with for the ϕitalic-ϕphi mode, it is clearly seen that the mixing causes distinct differences for the physical observables. The destructive combination of the two isospin amplitudes is responsible for the decrease in branching ratio, whereas the interference pattern reverses for the C​P𝐶𝑃CP-conjugated process, so that the direct C​P𝐶𝑃CP asymmetry varies from 5.0%percent5.05.0% without mixing to −19.3%percent19.3-19.3% with mixing. For the J/ψ𝐽𝜓J/psi process, the isospin conserving amplitude is three orders of magnitude higher than the violating one, significantly surpassing the suppression from the mixing angle. As a result the mixing effect has a strong influence on the J/ψ𝐽𝜓J/psi mode. It is apparent that the mixing effect can hugely amplify the rate of Λb→Σ​J/ψ→subscriptΛ𝑏Σ𝐽𝜓LambdabrightarrowSigma J/psi above the estimation without mixing, by as much as two orders of magnitude. The resulting asymmetries have also changed drastically. Experimentally, an upper limit on the isospin amplitude ratio ℛ=|A​(Λb→Σ0​J/ψ)/A​(Λb→Λ​J/ψ)|ℛ𝐴→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΣ0𝐽𝜓𝐴→subscriptΛ𝑏Λ𝐽𝜓R=|A(LambdabrightarrowSigma0J/psi)/A(LambdabrightarrowLambda J/psi)| is measured to be 1/21.8 at 95%percent9595% confidence level by LHCbcitebib.bib98. In the absence of Σ−ΛΣΛSigma-Lambda mixing, we have the isospin amplitude ratio ℛ=0.00185ℛ0.00185R=0.00185, which is far below the experimental upper limit. Taking into account the mixing, this ratio substantially rises to 0.0214, reaching half of the upper bound. This estimate is also in agreement with that incitebib.bib37 and supports the assumption made incitebib.bib37 that the dynamic contribution to ℛℛR is much smaller than the static mixing component within SM. If an enhancement of such dynamic contribution owing to the NP is excluded, the observation of a substantial decay rate of Λb→Σ0​J/ψ→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΣ0𝐽𝜓LambdabrightarrowSigma0J/psi in the future experiments would be strong evidence of the Λ−ΣΛΣLambda-Sigma mixing. It follows that the improved precision measurement of the isospin amplitude ratio is desirable to constrain the mixing angle.
content_sections.IV conclusion: In this work we have studied two purely isospin-violating decays Λb→Σ0​ϕ,Σ0​J/ψ→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΣ0italic-ϕsuperscriptΣ0𝐽𝜓LambdabrightarrowSigma0phi,Sigma0J/psi, which are generated via the exchange topologies induced by the b​u→s​u→𝑏𝑢𝑠𝑢burightarrow su transition. Since ΛbsubscriptΛ𝑏Lambdab and Σ0superscriptΣ0Sigma0 belong to different isospin representations, the Λb→Σ0→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΣ0LambdabrightarrowSigma0 transition is forbidden in the SM. The factorization approach and the diquark picture failed in describing such decays. To estimate the exchange topological contributions, we employ the leading order PQCD formalism, which allows us to evaluate all relevant topological diagrams involving two hard gluon exchanges systematically. We first explore the decays under consideration without the Σ−ΛΣΛSigma-Lambda mixing. At the quark level, the decay amplitude for an isospin-violating transition receives contributions from both the tree and electroweak penguins, whereas the QCD penguin vanishes because it preserves isospin symmetry. The calculated invariant amplitudes and helicity amplitudes are presented in detail in corresponding Tables. We compare at some length the distinctive patterns of the two modes as well as their Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0 counterparts. It has been demonstrated that the helicity amplitudes H−12​0subscript𝐻120H-frac120 and H12​1subscript𝐻121Hfrac121 respectively dominate the ϕitalic-ϕphi and J/ψ𝐽𝜓J/psi modes, due to different relative contributions from the Σ0superscriptΣ0Sigma0 baryon LCDAs. The obtained branching ratios are typically small, at 10−8−10−9superscript108superscript10910-8-10-9 level, and the direct C​P𝐶𝑃CP violations are around several percents. Furthermore, we predict for the first time a set of asymmetry parameters, which will be tested theoretically and experimentally in the future. Taking into account the Σ−ΛΣΛSigma-Lambda mixing effect, the decays of ΛbsubscriptΛ𝑏Lambdab to the physical final states involve both the isospin-conserving and isospin-violating contributions. The PQCD calculations reveal that the former are at least an order of magnitude greater than the latter, which is sufficient to compensate for the suppression caused by the mixing angle, so that the influence of mixing is more pronounced for the Λb→Σ→subscriptΛ𝑏ΣLambdabrightarrowSigma decays than the Λb→Λ→subscriptΛ𝑏ΛLambdabrightarrowLambda ones. The numerical results manifest that the mixing effect modifies significantly both the decay rates and the asymmetry parameters. In particular, it boost the branching ratio of Λb→Σ​J/ψ→subscriptΛ𝑏Σ𝐽𝜓LambdabrightarrowSigma J/psi to the order of 10−7superscript10710-7, which is encouraging for future measurements. Therefore, this mode is particularly suitable to investigate the isospin violation and to extract the mixing angle. Acknowledgements. We would like to acknowledge Prof. Hsiang-nan Li and Prof. Yue-Long Shen for helpful discussions. This work is supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grants No. 12075086 and No. 11605060 and the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province under Grants No. A2021209002 and No. A2019209449.

sorted_papers.title:
SU(3) analysis of four-quark operators: $K\to\pi\pi$ and vacuum matrix
  elements
sorted_papers.abstract:
Hadronic matrix elements of local four-quark operators play a central role in
non-leptonic kaon decays, while vacuum matrix elements involving the same kind
of operators appear in inclusive dispersion relations, such as those relevant
in $\tau$-decay analyses. Using an $SU(3)_L\otimes SU(3)_R$ decomposition of
the operators, we derive generic relations between these matrix elements,
extending well-known results that link observables in the two different
sectors. Two relevant phenomenological applications are presented. First, we
determine the electroweak-penguin contribution to the kaon CP-violating ratio
$\varepsilon'/\varepsilon$, using the measured hadronic spectral functions in
$\tau$ decay. Second, we fit our $SU(3)$ dynamical parameters to the most
recent lattice data on $K\to\pi\pi$ matrix elements. The comparison of this
numerical fit with results from previous analytical approaches provides an
interesting anatomy of the $\Delta I = \frac{1}{2}$ enhancement, confirming old
suggestions about its underlying dynamical origin.
sorted_papers.reason:
论文核心基于SU(3)对称性分析四夸克算符，明确涉及U-spin与同位旋（isospin）对称性在K→ππ衰变与真空矩阵元中的应用，与查询中“SU(3)味对称性与U-spin、同位旋”完全匹配。研究通过SU(3)_L⊗SU(3)_R分解建立关联，直接处理U-spin和同位旋相关的矩阵元关系，且明确讨论ΔI=1/2增强机制，契合查询核心。

约束项检查：

sorted_papers.title: A U-spin Puzzle in $B$ Decays
sorted_papers.abstract:
We impose U spin symmetry ($SU(2)_{\rm Uspin}$) on the Hamiltonian for $B$
decays. As expected, we find the equality of amplitudes related by the exchange
$d \leftrightarrow s$. We also find that the amplitudes for the $\Delta S=0$
processes $B^0 \to \pi^+\pi^-$, $B_s^0\to\pi^+ K^-$ and $B^0\to K^+ K^-$ form a
U-spin triangle relation. The amplitudes for $B_s^0\to K^+ K^-$, $B^0\to\pi^-
K^+$ and $B_s^0\to\pi^+\pi^-$ form a similar $\Delta S=1$ triangle relation.
And these two triangles are related to one another by $d \leftrightarrow s$. We
perform fits to the observables for these six decays. If perfect U spin is
assumed, the fit is very poor. If U-spin-breaking contributions are added, we
find many scenarios that can explain the data. However, in all cases, 100\%
U-spin breaking is required, considerably larger than the naive expectation of
$\sim 20\%$. This is the U-spin puzzle; it may be strongly hinting at the
presence of new physics.

sorted_papers.title: Symmetries in particle physics: from nuclear isospin to the quark model
sorted_papers.abstract:
We present a concise pedagogic introduction to group representation theory
motivated by the historical developments surrounding the advent of the
Eightfold Way. Abstract definitions of groups and representations are avoided
in favour of the physical intuition of symmetries of the nuclear interaction.
The concept of nuclear isospin is used as a physical motivation to introduce
SU(2) and discuss the main techniques of representation theory. The discovery
of strange particles motivates extending the symmetry group to SU(3), at first
in the context of the Sakata model. We highlight the successes in fitting
mesons in the SU(3) octet, discuss the drawbacks of the Sakata model for
baryonic classifications, and how the Eightfold Way finally led to the quark
model. This approach has two major advantages: (i) the main concepts of the
theory of Lie groups are introduced and discussed without ever losing touch
with its applications in particle physics; (ii) it allows the beginner to study
group theory while also becoming acquainted with the historical developments of
particle physics that led to the concept of quarks. In particular, in this
pedagogical path the quarks appear as yet another class of particles predicted
from symmetry principles, rather than being introduced ad hoc for postulating
an SU(3) symmetry, as usually done in the literature.

sections.1: Gasiorowicz, Elementary particle physics (Wiley, New York, 1966).
sections.4: Georgi, Lie algebras in particle physics, 2nd ed., Vol
sections.20: Gasiorowicz, Elementary particle physics (Wiley, New York, 1966).
sections.23: Zee, Group Theory in a Nutshell for Physicists (Princeton University Press, USA, 2016).
sections.29: Gasiorowicz, Elementary particle physics (Wiley, New York, 1966).
sections.30: Gell-Mann, The eightfold way: A theory of strong interaction summetry (California Institute of Technology, 1961).

qualitative: SU(3) flavor symmetry extends the concept of isospin (SU(2)) to include the strange quark, allowing classification of hadrons into multiplets based on their flavor quantum numbers.

Isospin (SU(2)) symmetry treats the proton and neutron as two states of the same particle, related by a rotation in isospin space, and explains the near-degeneracy of nucleon masses and similar strong interaction properties.

U-spin is a subgroup of SU(3) flavor symmetry that exchanges up and strange quarks, analogous to isospin's exchange of up and down quarks, and is used to relate particles with the same quantum numbers except for the strange quark content.

The Eightfold Way uses SU(3) flavor symmetry to organize mesons and baryons into irreducible representations (octets and decuplets), predicting the existence and properties of undiscovered particles such as the Ω⁻.

The quark model emerges naturally from SU(3) symmetry as a physical realization of the underlying symmetry, with quarks as fundamental constituents carrying flavor quantum numbers that transform under SU(3).

While isospin is approximately conserved in strong interactions, SU(3) flavor symmetry is explicitly broken by the mass difference between the strange quark and the up/down quarks, leading to small splittings within SU(3) multiplets.
U-spin and isospin are both internal symmetries that act on the flavor space of quarks, with their generators forming part of the Lie algebra of SU(3).
quantitative: The isospin quantum number I = 1/2 for the nucleon doublet (proton and neutron), with I₃ = +1/2 for proton and I₃ = -1/2 for neutron.
The pion triplet (π⁺, π⁰, π⁻) forms an SU(2) isospin triplet with I = 1 and I₃ = +1, 0, -1.
The Clebsch-Gordan coefficient ratio for nucleon-nucleon to pion-nucleon scattering cross sections: σ(pp → pp) / σ(pn → pn) = 3, derived from SU(2) representation addition.

The Ω⁻ baryon, predicted by the Eightfold Way as the lowest state in the SU(3) decuplet with strangeness S = -3, charge Q = -1, and spin J = 3/2, was discovered in 1964 with properties matching the prediction.

The mass splitting within the SU(3) octet (e.g., between Σ⁺, Σ⁰, Σ⁻) is approximately 10–15 MeV, reflecting the breaking of SU(3) symmetry due to the strange quark mass being larger than up/down quark masses.

The U-spin transformation exchanges u ↔ s quarks, and the U-spin quantum numbers (U and U₃) are used to classify particles: for example, the K⁺ (u s̄) and K⁰ (d s̄) form a U-spin doublet with U = 1/2, U₃ = +1/2 and -1/2 respectively.

content_sections.I Introduction: Throughout the history of particle physics, symmetry principles have proven to be extremely productive tools for model building. The recognition of an underlying symmetry of Nature allows one to predict properties of fundamental particles, to obtain quantitative predictions on relations between observables, and even to anticipate the precise forms of interaction terms in the Lagrangian. In fact, the entire edifice of the Standard Model and its attempted low-energy extensions rests upon symmetry principles such as gauge invariance, a statement about local symmetries of the fundamental interactions. It is therefore essential that any novice in particle physics be fluent in the mathematical description of symmetries and their physical consequences. This is precisely the scope of the discipline called group representation theory. Symmetries of physical laws are most easily illustrated in the context of Lorentz covariance and relativity. In this case the transformation between inertial frames can be readily visualized, and representation theory can be motivated as a tool for building objects that transform in a well-defined way under a change of frame, allowing different observers to compare their experimental outputs. But the Lorentz group is certainly not the simplest, and the study of its representations relies on some previous knowledge of SU. On the other hand, more basic group structures could be introduced by appealing to internal symmetries, but attempting to use the Standard Model gauge groups as motivation makes the situation significantly murkier. To explain concepts such as weak isospin and the SUL symmetry of weak interactions, one must invoke a notion of “similarity” between electrons and neutrinos, between up- and down-type quarks. But any such similarity is blatantly badly broken by the sharp discrepancy in these particles’ masses. And it’s not helpful to explain this as a manifestation of symmetry breaking by the Higgs mechanism: the elegance of this solution is only appreciated if one already grasped the importance of enforcing these symmetries in the first place. One can safely say that attempting to build an intuition of internal symmetries by invoking a case where it is badly broken is not a very pedagogic approach. The situation is improved a little if one starts from the SUcsubscript3𝑐c case of chromodynamics, since this one is indeed preserved. However, this also requires introducing new concepts such as color charge, and has the drawback of using the more complicated SU3 group as a starting point. An alternative and more pedagogic path is to discuss internal symmetries starting from their historical origins, namely Heisenberg’s notion of nuclear isospin. For that, the only prerequisite are the concepts of protons and neutrons familiar to any undergraduate student. One can then use representation theory to predict the properties of the pions, and predict the ratio of nuclear reaction cross sections from Clebsch-Gordan coefficients. Then, by extending the symmetry group from SU2 to SU3, one can understand hadronic classification schemes, discuss the successful prediction of the Ω−superscriptΩOmega- particle, and introduce the concept of quarks in their appropriate historical background. While this approach can be found in many excellent textbooks on the subject, each has its own goals and emphasis, leaving out a good deal of important material. Some discuss much of the physics without going into detail on group theoretical developments, e. g. refs. citebib.bib1, bib.bib2. Others emphasize only the necessary group theory for the subsequent application to the Standard Model, leaving out important applications on nuclear physics, e. g. citebib.bib3. Others, while providing a very thorough discussion of the history behind these symmetry principles, were written before the full development and confirmation of the quark model and hence would not discuss it fullycitebib.bib41It is also worth noticing that ref. citebib.bib4 uses this hadronic classification approach to introduce the use of symmetries in particle physics not by pure choice, but also because the more fundamental internal symmetries of the weak and strong interactions were still unknown at the time of its writing. . There is also a plethora of pedagogic materials aimed at introducing group theory for particle physicistscitebib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7. Many of these also discuss the use of internal symmetries at the dawn of nuclear physics, but the physics is inevitably swamped in the dense formalism of generic group theoretical results. Our purpose in this paper is to offer a concise yet self-contained introduction to the main elements of group representation theory through its application to the hadronic classification schemes, starting with Heisenberg’s idea of isospin and culminating in the Eightfold Way and the quark model. Although the content presented here is not new, the novelty is in the presentation sequence. We have aimed for a healthy balance between the formal aspects of representation theory and the underlying developments in hadronic physics. In doing so we omitted formal definitions of group theory (some of which we put in the appendix), but without sacrificing the overall understanding of the main concepts and techniques. In fact, these formal definitions can even obscure rather than enlighten the topic to the very beginner. Though this presentation does not exhaust the topic, it should allow the reader to transition smoothly to other standard texts on the subject, including (but not restricted to) those cited above. The paper is organized as follows. In section we motivate the concept of nuclear isospin, show that it is associated to the SU2 group, and build its representations. Then in section we discuss rules for summing isospin, i. e. building representations related to composite states. This culminates in the Clebsch-Gordan coefficients, which we use to predict the ratio of certain nucleon + nucleon and pion + nucleon scattering cross-sections. In section we extend the symmetry group to SU3 to account for the “particle zoo” of the 1940s and 50s and discuss its representations, culminating in the Eightfold Way and the quark model. Our conclusions are reserved for section.
content_sections.II Nuclear isospin: The discovery of the neutron in 1932, and the recognition of its role as a constituent of the atomic nucleus, immediately required a postulated new interaction responsible for keeping neutrons and protons bound together. The first concrete attempt at a description of this force was put forward by Heisenberg in his seminal 1932 series of paperscitebib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, merely a few months after the reports on the discovery of the neutron2See alsocitebib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13 for examples and discussions on the early history of the concept of isospin in nuclear physics. . Motivated by the short-ranged nature of the nuclear force, Heisenberg thought of this interaction as analogous to the covalent bond in the dihydrogen cation H2+superscriptsubscript𝐻2H2+citebib.bib14, depicted in figure (left). In this case, two protons are held together by the exchange of an electron, which takes place when the two orbitals overlap. As soon as the protons are separated by a distance larger than the orbital size, i. e. the Bohr radius ∼10−10similar-toabsentsuperscript1010sim 10-10m, the exchange ceases and the electron becomes attached to one of protons only, turning it into a neutral H𝐻H atom which no longer interacts electromagnetically with the remaining H+superscript𝐻H+ ion. Heisenberg intuited that a similar mechanism was responsible for the short-ranged nuclear force, but involving the exchange of another type of charge altogether. Specifically, he postulated that the particles in the nucleus — so-called nucleons — carry another charge whose value characterizes them as a proton or a neutron. The nucleon is thus a two-level system, analogous to a spin-1/2 particle: the +1/212+1/2 state corresponds to a proton, the −1/212-1/2 to a neutron. Due to this similarity with spin, this charge is called isospin3In the beginning people called it isotopic spin. But in reality the transformation protons ↔↔leftrightarrow neutrons relates isobar nuclei, rather than isotopes, so other people called it isobaric spin. Eventually the notation was simplified to isospin. .
content_sections.II Nuclear isospin II.1 Charge independe: Now, protons and neutrons have remarkably near degenerate massescitebib.bib15, mp=938.27208816±0.00000029​MeV,mn=939.5654205±0.0000005​MeV,formulae-sequencesubscript𝑚𝑝plus-or-minus938.272088160.00000029MeVsubscript𝑚𝑛plus-or-minus939.56542050.0000005MeVbeginsplitmp=938.27208816pm 0.00000029textMeV, mn=939.5654205pm 0.0000005textMeV,endsplit amounting to a relative mass difference mn−mp(mn+mp2)≈0.14%. subscript𝑚𝑛subscript𝑚𝑝subscript𝑚𝑛subscript𝑚𝑝2percent0.14fracmn-mpleft(fracmn+mp2right)approx 0.14%. This means that a “flip” in the isospin of a free nucleon involves a comparably negligible amount of energy, so that free protons and free neutrons are approximately degenerate states. As for when they are interacting inside the nucleus, as soon as more information was gathered on the phenomenology of this nuclear force, it gradually became clear that this interaction did not seem to distinguish protons from neutrons. This is known as the approximate “charge independence” of the nuclear force. One evidence for this comes from the similar properties of proton-proton, proton-neutron and neutron-neutron scatterings (discounting for electromagnetic effects, which affect each of these processes differently)citebib.bib16, bib.bib17. Quantitatively, the nuclear potentials describing these three processes differ from one another by no more than ∼2.5%similar-toabsentpercent2.5sim 2.5%citebib.bib17. Another evidence comes from the strikingly similar energy levels of mirror nuclei, such as P and S, as shown in figure. These nuclei are related to one another by a proton ↔↔leftrightarrow neutron exchange4The P nucleus has 15 protons and 16 neutrons, whereas S has 16 protons and 15 neutrons. . Small differences in energy levels of these nuclei are well accounted for by electromagnetic effectscitebib.bib18, which again shows that the nuclear interaction (which is dominant inside the nucleus) does not sharply differentiate protons and neutrons. Altogether, this means that a “rotation” in isospin space is an approximate symmetry of nature. By this we mean that there exist certain transformations that can be performed over the physical system while keeping its dynamics completely unaltered. The system before and after the transformation are completely identical. The mere recognition of this fact has deep physical implications, as we will see in the following.
content_sections.II Nuclear isospin II.2 Isospin and SU(2: Let us put the above discussion in more formal terms. We have argued above that the nuclear interaction does not seem to distinguish protons from neutrons5Obviously the electromagnetic interaction is able to distinguish them, but its effects are subdominant compared to the nuclear force, which is typically much stronger. . From the previous discussion, we can postulate that, as far as the nuclear interaction is concerned, the proton and the neutron behave as the same kind of particle, which we call the nucleon (i. e. a particle of the atomic nucleus). This nucleon can be found in two different states in Nature, p=,n=,formulae-sequence𝑝matrix10𝑛matrix01p=beginpmatrix1 0endpmatrix,qquad n=beginpmatrix0 1endpmatrix, corresponding to “isospin up” and “isospin down” states, respectively. In general, a nucleon state can be found as a superposition described by N=(ψpψn)=ψp​p+ψn​n. 𝑁matrixsubscript𝜓𝑝subscript𝜓𝑛subscript𝜓𝑝𝑝subscript𝜓𝑛𝑛N=beginpmatrixpsip psinendpmatrix=psip,p+psin,n. Isospin symmetry means that any redefinition of p𝑝p and n𝑛n describes an equivalent physics. This transformation can be described via the matrix N′=U​N,withU=(αβγδ). formulae-sequencesuperscript𝑁′𝑈𝑁with𝑈matrix𝛼𝛽𝛾𝛿Nprime=UN,quadtextwithquad U=beginpmatrixalphabeta gammadeltaendpmatrix. Since the physics is unaltered by this transformation, all probabilities must be the same when calculated with primed or unprimed states. In particular, N†​N=N′⁣†​N′=N†​U†​U​N. superscript𝑁†𝑁superscript𝑁′†superscript𝑁′superscript𝑁†superscript𝑈†𝑈𝑁NdaggerN=Nprime,daggerNprime=NdaggerUdaggerUN. This implies that U𝑈U must be unitary, U†​U=𝟙2×2. superscript𝑈†𝑈subscript122UdaggerU=mathds12times 2. Consider now the matrix determinant 1=det𝟙2×2=det(U†​U)=(detU†)​detU=|detU|2.1subscript122superscript𝑈†𝑈superscript𝑈†𝑈superscript𝑈2beginsplit1=detmathds12times 2=det(UdaggerU) =(det Udagger)det U=|det U|2. endsplit Since U𝑈U is a complex matrix, it follows that the determinant of U𝑈U is an overall complex phase, detU=exp⁡(i​θ)𝑈𝑖𝜃det U=exp(itheta) for some θ∈ℝ𝜃ℝthetainR. It is well known that multiplying a quantum state by an overall complex phase has no physical implications, so we can restrict outselves to transformations with detU=1𝑈1det U=1. Put another way, the only novelty in eq. lies in the invariance of physics under a 2×2222times 2 unitary transformation with detU=1𝑈1det U=1. The set of matrices satisfying these conditions forms a group called SU6This is an example of a “special unitary” group. The “S” stands for “special”, meaning unitary determinant. The “U” stands for “unitary matrices” and the “2” means the matrices are 2×2222times 2. . The transformation described in eq. is analogous to a “rotation” in isospin space. In other words, it is a redefinition of what we call “up” and “down” or “proton” and “neutron” states (in much the same way that a spacetime rotation is a redefinition of what we call x,y𝑥𝑦x,y and z𝑧z axes). This is an example of an internal symmetry of nature, so-called because the transformation does not act on spacetime coordinates (like usual spatial rotations), but involves field redefinitions. The symmetry statement, in this case, is that the nuclear interaction does not distinguish protons and neutrons, so a redefinition of these fields (a re-orientation of directions in this field space) has no impact on the physical results. The recognition that a “rotation” in isospin space is a symmetry of the nuclear interaction has profound consequences. For example, consider a particle that carries some isospin, and therefore interacts via the nuclear force. If we perform an isospin redefinition, the nature of this particle, as far as the nuclear force is concerned, must remain unchanged. The interaction treats it as the same kind of particle, only in a different isospin state (otherwise this transformation wouldn’t be a symmetry). Consequently, each type of particle belongs to a vector space where the state vectors can be related to one another by some isospin transformation. In other words, the transformation does not map a state to another belonging to a different vector space, which would represent a different kind of particle. This statement is so important that it is worth phrasing it another way. If we redefine isospin, there must be a dictionary that allows us to find the “new” state of this particle in terms of the pre-transformed one. Only then would we be able to relate the physics under this new definition to the previous description and make sure that it remains indeed unaltered7Thinking about spacetime symmetries could make this argument simpler to grasp. Suppose 𝒮𝒮S is an inertial observer at rest with respect to some electric charge, and 𝒮′superscript𝒮′Sprime sees it moving with constant velocity. Clearly 𝒮𝒮S sees only a radial electric field emanating from the charge, while 𝒮′superscript𝒮′Sprime sees also a current and therefore a magnetic field as well. They disagree as to the intensity of the electric and magnetic fields, as well as to the charge and current distribution. But there must be a way to translate one’s observations into the other’s, for only then can they mutually communicate about their experiments and reach an agreement as to the universality of Maxwell’s equations. . The state space of a particle is the space of all objects that can be transformed into one another by an isospin transformation. Technically, this means that physical states must belong to representations of the SU symmetry group. We have already seen an instance of this, when we said that “proton” and “neutron” are two different states of the nucleon, which means that the space of nucleon states is two-dimensional. Other particles may belong to higher-dimensional representations. How do we construct these representation spaces? Starting from one reference state |ψ⟩ket𝜓|psi, we can apply an SU transformation (an isospin redefinition), mapping this state onto some |ψ′⟩ketsuperscript𝜓′|psiprime. This new state still describes the same particle, only in a different isospin configuration. Then perform another isospin redefinition, taking us from |ψ′⟩ketsuperscript𝜓′|psiprime to |ψ′′⟩ketsuperscript𝜓′′|psiprimeprime, and so on. If the state space of such particle is finite dimensional, eventually this procedure will cease to produce linearly independent vectors. Thus, by performing all possible symmetry transformations on a reference state, one can obtain the whole state space of the system. By construction all transformations will map this space onto itself, and all states are relatable to one another by one such transformation: then we will have built a representation. The remaining question is: what does an isospin redefinition looks like when acting on higher-dimensional spaces? Eq. reveals only how it affects the two-dimensional (isospin 1/2121/2) representation. Before trying to generalize it, it is worth looking into this representation in more detail.
content_sections.II Nuclear isospin II.3 Infinitesimal tr: The group SU has a structure that simplifies enormously our task of finding representations: it is a group of continuous transformations, meaning that we can “rotate” in isospin space by an infinitesimal amount. Think about infinitesimal parameters in equation, for instance. Such a continuous group is called a Lie group. Because of this, instead of having to perform all possible rotations on our reference state |ψ⟩ket𝜓|psi to build the representation space, we need only perform infinitesimal rotations along independent “directions”. What does the matrix U𝑈U in eq. looks like for infinitesimal transformations? By definition this is very close to “doing nothing” (we are rotating by tiny, insignificant “angles”), so the transformation matrix must be close to the identity. Indeed, we could perform a Taylor expansion and, neglecting terms of quadratic order or higher, one expects U=𝟙2×2+i​ω,ω=(ω11ω12ω21ω22). formulae-sequence𝑈subscript122𝑖𝜔𝜔matrixsubscript𝜔11subscript𝜔12subscript𝜔21subscript𝜔22U=mathds12times 2+iomega,qquadomega=beginpmatrixomega11omega12 omega21omega22endpmatrix. Without this factor it would be anti-hermitean, which wouldn’t change much of the conclusions below, but would introduce some inconvenient factors of (-1). ω=ω†. 𝜔superscript𝜔†omega=omegadagger. Notice that T3subscript𝑇3T3 is an operator whose eigenvectors are the p𝑝p and n𝑛n states. It tells us the isospin configuration of the nucleon in a certain state. On the other hand T1subscript𝑇1T1 and T2subscript𝑇2T2 can be combined into T1+i​T2=andT1−i​T2=,formulae-sequencesubscript𝑇1𝑖subscript𝑇2matrix0100andsubscript𝑇1𝑖subscript𝑇2matrix0010T1+iT2=beginpmatrix01 00endpmatrixquadtextandquad T1-iT2=beginpmatrix00 10endpmatrix, which act on the nucleon states such that (T1+i​T2)​n=p,(T1−i​T2)​p=n,formulae-sequencesubscript𝑇1𝑖subscript𝑇2𝑛𝑝subscript𝑇1𝑖subscript𝑇2𝑝𝑛beginsplit(T1+iT2),n=p, (T1-iT2),p=n,endsplit i. e. they respectively raise and lower the value of isospin by one unit. For this reason they are called “raising” and “lowering” operators. Thus the state space of nucleons can be constructed by starting from the p𝑝p state and continuously applying the lowering operator. Applying it once yields n𝑛n. Applying it again on n𝑛n yields the zero vector: no new state is obtained and the procedure stops. Thus the space of nucleon states is two-dimensional.

Thus the representations of SU are characterized by half-integers I=0,12,1,32,2,…,𝐼0121322…I=0,frac12,1,frac32,2,ldots, and each has dimension 2​I+12𝐼12I+1 corresponding to states m=−I,−I+1,…,I−1,I. 𝑚𝐼𝐼1…𝐼1𝐼m=-I,-I+1,ldots,I-1,I. It is now worth pausing to summarize these achievements. First, note that all of the above developments follow from the commutation relations of the generators of infinitesimal transformations, eq. . This is a general result that underlies the theory of Lie groups: we can build representations just be looking at how states transform under infinitesimal transformations. In other words, we can focus almost exclusively on the Lie algebra. Second, we see that the existence of a symmetry allows us to partition the entire state space of our theory into a number of independent subspaces. The procedure of finding representations of the symmetry group can then be seen as a way of organizing the total Hilbert space of states of a system. Indeed, we have defined the concept of “representation space” such that, under a symmetry transformation, the states of each of these subspaces transform only among themselves: there is no isospin rotation that can take a state in the I𝐼I space to a different I′superscript𝐼′Iprime. Thus the transformation matrix acting on the total state space can be decomposed in block diagonal form14The UI=1/2subscript𝑈𝐼12UI=1/2 suatrix is the one given in eq. . , U=(UI=0UI=1/2UI=1⋱).
