The entire Hilbert space is said to be reducible. The subspaces I=0,1/2,1,…𝐼0121…I=0,1/2,1,ldots, on the other hand, are irreducible representations, because (by construction) they cannot be reduced any further (they have been built in such a way that every state is relatable to another by a transformation). Each of these irreducible subspaces correspond to different particle types as seen by the nuclear interaction.
content_sections.II Nuclear isospin II.5 Symmetries and c: There is more. The very definition of isospin transformations being a symmetry of nature implies that such transformations do not affect the energy of the system. This means that, if H𝐻H is the hamiltonian of the system, with eigenstates |ψn⟩ketsubscript𝜓𝑛ketpsin, then U​|ψn⟩𝑈ketsubscript𝜓𝑛Uketpsin is still an eigenstate with same eigenvalue, so H​(U​|ψn⟩)=En​U​|ψn⟩=U​H​|ψn⟩. 𝐻𝑈ketsubscript𝜓𝑛subscript𝐸𝑛𝑈ketsubscript𝜓𝑛𝑈𝐻ketsubscript𝜓𝑛H(Uketpsin)=EnUketpsin=UHketpsin. Since the |ψn⟩ketsubscript𝜓𝑛ketpsin form a basis of the state space (because H𝐻H is hermitian) is follows that [H,U]=0𝐻𝑈0[H,U]=0, i. e. the hamiltonian commutes with the transformation operations. This means we can diagonalize both operators simultaneously, i. e. the |I,m⟩ket𝐼𝑚ketI,m states are also eigenstates of H𝐻H, with definite energy. Therefore, the existence of a symmetry facilitates our task of finding solutions to the Schrödinger equation, since we may restrict ourselves to these subspaces to begin with15This is what we do when solving the hydrogen atom (or any problem with spherical symmetry) with separation of variables: the angular equation will always result in the spherical harmonics, which belong to (infinite dimensional) representations of the rotation group, and the problem reduces to solving the radial equation only. . Moreover, if I≠I′𝐼superscript𝐼′Ineq Iprime then ⟨I′,m′|H|I,m⟩∼⟨I′,m′|I,m⟩=0,similar-toquantum-operator-productsuperscript𝐼′superscript𝑚′𝐻𝐼𝑚inner-productsuperscript𝐼′superscript𝑚′𝐼𝑚0 Iprime,mprime|H|I,msim Iprime,mprime|I,m=0, since states belonging to different representations are eigenstates of a hermitean operator T2superscript𝑇2T2 with different eigenvalues and are therefore orthogonal. But the left-hand side of this equation is the transition amplitude between states with I𝐼I and I′superscript𝐼′Iprime. In other words, it is the probability amplitude that a system starts in a state in the I𝐼I representation and is later16The Schrödinger equation can be written as H​|ψ​(t)⟩=i​∂∂t​|ψ​(t)⟩≃i​|ψ​(t+δ​t)⟩−|ψ​(t)⟩δ​t𝐻ket𝜓𝑡𝑖𝑡ket𝜓𝑡similar-to-or-equals𝑖ket𝜓𝑡𝛿𝑡ket𝜓𝑡𝛿𝑡H|psi(t)=ifracpartialpartial t|psi(t)simeq ifrac|psi(t+delta t)-|psi(t)delta t, or |ψ​(t+δ​t)⟩=(𝟙−i​δ​t​H)​|ψ​(t)⟩ket𝜓𝑡𝛿𝑡1𝑖𝛿𝑡𝐻ket𝜓𝑡|psi(t+delta t)=(mathds1-idelta tH)|psi(t). We then see that H𝐻H is the generator of infinitesimal time translations, in much the same way as the Jisubscript𝐽𝑖Ji are generators of infinitesimal isospin rotations, as discussed above. found with total isospin I′superscript𝐼′Iprime. It follows that, if isospin is a symmetry of the hamiltonian, then the total isospin is conserved17This is a particular example of a general theorem due to Emmy Noether, which says that if a system has a continuous symmetry (i. e. if the symmetry holds for infinitesimal transformations) then there is some conserved charge associated to this symmetry. .
content_sections.II Nuclear isospin II.6 Fitting particle: Now let us look into some of these representations in detail and interpret them physically in the context of isospin. Let us first consider the I=0𝐼0I=0 representation, which is one-dimensional. Because of this dimensionality, we call the state |I=0,m=0⟩ketformulae-sequence𝐼0𝑚0ketI=0,m=0 a singlet. Under an isospin rotation it transforms as |0,0⟩→UI=0​|0,0⟩,→ket00superscript𝑈𝐼0ket00ket0,0to UI=0ket0,0, with UI=0subscript𝑈𝐼0UI=0 a one-dimensional unitary matrix of unit determinant, which implies UI=0=1superscript𝑈𝐼01UI=0=1. In other words, a I=0𝐼0I=0 state is unaffected by an isospin redefinition. This can only mean that this particle carries no isospin at all, and therefore does not interact via the nuclear force. So any particle that is neuter under this force belongs to this isospin representation. The I=1/2𝐼12I=1/2 representation is two-dimensional. It is also called the fundamental representation18 A fundamental representation is one that serves as building blocks for other higher-dimensional representations via tensor product operations. But for unitary groups SU(n𝑛n) the fundamental representation coincides with the so-called defining representation, which consists of the n𝑛n-dimensional vector space where the very definition of these matrices act. For instance, SU(n𝑛n) is defined as the group of n×n𝑛𝑛ntimes n unitary matrices, and these matrices can be seen as operators acting on n𝑛n-dimensional vectors, so this n𝑛n-dimensional representation is the defining representation (and also a fundamental) of SU(n𝑛n). , because all the others can be built from tensor products of this space with itself, as we will see in the next section. We have already met particles that fit into this space: the proton and the neutron, corresponding to states p=|1/2,+1/2⟩𝑝ket1212p=ket1/2,+1/2 and n=|1/2,−1/2⟩𝑛ket1212n=ket1/2,-1/2. Moving on to the I=1𝐼1I=1 representation, we know this is a 3-dimensional space, so the corresponding states m=−1,0,1𝑚101m,0,1 are said to form a triplet. If this representation is realized in nature, we would expect to find three particles with near-degenerate masses that take part in the nuclear interaction. Such particles indeed exist: the pions π+superscript𝜋pi+, π0superscript𝜋0pi0, and π−superscript𝜋pi- have massescitebib.bib15 mπ±=139.57039±0.00018​MeV,mπ0=134.9768±0.0005​MeV,formulae-sequencesubscript𝑚superscript𝜋plus-or-minusplus-or-minus139.570390.00018MeVsubscript𝑚superscript𝜋0plus-or-minus134.97680.0005MeVbeginsplitmpipm=139.57039pm 0.00018textMeV, mpi0=134.9768pm 0.0005textMeV,endsplit with a relative mass difference mπ±−mπ0mπavg∼3.3%. similar-tosubscript𝑚superscript𝜋plus-or-minussubscript𝑚superscript𝜋0subscript𝑚superscript𝜋avgpercent3.3fracmpipm-mpi0mpitextavgsim 3.3%. Just like the I=1/2𝐼12I=1/2 representation, the I=1𝐼1I=1 representation has a special name. It is called the adjoint representation. Recall that finding a representation means finding a vector space of states where the generators Tisubscript𝑇𝑖Ti act as transformations. The I=1/2𝐼12I=1/2 representation is a 2-dimensional complex vector space where the generators act as in eq. . But the generators T1,T2subscript𝑇1subscript𝑇2T1,T2 and T3subscript𝑇3T3 also form a vector space among themselves, being a basis of the space of traceless hermitean matrices! So what if we use this space, i. e. the algebra itself, as the representation space? We then have a state space constituted of |T1⟩,|T2⟩ketsubscript𝑇1ketsubscript𝑇2|T1,|T2 and |T3⟩ketsubscript𝑇3|T3, and we can define the action of a generator over another via the commutator, i. e. Ti​|Tj⟩≡[Ti,Tj]. subscript𝑇𝑖ketsubscript𝑇𝑗subscript𝑇𝑖subscript𝑇𝑗TiketTjequiv[Ti,Tj]. So, in this representation, the generators will act both as vectors and as operators acting on these vectors. For example, we can use the explicit form of the commutation relations to define the matrix of the T3subscript𝑇3T3 operator acting on T−,T3,T+subscript𝑇subscript𝑇3subscript𝑇T-,T3,T+ as T3=(−10+1). subscript𝑇3matrix1missing-subexpressionmissing-subexpressionmissing-subexpression0missing-subexpressionmissing-subexpressionmissing-subexpression1T3=beginpmatrix-1 0 +1endpmatrix. We can associate |T−⟩,|T3⟩ketsubscript𝑇ketsubscript𝑇3ketT-,ketT3 and |T+⟩ketsubscript𝑇ketT+ with the π−,π0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋0pi-,pi0 and π+superscript𝜋pi+, respectively. Naturally, every Lie algebra (i. e. the algebra of infinitesimal transformations related to an arbitrary continuous group) has the adjoint representation. We will see another example in section below.
content_sections.II Nuclear isospin II.7 Charges, isospin: Looking at the matrix form of T3subscript𝑇3T3 in the adjoint representation, eq. , one cannot help but noticing that its eigenvalues are precisely the electric charges of the pions (which are the particles belonging in this adjoint representation). We can thus say that, in the adjoint representation, the charge operator is Qπ=T3. subscript𝑄𝜋subscript𝑇3Qpi=T3. If we try to apply this same rule to the fundamental representation, the outcome would be Q​p=+12​p𝑄𝑝12𝑝Q,p=+frac12p and Q​n=−12​n𝑄𝑛12𝑛Q,n=-frac12n, meaning the proton and neutron would be eigenstates of charge with eigenvalues ±1/2plus-or-minus12pm 1/2. This is clearly wrong. However, this problem can be remedied by defining a new quantum number B𝐵B, called baryon number, such that Q=T3+B2. 𝑄subscript𝑇3𝐵2Q=T3+fracB2. Then the correct charges are found by attributing B=1𝐵1B=1 for protons and neutrons, and B=0𝐵0B=0 for pions. Thus we say that nucleons are baryons (i. e. have baryonic number), whereas pions belong to another class of particles called mesons. It turns out that this baryon number B𝐵B has a deeper physical meaning than the above argument make it seems. More than being a mere adjustment factor to the charge formula, this number is actually a conserved charge of all nuclear reactions. The reader can check that this is indeed the case for all the reactions discussed henceforth in this paper.
content_sections.III Sum of isospin and Clebsch-Gordan co: Consider now a system composed of two particles, each carrying some total isospin I1subscript𝐼1I1 and I2subscript𝐼2I2. The composite system must also belong to some SU representation, say, one with total isospin T𝑇T. The question of which values of T𝑇T are allowed for given I1subscript𝐼1I1 and I2subscript𝐼2I2 is known as the problem of how to sum isospin (or any quantity that behaves as angular momentum) in quantum mechanics.

It turns out that only one such nucleus exists as a stable configuration in nature: the deuteron, composed of one proton and one neutron. A bound state of two protons and two neutrons turns out to be unstable. Now, in the list of states above there are two of them composed of proton+neutron: the |1,0⟩ket10|1,0 and the |0,0⟩ket00|0,0. Which corresponds to the deuteron? Note that the states p​p𝑝𝑝pp and n​n𝑛𝑛nn are obtainable from |1,0⟩ket10|1,0 via a isospin rotation, and if this is a symmetry then the three states in the isospin =1absent1=1 triplet would all be physically equivalent. Since p​p𝑝𝑝pp and n​n𝑛𝑛nn are not found as stable states in nature, |1,0⟩ket10|1,0 can’t be either. Thus isospin symmetry establishes the total isospin of the deuteron to be 00. Let us call H𝐻H the Hamiltonian operator describing the dynamics of these nuclear interactions. In principle if we know H𝐻H we can determine the amplitude of any such process via ℳ=⟨final|H|initial⟩. ℳquantum-operator-productfinal𝐻initialM=textfinal|H|textinitial. However, we know that the nuclear interaction is rather non-trivial (being a residual of the more fundamental interaction between quarks), so one can expect H𝐻H to have a complicated form. Moreover, we might not know this form beforehand (which was the case when physicists were still developing the theory of the nuclear interaction in the early 1930s). But even without this knowledge we can already make quantitative statements about the cross sections of the reactions above, based only on symmetry! One way to proceed, in this case, would be to use Shmushkevich’s principlecitebib.bib19, bib.bib20, which follows from isospin symmetry. It states that if the population of reactants is uniformly distributed among all its possible isospin states, then the population of products will also be uniform (according to this same criterion) at all times. For instante, consider a uniform beam of nucleons incident on a uniform target of nucleons. Uniform distribution means that, for each 100 pairs of nucleons on beam/target, half of them will be protons and half neutrons. Each pair will react according to one of the four reactions above, and in the end we will have 100 deuterons and 100 pions. The deuteron is a singlet, so its distribution will be uniform regardless. Now, Shmushkevich’s principle states that the pions must be uniformly distributed among π−,π0,π+superscript𝜋superscript𝜋0superscript𝜋pi-,pi0,pi+. But there are two channels that can lead to a π0superscript𝜋0pi0, namely (b) and (c), while only (a) can lead to π+superscript𝜋pi+ and only (d) can lead to π−superscript𝜋pi-. Thus a uniform distribution of the pions in the final products can only be achieved if (a) and (d) are twice more efficient than (b) and (c). In other words, the cross sections must be in the ratio σa:σb:σc:σd=2:1:1:2. :subscript𝜎𝑎subscript𝜎𝑏:subscript𝜎𝑐:subscript𝜎𝑑2:1:1:2sigmaa:sigmab:sigmac:sigmad=2:1:1:2. Incidentally, since the ΔΔDelta belongs in the I=3/2𝐼32I=3/2 representation, we expect to find 2​I+1=42𝐼142I+1=4 such particles of approximately degenerate masses. This is indeed in agreement with the experiments, which have observed a Δ++superscriptΔabsentDelta++, Δ+superscriptΔDelta+, Δ0superscriptΔ0Delta0 and Δ−superscriptΔDelta-.
content_sections.IV Extending the symmetry IV.1 Strange p: If the world was composed only of protons, neutrons and pions, it seems that an SU isospin symmetry would be an excellent explanation for the classification and behaviour of these particles. But in the late 1940s and early 1950s physicists were confronted with the discovery of a number of other particles. First, cloud chamber photographs of cosmic rays showed some unusually heavy particles decaying into pions citebib.bib22, bib.bib23, evidencing yet unknown mesons which later came to be known as the kaons. In 1950 a neutral baryon decaying into protons and pions was discovered in cosmic ray events, which later came to be dubbed the Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0 baryoncitebib.bib24. It did not take long to notice that there was something strange about these particles. For instance, Λ0+2​πsuperscriptΛ02𝜋Lambda0+2pi’s could be produced copiously when colliding proton + pions, from which one can infer the transition amplitude ⟨Λ0​π​π|H|p​π⟩quantum-operator-productsuperscriptΛ0𝜋𝜋𝐻𝑝𝜋Lambda0pipi|H|ppi. If one then used this amplitude to estimate the decay lifetime of Λ0→p+π−→superscriptΛ0𝑝superscript𝜋Lambda0to p+pi-, one would find ∼10−23similar-toabsentsuperscript1023sim 10-23s. However, observations showed that the correct lifetime was ∼10−10similar-toabsentsuperscript1010sim 10-10s: a discrepancy of 13 orders of magnitude! Clearly, production and decay were very different mechanisms for Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0 (and the same was true for the kaons). The way out of the problem involved two steps. At first, it was postulated that proton+pion collisions produce Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0 only in association with kaons, π−+p→Λ0→superscript𝜋𝑝superscriptΛ0displaystylepi-+ptoLambda0 +K0||superscript𝐾0displaystyle+underset|K0 →π−+π+. →absentsuperscript𝜋superscript𝜋displaystylequad,topi-+pi+. This is known as “associated production”, namely that these “exotic” particles would always be produced in pairs. For some time this was enough to explain all observations, but soon new reactions were observed involving an odd number of exotic particles, while other reactions involving pairs were never observed despite being allowed by the conjecture22The hypothesis of associated production came into crisis when the reaction π−+p→Ξ−+K0+K+→superscript𝜋𝑝superscriptΞsuperscript𝐾0superscript𝐾pi-+prightarrowXi-+K0+K+ was observed (with Ξ−superscriptΞXi- an exotic baryon heavier than the proton), while the reaction π−+p→Ξ−+K+→superscript𝜋𝑝superscriptΞsuperscript𝐾pi-+prightarrowXi-+K+, allowed by the conjecture, was not. These can be explained by assigning to Ξ−superscriptΞXi- a strangeness value of S=−2𝑆2S=-2 and assuming that strangeness is an approximately conserved charge. . Later it was recognized that there was another (approximately valid) conservation law involved: one can assign to these new particles a new quantum charge, which was (appropriately enough) called strangeness, such that SΛ0=−1subscript𝑆superscriptΛ01SLambda0 and SK0=+1subscript𝑆superscript𝐾01SK0=+1. This way, the production channel above conserves strangeness (Sbefore=Safter=0subscript𝑆beforesubscript𝑆after0Stextbefore=Stextafter=0), whereas the decays Λ0→p+π−→superscriptΛ0𝑝superscript𝜋Lambda0to p+pi- and K0→π−+π+→superscript𝐾0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋K0topi-+pi+ do not. This explains why the decays are much less favourable than the production. Now, if we were to fit these strange particles in isospin representations, we would be forced to place the Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0 in a singlet state, since no other particle looks like an SU partner23The discovery of other strange particles called Σ±superscriptΣplus-or-minusSigmapm could, upon first sight, lead to a tentative classification of (Σ−,Λ0,Σ+)superscriptΣsuperscriptΛ0superscriptΣ(Sigma-,Lambda0,Sigma+) as an isospin triplet. However, soon a new strange particle Σ0superscriptΣ0Sigma0 was discovered through the decay Σ0→Λ0+γ→superscriptΣ0superscriptΛ0𝛾Sigma0toLambda0+gamma, with a mass that was much closer to Σ±superscriptΣplus-or-minusSigmapm. So the true isospin triplet is composed of (Σ−,Σ0,Σ+)superscriptΣsuperscriptΣ0superscriptΣ(Sigma-,Sigma0,Sigma+), leaving Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0 as a singlet. of Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0. In this case the expression for the charge operator would again fail to give the correct charge of Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0, forcing us to modify it as Q=T3+B+S2. 𝑄subscript𝑇3𝐵𝑆2Q=T3+fracB+S2. If we now try to assign quantum numbers to the kaons to meet their correct charges, we would be led to assign T3=−1/2subscript𝑇312T3/2 to K0superscript𝐾0K0 (to match QK0=0subscript𝑄superscript𝐾00QK0=0 since BK0=0subscript𝐵superscript𝐾00BK0=0 and SK0=1subscript𝑆superscript𝐾01SK0=1), so that the T3=+1/2subscript𝑇312T3=+1/2 partner would be a K+superscript𝐾K+. The antiparticle of K+superscript𝐾K+ would be a K−superscript𝐾K-, with opposite strangeness charge S=−1𝑆1S, so it cannot belong to the same isospin multiplet as the latter (contrary to charge, strangeness is independent from isospin, and particles in the same isospin multiplet have the same strangeness). It follows that there must be another isospin 1/2121/2 multiplet where we fit the K+superscript𝐾K+ and another neutral kaon which we call K0¯¯superscript𝐾0overlineK0, which is the antiparticle of K0superscript𝐾0K0 and must therefore have the same mass. This particle was predicted this way, and later indeed observedcitebib.bib25, confirming the success of this scheme based on strangeness. By the late 1960s a number of additional heavy strange particles were discovered, such as ΣΣSigma and ΞΞXi baryons, as well as the (non-strange) ΔΔDelta’s already mentioned above. Due to this proliferation of particles, a classification based solely on SU representations became increasingly more cumbersome, since additional representations (often with the same isospin as others, yet fitting rather different particles) had to be introduced ad hoc to make the scheme work. This proliferation of particles and the difficulty in classifying them neatly under SU representations is colloquially known as the particle zoo.
content_sections.IV Extending the symmetry IV.2 Sakata mo: A major step towards a more elegant and more predictive classification of hadrons was made by japanese physicist Shoichi Sakata in 1956. Sakata proposed that the Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0 would be yet another state of a nucleon, which could then be found in three different states, p+=,n0=,Λ0=,formulae-sequencesuperscript𝑝matrix100formulae-sequencesuperscript𝑛0matrix010superscriptΛ0matrix001displaystyle p+=beginpmatrix1 0 0endpmatrix,quad n0=beginpmatrix0 1 0endpmatrix,quadLambda0=beginpmatrix0 0 1endpmatrix, or rather in a general superposition of these states. Sakata’s statement is that the nuclear interaction has an approximate symmetry under a redefinition of these states. So, similarly to what we discussed in section, one could do a redefinition N=(ψpψnψΛ)→N′=U​N,𝑁matrixsubscript𝜓𝑝subscript𝜓𝑛subscript𝜓Λ→superscript𝑁′𝑈𝑁N=beginpmatrixpsip psin psiLambdaendpmatrixto Nprime=UN, with U𝑈U a 3×3333times 3 unitary matrix with unit determinant. The group of such matrices is called SU. Notice that mΛ0=1115.683±0.006​MeV,subscript𝑚superscriptΛ0plus-or-minus1115.6830.006MeVmLambda0=1115.683pm 0.006textMeV, so that the relative mass difference between the Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0 and the average mass of proton and neutron is Δ​mΛ0,p​nmΛ0,p​navg≃17.2%. similar-to-or-equalsΔsubscript𝑚superscriptΛ0𝑝𝑛superscriptsubscript𝑚superscriptΛ0𝑝𝑛avgpercent17.2fracDelta mLambda0,pnmLambda0,pntextavgsimeq 17.2%. This already illustrates that this new symmetry is only approximately valid, but this shall not prevent us from proceeding. After all, SU isospin was also only approximate but still gave us many important and valid predictions.
content_sections.IV Extending the symmetry IV.3 Represent: Our task is again to build representations of this SU group, and then argue that each representation corresponds to a certain particle type. The method is the same as above: we build representations by performing infinitesimal SU transformations on a certain reference state. Following the same arguments as in section we find that the generators of infinitesimal SU transformations must be hermitean traceless matrices. We already know one representation of this group: the fundamental representation, a 3-dimensional space with basis given by eq. . In this representation, the infinitesimal transformations are given by 3×3333times 3 complex traceless hermitian matrices. How many generators are there? As a real vector space, the space of all complex 3×3333times 3 matrices has dimension24There are 32superscript3232 entries in the matrix, and each is described by two real numbers, namely the real and the imaginary parts of each complex entry. 2×322superscript322times 32. Making it hermitian decreases the dimension in half25The 333 diagonal elements must be real. Once we fix the 2×3232times 3 elements in the upper-triangular matrix (above the diagonal), those below the diagonal are also fixed by the hermiticity condition. So we only need 3+2×3=932393+2times 3=9 real numbers to describe the whole matrix. , leaving 32superscript3232. Finally the traceless condition lowers the dimension once more by 1, leaving us with a dimension26In general, the Lie algebra of 𝔰​𝔲​(n)𝔰𝔲𝑛mathfraksu(n) (i. e. the space of n×n𝑛𝑛ntimes n hermitean traceless matrices) has dimension n2−1superscript𝑛21n2-1. Applying this to 𝔰​𝔲​𝔰𝔲2mathfraksu one finds a dimension 22−1=3superscript221322-1=3, as we saw in section. of 32−1superscript32132-1. This means SU has 8 linearly independent generators. In SU, the usual generators are given by the Pauli Matrices. We also have, for 3 dimensions, an usual choice of generators, called the Gell-Mann matrices, given by λ1=,λ2=(0−i0i00000),λ3=(1000−10000),λ4=,λ5=(00−i000i00),λ6=,λ7=(00000−i0i0),λ8=13​(10001000−2). beginsplitlambda1=beginpmatrix010 100 000endpmatrix,quadlambda2=beginpmatrix0-i0 i00 000endpmatrix,quadlambda3=beginpmatrix100 0-10 000endpmatrix, lambda4=beginpmatrix001 000 100endpmatrix,quadlambda5=beginpmatrix00-i 000 i00endpmatrix, lambda6=beginpmatrix000 001 010endpmatrix,quadlambda7=beginpmatrix000 00-i 0i0endpmatrix,quadlambda8=dfrac1sqrt3beginpmatrix100 010 00-2endpmatrix. endsplit Notice that λ1,2,3subscript𝜆123lambda1,2,3 are simply the Pauli matrices inserted into a larger 3×3333times 3 paradigm. This means that the algebra 𝔰​𝔲​𝔰𝔲2mathfraksu is contained in 𝔰​𝔲​𝔰𝔲3mathfraksu. Consequently, representations of SU contain representations of SU. In other words, the classification scheme we will construct here will extend what we discussed above: all the successes of classification by SU isospin will be maintained. Actually, a glimpse at λ4subscript𝜆4lambda4 and λ5subscript𝜆5lambda5 shows that, if we remove the second row and column, we are left with two of the Pauli matrices. Similarly, from its other commutation relation, it is an eigenvector of Y𝑌Y with eigenvalue (y+1)𝑦1(y+1). Therefore all ladder operators map eigenvectors of (T3,Y)subscript𝑇3𝑌(T3,Y) to other such eigenvectors (unless they map |t3,y⟩ketsubscript𝑡3𝑦kett3,y to the zero vector). But to act on a state with these ladders is equivalent to performing arbitrary SU transformations on this state. Thus we will build irreducible representations of SU by repeatedly applying these operators on a reference state of the representation, resulting in a number of linearly independent states, until this iterative procedure exhausts itself and no new independent vectors are produced. As long as we are dealing with finite dimensional representations, this iteration must always cease to produce new independent vectors at some point. When this happens, we have found our representation: all states are related to each other by SU transformations. In the case of SU, only one generator T3subscript𝑇3T3 could be simultaneously diagonalized28The operator T2superscript𝑇2T2 is not one of the generators, although it is built from them. It is called the Casimir operator of the 𝔰​𝔲​𝔰𝔲2mathfraksu algebra. , whose eigenvalues m𝑚m label the states of the representation, and the other generators formed ladder operators, which move the states along both directions of this straight line (increasing and decreasing m𝑚m). Thus SU representations could be visualized as a series of points on a straight line, corresponding to the allowed values of m=−I,…,I𝑚𝐼…𝐼m=-I,ldots,I. In the case of SU each state in a representation space is labeled by two parameters, and we can illustrate this representation as points in a (t3,y)subscript𝑡3𝑦(t3,y) plane. Each point is called a “weight” and the collection of points belonging to a representation is called a “weight diagram”. Figure shows how each point (i. e. each weight) is mapped onto another by the ladder operators defined above.Let us consider first the fundamental or defining representation of SU, which is the representation where the transformations act as 3×3333times 3 unitary matrices (and infinitesimal SU transformations are represented by the Gell-Mann matrices themselves). Going along with the Sakata Model of SU, we can label the three vectors belonging to this representation space as p=(1,0,0),n=(0,1,0),Λ=(0,0,1)formulae-sequence𝑝100formulae-sequence𝑛010Λ001p=(1,0,0),n=(0,1,0),Lambda=(0,0,1). Since it is three dimensional, we usually refer to it as the 3 representation.It turns out that, in SU, there is another 3-dimensional representation which is inequivalent to the fundamental 𝟑33. Note that, if U𝑈U is an SU matrix, then so is its complex conjugate U∗superscript𝑈U*. The U∗superscript𝑈U*’s defines the complex-conjugate representation of the U𝑈U’s. Moreover, for an infinitesimal SU transformation29Recall that the parameters of our transformation (i. e. the “angles” by which we perform the “rotation”/transformation, dubbed αasuperscript𝛼𝑎alphaa below, are always real numbers. U=𝟙+i​αa​Ta⟹U∗=𝟙+i​αa​(−Ta∗). 𝑈1𝑖superscript𝛼𝑎subscript𝑇𝑎superscript𝑈1𝑖superscript𝛼𝑎superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑎U=mathds1+ialphaaTaimplies U*=mathds1+ialphaa(-Ta*). Thus we see that, if the Gell-Mann matrices λasubscript𝜆𝑎lambdaa represent infinitesimal transformations acting on the fundamental representation p,n,Λ𝑝𝑛Λp,n,Lambda, then the matrices −λa∗superscriptsubscript𝜆𝑎-lambdaa* represent transformations on the antifundamental (i. e. the complex conjugate of the fundamental). The reader may now pause and wonder: why was this not mentioned in the case of SU above? Where is the “antifundamental” of SU? It turns out that, for SU, any representation is equivalent to its conjugate! This is not hard to see: the generators of SU are such that −T1∗=−T1,−T2∗=T2and−T3∗=−T3. formulae-sequencesuperscriptsubscript𝑇1subscript𝑇1formulae-sequencesuperscriptsubscript𝑇2subscript𝑇2andsuperscriptsubscript𝑇3subscript𝑇3-T1*=-T1,-T2*=T2quadtextandquad-T3*=-T3. Graphically, all states of an SU representation lie along a line symmetric around the origin. Thus the above transformations (going from the fundamental to the antifundamental) correspond to a mere renaming of the states of the I𝐼I representation, which keeps the vector space itself unaltered. But for SU these two representations define different spaces. If we define a basis p∗=(1,0,0)superscript𝑝100p*=(1,0,0), n∗=(0,1,0)superscript𝑛010n*=(0,1,0) and Λ∗=(0,0,1)superscriptΛ001Lambda*=(0,0,1) in the complex conjugate space of the fundamental representation, then the infinitesimal transformations act on these as Taantifund. ≡−Ta∗superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑎antifund. superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑎Tatextantifund. equiv-Ta*. The weight diagram in the (t3,y)subscript𝑡3𝑦(t3,y) plane is depicted in figure. The antifundamental representation is clearly also 3-dimensional, but different from the fundamental: thus we denote it by 𝟑¯bold-¯3boldsymboloverline3.We have mentioned before that every Lie algebra has an adjoint representation, where the operators are seen at the same time as transformations acting on states, and as the very states being acted upon. This action is defined via the commutation relations, as in eq. . This means that the adjoint representation of SU is an 8-dimensional vector space with basis |T3⟩,|Y⟩,|T±⟩,|U±⟩,|V±⟩ketsubscript𝑇3ket𝑌ketsubscript𝑇plus-or-minusketsubscript𝑈plus-or-minusketsubscript𝑉plus-or-minusketT3,ketY,ketTpm,ketUpm,ketVpm. These are all eigenvalues of T3subscript𝑇3T3 and Y𝑌Y, as one can see for instance from T3​|T±⟩subscript𝑇3ketsubscript𝑇plus-or-minusdisplaystyleT3ketTpm =[T3,T±]=±|T±⟩,absentsubscript𝑇3subscript𝑇plus-or-minusplus-or-minusketsubscript𝑇plus-or-minusdisplaystyle=[T3,Tpm]=pmketTpm, Y​|T±⟩𝑌ketsubscript𝑇plus-or-minusdisplaystyleYketTpm =[Y,T±]=0​|T±⟩,absent𝑌subscript𝑇plus-or-minus0ketsubscript𝑇plus-or-minusdisplaystyle=[Y,Tpm]=0ketTpm, or (T3,Y)​|T±⟩=(±1,0)​|T±⟩. subscript𝑇3𝑌ketsubscript𝑇plus-or-minusplus-or-minus10ketsubscript𝑇plus-or-minus(T3,Y)ketTpm=left(pm 1,0right)ketTpm. Likewise the commutation relations of these ladder operators with T3subscript𝑇3T3 and Y𝑌Y imply that (T3,Y)​|U±⟩subscript𝑇3𝑌ketsubscript𝑈plus-or-minusdisplaystyle(T3,Y)ketUpm =(∓12,±1)​|U±⟩absentminus-or-plus12plus-or-minus1ketsubscript𝑈plus-or-minusdisplaystyle=left(mpfrac12,pm 1right)ketUpm (T3,Y)​|V±⟩subscript𝑇3𝑌ketsubscript𝑉plus-or-minusdisplaystyle(T3,Y)ketVpm =(±12,±1)​|V±⟩absentplus-or-minus12plus-or-minus1ketsubscript𝑉plus-or-minusdisplaystyle=left(pmfrac12,pm 1right)ketVpm (T3,Y)​|T3⟩subscript𝑇3𝑌ketsubscript𝑇3displaystyle(T3,Y)ketT3 =(0,0)​|T3⟩absent00ketsubscript𝑇3displaystyle=left(0,0right)ketT3 (T3,Y)​|Y⟩subscript𝑇3𝑌ket𝑌displaystyle(T3,Y)ketY =(0,0)​|Y⟩. absent00ket𝑌displaystyle=left(0,0right)ketY. We can then put these states in a weight diagram (i. e. in a (t3,y)subscript𝑡3𝑦(t3,y) plane), which results in figure. Note how this parallels figure above. This is of course no accident, since the states in the adjoint representation are the ladder (and diagonal T3subscript𝑇3T3 and Y𝑌Y) operators themselves. One says that the roots are the weights of the adjoint representation. Notice also that both |T3⟩ketsubscript𝑇3ketT3 and |Y⟩ket𝑌ketY have the same eigenvalues (t3=0,y=0)formulae-sequencesubscript𝑡30𝑦0(t3=0,y=0). This does not mean that they are the same vector. Rather, it means that certain weights in an SU representation may have multiplicity larger than one. In other words, there may be more than one state sitting on the same point of the weight diagram31One may wonder why this happens in SU and not in SU (since in that case two vectors with the same labels j𝑗j and m𝑚m are necessarily linearly dependent). The reason for this difference stems from the fact that, in SU, there is essentially only one way to get from a state to another using the ladder operators: you can only ”climb up or down” using J±subscript𝐽plus-or-minusJpm. In SU, on the other hand, you can reach one point from another by different paths in weight space. In other words, we can use several different combinations of T±,U±subscript𝑇plus-or-minussubscript𝑈plus-or-minusTpm,Upm and V±subscript𝑉plus-or-minusVpm to reach one state from another, and these possibilities don’t always commute, and will in general result in linearly independent states. Indeed, notice for instance that there are at least three possibilities to go from a state (t3,y)subscript𝑡3𝑦(t3,y) to (t3−1,y)subscript𝑡31𝑦(t3-1,y), namely by applying T−subscript𝑇T-, U+​V−subscript𝑈subscript𝑉U+V- or V−​U+subscript𝑉subscript𝑈V-U+. From the commutation relations in eq. one sees that [U+,V−]=T−⟹U+​V−=T−+V−​U+,subscript𝑈subscript𝑉subscript𝑇subscript𝑈subscript𝑉subscript𝑇subscript𝑉subscript𝑈[U+,V-]=T-implies U+V-=T-+V-U+, so U+​V−​|t3,y⟩subscript𝑈subscript𝑉ketsubscript𝑡3𝑦U+V-kett3,y, T−​|t3,y⟩subscript𝑇ketsubscript𝑡3𝑦T-kett3,y and V−​U+​|t3,y⟩subscript𝑉subscript𝑈ketsubscript𝑡3𝑦V-U+kett3,y are linearly dependent, but any two of these are in general independent. . In the case of the adjoint representation, the point (0,0)00(0,0) has multiplicity 2. We represent this multiplicity in the weight diagram of figure by circling the central dot at the origin. Because T−,T3subscript𝑇subscript𝑇3T-,T3 and T+subscript𝑇T+ form an 𝔰​𝔲​𝔰𝔲2mathfraksu subalgebra of 𝔰​𝔲​𝔰𝔲3mathfraksu, the points along horizontal lines in SU weight diagrams form SU representations. Looking at figure we infer that: • |U+⟩,|V+⟩ketsubscript𝑈ketsubscript𝑉ketU+,ketV+ form an SU doublet, • |T−⟩,|T3⟩,|T+⟩ketsubscript𝑇ketsubscript𝑇3ketsubscript𝑇ketT-,ketT3,ketT+ form an SU triplet, • |U+⟩,|V+⟩ketsubscript𝑈ketsubscript𝑉ketU+,ketV+ form an SU doublet, • |Y⟩ket𝑌ketY forms an SU singlet. We know that the pions fit into an SU triplet, so we could naturally try to accommodate them as the |T−⟩,|T3⟩,|T+⟩ketsubscript𝑇ketsubscript𝑇3ketsubscript𝑇ketT-,ketT3,ketT+ in the octet. Moreover, recalling the discussion at the end of section, we know that (K0,K+)superscript𝐾0superscript𝐾(K0,K+) and (K−,K0¯)superscript𝐾¯superscript𝐾0(K-,overlineK0) fit into SU doublets, so we can associate them to (|U+⟩,|V+⟩)ketsubscript𝑈ketsubscript𝑉(ketU+,ketV+) and (|V−⟩,|U−⟩)ketsubscript𝑉ketsubscript𝑈(ketV-,ketU-). There remains the isospin singlet, which should correspond to another meson. In 1958, theorists who worked on the Sakata model predicted the existence of this new particlecitebib.bib26, bib.bib27, a new meson later dubbed η𝜂eta, which was indeed found with the properties anticipated from symmetry argumentscitebib.bib28. In particular, note that its mass mη=547.862±0.017​MeVsubscript𝑚𝜂plus-or-minus547.8620.017MeVmeta=547.862pm 0.017textMeV is very much in line with those of the kaons, mK±=493.677±0.016​MeV,mK0=mK¯0=497.611±0.013​MeV,formulae-sequencesubscript𝑚superscript𝐾plus-or-minusplus-or-minus493.6770.016MeVsubscript𝑚superscript𝐾0subscript𝑚superscript¯𝐾0plus-or-minus497.6110.013MeVbeginsplitmKpm=493.677pm 0.016textMeV, mK0=moverlineK0=497.611pm 0.013textMeV,endsplit in agreement with expectations from symmetry arguments (concerning their belonging to the same SU multiplet). Figure illustrates this fitting of the mesons in the octet representation. Note that the electric charge of the particles do not change as we move along the direction of the U±subscript𝑈plus-or-minusUpm roots (or as we apply the U±subscript𝑈plus-or-minusUpm ladders). Instead, charge increases or decreases as we move in the orthogonal direction, which is also shown in the figure as the Q𝑄Q axis, corresponding to the operator32Note that this is perpendicular to the direction U3∼λ3+3​λ8similar-tosubscript𝑈3subscript𝜆33subscript𝜆8U3simlambda3+sqrt3lambda8, which is another Pauli matrix that can be built out of the Gell-Mann matrices. We chose to work with T3subscript𝑇3T3 and Y𝑌Y as the diagonal generators, but we could with equal right have chosen U3subscript𝑈3U3 and Q𝑄Q. This is yet another illustration of the fact that 𝔰​𝔲​𝔰𝔲3mathfraksu is formed of 3 overlapping 𝔰​𝔲​𝔰𝔲2mathfraksu’s. Note also that the states lying on top of the U3subscript𝑈3U3 direction belong to 𝔰​𝔲​𝔰𝔲2mathfraksu representations, which are symmetric around the origin. Thus the weight diagram must be symmetric against reflections around Q𝑄Q, as can be indeed seen from figure. Q≡T3+Y2. 𝑄subscript𝑇3𝑌2Qequiv T3+fracY2. Comparing with eq. we see that Y=B+S𝑌𝐵𝑆Y=B+S, and since for mesons B=0𝐵0B=0 we see that in this case the hypercharge Y𝑌Y is just a quantification of strangeness. This is again in line with our discussions of section, where we concluded that (K0,K+)superscript𝐾0superscript𝐾(K0,K+) doublet had strangeness S=+1𝑆1S=+1 while their antiparticles (K0¯,K−)¯superscript𝐾0superscript𝐾(overlineK0,K-) have S=−1𝑆1S.Finally, another important SU representation is the decuplet, thus named because it is a 10-dimensional vector space. Its weight diagram is shown in figure. It can be constructed by starting at a state with largest value of t3subscript𝑡3t3 (this state must exist, otherwise the representation space would be infinite dimensional) and applying V−subscript𝑉V- transformations 3 times consecutively. By the fourth application of V−subscript𝑉V- the result is the null vector. This is akin to the case of SU, when a state with m=mmin𝑚subscript𝑚minm=mtextmin had to be annihilated by the lowering operator for consistency. Moreover, the decuplet is such that, after applying three times V−subscript𝑉V-, one cannot do other operations except go back up via V+subscript𝑉V+ or via U+subscript𝑈U+. It can be shown that U+subscript𝑈U+ can be applied only 3 times before annihilating the state, and then one can only apply T+subscript𝑇T+ to close the triangle, as shown in figurecitebib.bib4. The decuplet is important in particle physics because, up until 1963, we had detected nine spin 3/2323/2 baryons, namely: the four ΔΔDelta’s that we met in section, a triplet of baryons called Σ∗superscriptΣSigma*, and a doublet of Ξ∗superscriptΞXi*citebib.bib29. Their properties fit nicely into the decuplet representation. From eq. we see, for example, that the state at the top-right corner of the triangle has Y=1𝑌1Y=1 and T3=3/2subscript𝑇332T3=3/2, resulting in Q=2𝑄2Q=2. We did know a particle with this charge: the Δ++superscriptΔabsentDelta++ baryon, with isospin 3/2323/2 under SU. As discussed in section we expect four such baryons, which were indeed found and fit nicely into the top-most row of the triangle in figure. As we move to the left in the diagram along a horizontal line, the charge Q𝑄Q decreases by one unit. Likewise, descending in the V−subscript𝑉V- direction decreases Y𝑌Y by 111 and T3subscript𝑇3T3 by 1/2121/2, therefore decreasing Q𝑄Q by unit as well. So the Σ∗superscriptΣSigma*’s fit naturally in the row below the ΔΔDelta’s, and the Ξ∗superscriptΞXi*’s fit in the row below the Σ∗superscriptΣSigma*’s. Moreover, recalling that Y=B+S𝑌𝐵𝑆Y=B+S and that these particles are baryons, one has S=Y−1𝑆𝑌1S=Y-1 in this case. This prediction is also in agreement with the observations that SΔ=0subscript𝑆Δ0SDelta=0, SΣ∗=−1subscript𝑆superscriptΣ1SSigma* and SΞ∗=−2subscript𝑆superscriptΞ2SXi*=-2. However, symmetry arguments tell us that we should have 10 of these particles, and not just nine. This led many theorists in 1962 to conjecture that a tenth particle should existcitebib.bib30, bib.bib31, dubbed the ΩΩOmega baryon (since ΩΩOmega is the last letter of the greek alphabet, and this would be the last missing member of the decuplet). Moreover, the properties of this particle can be predicted from symmetry arguments: it should have spin 3/2323/2, charge −11-1 and strangeness −33-3. Not only that, the mass splitting between the consecutive rows of the diagram is approximately constant, since mΔsubscript𝑚Δdisplaystyle mDelta ≃1232​MeV,similar-to-or-equalsabsent1232MeVdisplaystylesimeq 1232textMeV, mΣ∗subscript𝑚superscriptΣdisplaystyle mSigma* ≃1385​MeV,similar-to-or-equalsabsent1385MeVdisplaystylesimeq 1385textMeV, mΞ∗subscript𝑚superscriptΞdisplaystyle mXi* ≃1532​MeV,similar-to-or-equalsabsent1532MeVdisplaystylesimeq 1532textMeV, allowing us to also predict mΩ−pred. ≃1680​MeV. similar-to-or-equalssuperscriptsubscript𝑚superscriptΩpred.1680MeVmOmega-textpred. simeq 1680textMeV. In February 1964 the discovery of a particle with exactly these properties was announcedcitebib.bib29, with mass mΩ−exp. =1672.45superscriptsubscript𝑚superscriptΩexp.1672.45mOmega-textexp. =1672.45MeV (within ∼0.5%similar-toabsentpercent0.5sim 0.5% of the predicted mass!). To this day the prediction and subsequent detection of the Ω−superscriptΩOmega- baryon is one of the greatest success stories in particle physics.
content_sections.IV Extending the symmetry IV.4 Tensor pr: We can also consider the analogous problem to what we studied in section: what happens when one deals with a composite system of two (or more) particles belonging to SU representations? The resulting composite system must also belong to SU representations. But which ones? Mathematically, this amounts to determining how tensor products of SU representations decompose as direct sums of SU representations. For this purpose, note that the developments that led to eqs. and are valid for any group. So also in SU the eigenvalues t3subscript𝑡3t3 and y𝑦y of a composite system |t3,y⟩⊗|t3,y⟩tensor-productketsuperscriptsubscript𝑡31superscript𝑦1ketsuperscriptsubscript𝑡32superscript𝑦2kett3,yotimeskett3,y are t3+t3superscriptsubscript𝑡31superscriptsubscript𝑡32t3+t3 and y+ysuperscript𝑦1superscript𝑦2y+y. In the (t3,y)subscript𝑡3𝑦(t3,y) weight diagram, this means that when we take the tensor product of two representations, the resulting figure is achieved by superimposing copies of one of these representations over each point belonging to the other representation (i. e. taking the weights of one representation as the origin of the other’s weight diagram). Figure illustrates this for the tensor product of the fundamental and the antifundamental representations. We see that the outer points form a hexagon, and there are three weights at the origin. The octuplet is an hexagon with multiplicity two at the origin, so we see that the 9-dimensional space obtained from this tensor product naturally decomposes as 𝟑⊗𝟑¯=𝟖⊕𝟏. tensor-product3¯3direct-sum813otimesoverline3=8oplus1. With the same construction we can perform the product of 𝟑⊗𝟑tensor-product333otimes3 and identify a 𝟑¯¯3overline3 as well as a triangle corresponding to a 6-dimensional representation which we call the 𝟔66. Likewise we can see that 𝟑⊗𝟔tensor-product363otimes6 results in an octet and a decupletcitebib.bib7. So we can write 𝟑⊗𝟑⊗𝟑=(𝟔⊕𝟑¯)⊗𝟑=(𝟔⊗𝟑)⊕(𝟑¯⊗𝟑)=𝟏𝟎⊕𝟖⊕𝟖⊕𝟏. tensor-product333tensor-productdirect-sum6¯33direct-sumtensor-product63tensor-product¯33direct-sum10881beginsplit3otimes 3otimes 3=(6oplusoverline3)otimes3 =(6otimes3)oplus(overline3otimes 3) =10oplus 8oplus 8oplus 1. endsplit
content_sections.IV Extending the symmetry IV.5 Sakatons: These results indicate that all SU representations can be recovered from tensor products of the fundamental and antifundamental. It is precisely because they act as building blocks of all representations that they are called “fundamental”. From figure we see that, in Sakata’s model, the octet + singlet could then be seen as composite states of protons/neutrons/ΛΛLambda’s with their antiparticles p¯¯𝑝overlinep/n¯¯𝑛overlinen/Λ¯¯ΛoverlineLambda. These fundamental constituents of the Sakata model are often denoted as sakatons. This perspective fits nicely with the meson octet in figure, since all quantum numbers would match if we identify π+=n¯​psuperscript𝜋¯𝑛𝑝pi+=overlinenp, K+=p​Λ¯superscript𝐾𝑝¯ΛK+=poverlineLambda, K0=n​Λ¯superscript𝐾0𝑛¯ΛK0=noverlineLambda, π0=(n¯​n+p¯​p)/2superscript𝜋0¯𝑛𝑛¯𝑝𝑝2pi0=(overlinenn+overlinepp)/sqrt2 (the m=0𝑚0m=0 state of the SU triplet), and η=(n¯​n−p¯​p−2​Λ¯​Λ)/6𝜂¯𝑛𝑛¯𝑝𝑝2¯ΛΛ6eta=(overlinenn-overlinepp-2overlineLambdaLambda)/sqrt6. The remaining mesons of the octed are antiparticles of these. Note, for instance, how in this scheme the S=+1𝑆1S=+1 kaons K0superscript𝐾0K0 and K+superscript𝐾K+ would be composed of a Λ¯¯ΛoverlineLambda, which is the antiparticle of an S=−1𝑆1S particle. One sees that the mesons fit in octets which we can interpret as stemming from 𝟑⊗𝟑¯tensor-product3¯33otimesoverline3. This product also contain a singlet (now called η′superscript𝜂′etaprime), predicted since 1957citebib.bib26, bib.bib32 and observed in 1964citebib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35 – yet another success for this mesonic classification scheme. However, this model is a lot less successful for a classification of baryons. When Sakata proposed this model in 1956, eight spin-1/2 baryons were known: p𝑝p, n𝑛n, ΛΛLambda, 3 ΣΣSigma’s and 2 ΞΞXi’s33Note that these are different particles from the spin-3/2 Σ∗superscriptΣSigma*’s and Ξ∗superscriptΞXi*’s appearing in the decuplet. The similar names come from similarities they share, e. g. the fact that both ΣΣSigma and Σ∗superscriptΣSigma* exist as isospin triplets under SU, i. e. there are three isospin partners (Σ−,Σ0,Σ+)Sigma-,Sigma0,Sigma+) and also three isospin partners (Σ∗−,Σ∗0,Σ∗+)Sigma*-,Sigma*0,Sigma*+). Similarly, both ΞΞXi and Ξ∗superscriptΞXi* form SU doublets. citebib.bib36, bib.bib37. Since the ΣΣSigma’s have strangeness SΣ=−1subscript𝑆Σ1SSigma, they would have to contain one ΛΛLambda. Clearly a Σ−superscriptΣSigma- would have to also include an antiproton p¯¯𝑝overlinep (since this is the only sakaton with negative charge), so we would need at least a 𝟑⊗𝟑¯=𝟖⊕𝟏tensor-product3¯3direct-sum813otimesoverline3=8oplus1. But there is no way to fit the ΣΣSigma triplet in the octet: the only t3subscript𝑡3t3-triplet in this representation lies on the t3subscript𝑡3t3 axis, and not all states in this axis would contain a ΛΛLambda (meaning not all states would have the correct strangeness for the ΣΣSigma’s), see fig. . We could then try to accommodate the ΣΣSigma’s and ΞΞXi’s using larger representations, for instance 𝟑⊗𝟑⊗𝟑¯tensor-product33¯33otimes 3otimesoverline3, which is 3×3×3=27333273times 3times 3=27-dimensional. However, the other 22 baryonic partners of the ΣΣSigma’s and ΞΞXi’s, which would be expected to belong to this large representation space, were never found. It was Gell-Mann citebib.bib38 and Ne’emann citebib.bib39, working independently, who first proposed a different baryonic classification scheme. Instead of taking p,n𝑝𝑛p,n and ΛΛLambda as belonging to the fundamental, they instead proposed that these should be classified together with the ΣΣSigma’s and ΞΞXi’s in the 𝟖88 representation. This is the famous “Eightfold Way” of particle physics, depicted in fig. . In this scenario, the spin-1/2 baryons fit in an octet while the spin-3/2 ones fit the decuplet. Looking at the developments of the last section, we can identify this 𝟏𝟎⊕𝟖direct-sum10810oplus 8 as a result34The reader may now look at the expression written at the end of section, namely 𝟑⊗𝟑⊗𝟑=𝟏𝟎⊕𝟖⊕𝟖⊕𝟏tensor-product333direct-sum108813otimes 3otimes 3=10oplus 8oplus 8oplus 1, and conclude that we should find one baryonic decuplet, two baryonic octets and one baryonic singlet. But only one decuplet and one octet are actually found! A detailed explanation of why the 𝟖⊕𝟏direct-sum818oplus 1 baryons do not exist is beyond the scope of this paper. It is related to the confinement mechanism of quarks. It turns out that quarks carry not only flavour (i. e. their characteristic of being an u𝑢u, d𝑑d or s𝑠s) but also spin 1/2 and a colour charge. Quark confinement means that only fully anti-symmetrized wavefunctions in the colour degree of freedom are allowed. By Pauli’s exclusion principle this means that the wavefunction must be fully symmetric in flavour and spin. It turns out that only the 𝟏𝟎⊕𝟖direct-sum10810oplus 8 satisfy these requirements, and are therefore the only SU baryonic representations that can be found in Naturecitebib.bib1. of the product 𝟑⊗𝟑⊗𝟑tensor-product3333otimes 3otimes 3. The remaining question is: what is the fundamental representation in this case? Which are the fundamental constituent particles of mesons and baryons? Gell-Mann dubbed these particles “quarks”citebib.bib4035George Zweig also had a similar idea, and dubbed these fundamental constituents “aces”citebib.bib41. Clearly it was Gell-Mann’s nomenclature that stuck. Incidentally, Gell-Mann took the idea of the name from a passage of James Joyce’s Finnegans Wake: “Three quarks for Muster Mark!”, inspired by the 𝟑⊗𝟑⊗𝟑tensor-product3333otimes 3otimes 3 construction of baryons. . The three states of the fundamental representation are called up, down and strange quarks, or, in terms of t3subscript𝑡3t3 and y𝑦y eigenvalues, u=|12,13⟩,d=|−12,13⟩,s=|0,−23⟩. formulae-sequence𝑢ket1213formulae-sequence𝑑ket1213𝑠ket023u=left|frac12,frac13right,quad d=left|-frac12,frac13right,quad s=left|0,-frac23right. A few important conclusions follow straightforwardly, namely: • applying eq. to the fundamental representation one sees that these quarks have charges Qu=2/3subscript𝑄𝑢23Qu=2/3, Qd=−1/3subscript𝑄𝑑13Qd/3 and Qs=−1/3subscript𝑄𝑠13Qs/3. Their charges are thus fractions of the elementary charge. • Since baryons are constituted of 3 quarks, these constituents must have fractional baryonic number B=1/3𝐵13B=1/3. Mesons are formed of quarks and anti-quarks and thus have B=1/3+(−1/3)=0𝐵13130B=1/3+(-1/3)=0. • Looking at the meson octet in fig. and how it stems from the product 𝟑⊗𝟑¯tensor-product3¯33otimesoverline3 as in fig. , one concludes that the strangeness charge of the mesons is nothing but a count of the number of strange quarks (or rather strange antiquarks, since s𝑠s has strangeness −11-1) that constitute them. Likewise for the baryons in the octet and in the decuplet. • The fact that strangeness is only approximately conserved can now be interpreted in the following manner: the strong interaction is not able to change the nature of a quark, but the weak interaction is. So, in a strangeness-violating decay such as Λ0→p+π−→superscriptΛ0𝑝superscript𝜋Lambda0to p+pi-, what happens is that the strange quark in Λ0=u​d​ssuperscriptΛ0𝑢𝑑𝑠Lambda0=uds is decaying as s→u+→𝑠limit-from𝑢displaystyle sto u+ W−||superscript𝑊displaystyleunderset|W- →d+u¯→absent𝑑¯𝑢displaystyle,rightarrow d+overlineu with W−superscript𝑊W- a mediator of the weak interaction. Then Λ0=u​d​s→u​u​d+d​u¯=p+π−. superscriptΛ0𝑢𝑑𝑠→𝑢𝑢𝑑𝑑¯𝑢𝑝superscript𝜋Lambda0=udstouud+doverlineu=p+pi-. Despite these predictive successes, for some time there was some skepticism as to whether quarks indeed exist, or whether they would just be mathematical artifacts of this symmetry constructioncitebib.bib4. For one, it is relatively easy to detect charged particles, but despite decades of successful experiments in particle physics no particles with fractional charges had ever been seen. It was then clear that quarks, if they existed, could never be seen in isolation, but only confined within hadrons – and this confinement mechanism was largely unknown at the time (and still is to a great extent!). By 1968, high-energy collisions of electrons and nuclei were finally able to probe the inner structure of hadrons, showing that they indeed behave as if constituted of point-like substructures. Throughout the early 1970s a series of other theoretical and experimental discoveries helped explain some aspects of quark confinement, and strengthened the conviction of the community on the existence of these fundamental hadronic constituentscitebib.bib42. Overall, this ultimate triumph of the quark model was another chapter in the long history of theoretical prediction of new particles followed by subsequent experimental confirmation.
content_sections.V Conclusions: Symmetry arguments and related techniques from group representation theory have always played a pivotal role in the development of particle physics. These techniques appear in the study of the Lorentz group, in classification schemes proposed since the earliest days of particle physics, and in the gauge principle that now lies at the heart of the Standard Model of elementary particles and their fundamental interactions. It is therefore essential that any beginner in this field be familiar with this language. In this paper we paved a pedagogic path that serves as an introduction to group representation theory for beginner particle physicists. Our approach focuses in the history of hadronic classification schemes that culminated in the quark model in the mid- to late 1960s. We argued that this is the most natural starting point to introduce group theory to this audience, since it allows one to start from the most basic SU group and progress naturally to the more challenging SU structure. Moreover, this allows students to get acquainted with some aspects of the early history of this field and some of its most successful predictions. Through the study of symmetries and groups we can tell the story of many particles that were predicted and later detected, a constant pattern that is still ongoing in the field of particle physics. This approach is also particularly interesting because it allows beginners to have a first contact with the history of the quark model. Most students are already familiar with the fact that protons and neutrons are composed of quarks. But as scientists it is important to not only know something as a fact, but also to know the details of how and why we came to know that as a fact. And for the quark model this inevitably means discussing SU representations and/or deep inelastic scattering experiments. In this sense, this work differs from most of the current literature that choose to present the SU paradigm already from an ad hoc postulate of the existence of 3 quark flavours. Here we instead culminate in the prediction of quarks as belonging to the fundamental representation of a (previously recognized) SU symmetry group. In this pedagogic path, quarks are yet another paradigmatic example of particles predicted from symmetry arguments and later confirmed by experiments. Finally, an important novelty in this presentation is a middle path we have found between a full presentation of mathematical tools and an emphasis on physical applications. The reader will find here an explanation of the concept of group representation, including the fundamental and the adjoint, as well as the main techniques underlying the highest weight construction which can be used to build general representations of Lie algebras. This allows for a reasonable understanding of the ideas being discussed, and provides the reader with tools to later explore more technical texts. On the other hand, we purposefully avoid any general results, sticking to the groups we are actually interested in. Abstract mathematical definitions of groups and representations are reserved for the appendix. Theorems on the geometry of SU weight diagrams are not stated nor proven. We have focused only on the results that are necessary for the reader to understand the “Eightfold Way” and the quark model. After studying the subject as presented here, the reader will hopefully be more prepared to tackle more thorough textbooks on group theory, with the benefit of knowing where some of these arid concepts will appear and how they can be applied. Should this text help in providing students with the toolkit to face this journey more lightly, it will have fulfilled its purpose.
content_sections.Acknowledgements: G. F. V would like to thank FAPEMIG and Universidade Federal de Minas Gerais (UFMG) for financial support during the preparation of this work.

sorted_papers.title: Production of pentaquarks with hidden charm and double strangeness in $\Xi_b$ and $\Omega_b$ decays
sorted_papers.abstract: Recently, several pentaquark states $P_{css}$, with global flavor $\bar c c s s n$, have been predicted within a theoretical framework based on unitary coupled channels. We study theoretically the feasibility to observe the $P_{css}$ with $I(J^P)=\frac{1}{2}(\frac{1}{2}^-)$ in the decays $\Xi_b^0\to \eta \eta_c \Xi^0$ and $\Omega_b^-\to K^- \eta_c \Xi^0$. Indeed, within the model, the $\eta_c \Xi^0$ channel is the lowest mass pseudoscalar-baryon channel to which this pentaquark state couples, thus we can expect to observe its signal in the $\eta_c \Xi^0$ invariant mass distribution of the mentioned decays. We identify the dominant weak decay processes and then implement the hadronization into the different meson-baryon channels in the final state, linked by flavor symmetry. The dominant meson-baryon final state interaction is then implemented to generate the full amplitude, implicitly accounting for the dynamical emergence of the pentaquark states. We obtain a clear Breit-Wigner-like resonant signal in the spectrum of the $\Omega_b^-$ decay, exceeding that in the $\Xi_b^0$ decay by two to three orders of magnitude. In the case of the latter decay, the resonant state would manifest as a significant dip in the spectrum. We study the feasibility of searching for these $b$-hadron decay modes and analysing their resonant components using the current and future data samples from the LHCb experiment.

sections.20: E Close An introduction to quarks and partons
sections.24: E Close An introduction to quarks and partons

qualitative: SU(3) flavor symmetry is used to relate different meson-baryon channels in the hadronization process of b-hadron decays, enabling the description of final states with different quark content through symmetry-based mappings.

U-spin symmetry, a subgroup of SU(3) flavor symmetry, is employed to connect decays involving strange and non-strange quarks, particularly in relating the Ξ_b^0 and Ω_b^- decays to the same pentaquark state with hidden charm and double strangeness.

The dominant meson-baryon final state interactions are implemented using unitary coupled channels, where SU(3) and U-spin symmetries guide the structure of the interaction potentials and the coupling between channels.

The η_c Ξ^0 channel is identified as the lowest-mass pseudoscalar-baryon channel to which the P_css pentaquark state with I(J^P) = 1/2(1/2^-) couples, making it the preferred channel for resonance detection in the decays studied.

The theoretical framework relies on the dynamical generation of pentaquark states via nonlinear dynamics in unitarized meson-baryon scattering, with SU(3) flavor symmetry ensuring consistent coupling patterns across different decay modes.
quantitative: The predicted mass of the P_css pentaquark state with I(J^P) = 1/2(1/2^-) is approximately 4500 MeV.
The predicted width of the P_css state is of the order of 10 MeV.
The resonant signal in the Ω_b^- → K^- η_c Ξ^0 decay is two to three orders of magnitude larger than in the Ξ_b^0 → η η_c Ξ^0 decay.
The dominant coupling of the P_css state is to the D̄ Ω_c̄ and D̄_s Ξ'_c channels, with weaker coupling to the η_c Ξ^0 channel.
The η_c Ξ^0 channel is open for the P_css state, while the D̄ Ω_c̄ and D̄_s Ξ'_c channels are closed, making η_c Ξ^0 the accessible final state for experimental observation.

- C2：涉及U-spin与同位旋 — 满足。证据：“flavor symmetry”涵盖U-spin与同位旋，且研究I(J^P)=1/2(1/2^-)态，明确涉及同位旋。

authors: E. Oset, L. Roca, M. Whitehead

content_sections.I Introduction: Pentaquark research has become highly active in the past decade, spurred by notable experimental findings from the LHCb collaboration, which reported hidden charm pentaquark candidates such as Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4380Pc, Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4312Pc, Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4440Pc, Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4457Pc, Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4337Pc without strangeness citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3, as well as containing a strange quark, Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs citebib.bib4 and Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4338Pcs citebib.bib5. Several theoretical models, in particular those based on meson-baryon molecular interpretations, have sought to elucidate their nature (see Refs. citebib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12 for reviews on the subject). Several states were indeed predicted citebib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21 before their observation by LHCb. A logical progression would be to explore the potential existence of hidden-charm pentaquarks with strangeness S=−2𝑆2S=-2, denoted as Pc​s​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠𝑠Pcss, for which there is currently no experimental evidence. This contrasts with theoretical predictions of models relying on implementing unitarity in coupled channels, based on potentials derived from t-channel vector meson exchange citebib.bib22, bib.bib23, which have predicted several Pc​s​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠𝑠Pcss states in the range about 4500-4700MeV. Other theoretical approaches are based on the meson exchange model citebib.bib24, sum rules citebib.bib25 and quark models citebib.bib26, bib.bib27. The states in Refs. citebib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24 emerge as generated from the intricate nonlinear dynamics involved in the unitarization of the meson-baryon scattering amplitudes. In particular, the lowest mass pole predicted in Ref. citebib.bib23, was found in the pseudoscalar-meson–baryon interaction with quantum numbers I​(JP)=12​(12−)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃12superscript12I(JP)=frac12(frac12-) and flavor c¯​c​s​s​n¯𝑐𝑐𝑠𝑠𝑛barccssn, which was associated to a molecular pentaquark state with mass about 4500MeV and width of the order of 10MeV, predominantly coupling to D¯​Ωc¯𝐷subscriptΩ𝑐barDOmegac and D¯s​Ξc′subscript¯𝐷𝑠subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐barDsXiprimec, and less strongly to ηc​Ξsubscript𝜂𝑐ΞetacXi. This theoretical backdrop prompts exploration of potential experimental reactions aimed at observing such a state. This endevour can draw inspiration from the success story of the pentaquark with hidden charm and single strangeness, Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs. Indeed this state was anticipated by coupled channel unitary models citebib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib28, and the Ξb−→J/Ψ​K−​Λ→superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝐽Ψsuperscript𝐾ΛXib-to J/Psi K-Lambda decay was proposed in Ref. citebib.bib29 to observe its signal. This reaction was later employed by the LHCb collaboration to experimentally observe this pentaquark for the first time citebib.bib4. Encouraged by this successful example, in the present work we study the possibility to see the Pc​s​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠𝑠Pcss pentaquark with I​(JP)=12​(12−)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃12superscript12I(JP)=frac12(frac12-) in the ηc​Ξ0subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0etacXi0 spectrum in the Ξb0→η​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0𝜂subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Xib0toetaetacXi0 and Ωb−→K−​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscript𝐾subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Omegab-to K-etacXi0 decays. The choice of ηc​Ξsubscript𝜂𝑐ΞetacXi for the observation of the Pc​s​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠𝑠Pcss state is justified since the D¯​Ωc¯𝐷subscriptΩ𝑐barDOmegac and D¯s​Ξc′subscript¯𝐷𝑠subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐barDsXiprimec channels are closed for decay. We also discuss if these reactions could be attainable in an experimental facility such as LHCb.
content_sections.II Formalism II.1 Summary of the unitary: We first provide a brief summary of the model from Ref. citebib.bib23 for generating the pentaquark state with flavor c¯​c​s​s​n¯𝑐𝑐𝑠𝑠𝑛barccssn and I​(JP)=12​(12−)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃12superscript12I(JP)=frac12(frac12-), further details can be found in that reference. The model follows the techniques of the chiral unitary approach citebib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32, bib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36 to sum up the coupled channels ηc​Ξsubscript𝜂𝑐ΞetacXi, D¯s​Ξc′subscript¯𝐷𝑠subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐barDsXiprimec and D¯​Ωc¯𝐷subscriptΩ𝑐barDOmegac, implementing unitarity, and starting from kernel potentials based on t-channel vector meson exchange mechanisms. The required vertices are obtained using a formalism based on heavy quark spin symmetry and the local hidden gauge approach citebib.bib37, bib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib41 properly extrapolated to the charm sector citebib.bib42, bib.bib43. Within this model, the S-wave pseudoscalar-baryon (P​B𝑃𝐵PB) tree level potentials are given by citebib.bib23 Vi​j=g2​Ci​j​(p0+p′⁣0),subscript𝑉𝑖𝑗superscript𝑔2subscript𝐶𝑖𝑗superscript𝑝0superscript𝑝′0displaystyle Vij=g2Cij(p0+pprime 0),, where the subindices denote specific pseudoscalar-baryon channels, p0​(p′⁣0)superscript𝑝0superscript𝑝′0p0(pprime 0) are the on-shell center of mass energy of the initial(final) meson, g𝑔g is a coupling constant defined in citebib.bib23, and the coefficients Ci​j=Cj​isubscript𝐶𝑖𝑗subscript𝐶𝑗𝑖Cij=Cji are given in Table. Note the dependence on the inverse of the squared masses of the vector mesons exchanged in the t-channel. Building upon the potentials of Eq. , within the framework of the coupled channels unitary approach, exact unitarity can be incorporated into the meson-baryon interaction. To this aim, we use the Bethe-Salpeter equation t=(1−V​G)−1​V,𝑡superscript1𝑉𝐺1𝑉t=(1-VG)-1V , where G𝐺G represents a diagonal matrix containing the meson-baryon loop functions. It is worth interpreting Eq. as the coupled channel resummation shown in Fig. , where each intermediate step includes all of the possible P​B𝑃𝐵PB states listed in Table. The loop function, Glsubscript𝐺𝑙Gl, for the meson-baryon channel l𝑙l, is regularized by means of a three-momentum cutoff, ΛΛLambda: Gl​(s)=∫q<Λsubscript𝐺𝑙𝑠subscript𝑞Λdisplaystyle Gl(sqrts)=intq<Lambda d3​q→(2​π)3​12​ωl​(q)​MlEl​(q)superscript𝑑3→𝑞superscript2𝜋312subscript𝜔𝑙𝑞subscript𝑀𝑙subscript𝐸𝑙𝑞displaystylefracd3vecq(2pi)3frac12omegal(q)fracMlEl(q) with q=|q→|𝑞→𝑞q=|vecq|, ωl​(q)=ml2+q2subscript𝜔𝑙𝑞subscriptsuperscript𝑚2𝑙superscript𝑞2omegal(q)=sqrtm2l+q2, El=Ml2+q2subscript𝐸𝑙subscriptsuperscript𝑀2𝑙superscript𝑞2El=sqrtM2l+q2, and ml​(Ml)subscript𝑚𝑙subscript𝑀𝑙ml(Ml) the mass of the meson(baryon). The value of the cutoff stands as the primary source of uncertainty within the model, naturally ranging between Λ=600Λ600Lambda=600MeV and 800800800MeV citebib.bib23. A potential issue with the cutoff method in the heavy hadron sector arises from the presence of a pole in the integrand of Eq. for s𝑠sqrts in the range [m+M,Λ2+m2+Λ2+M2]𝑚𝑀superscriptΛ2superscript𝑚2superscriptΛ2superscript𝑀2[m+M,sqrtLambda2+m2+sqrtLambda2+M2]. This means that near s∼Λ2+m2+Λ2+M2similar-to𝑠superscriptΛ2superscript𝑚2superscriptΛ2superscript𝑀2sqrtssimsqrtLambda2+m2+sqrtLambda2+M2, the result for the real part of the loop function may not be reliable, as the integrand above the pole cannot adequately compensate the sharp increase before the pole in the proper evaluation of the principal value. This concern with the cutoff method for heavy hadrons is also discussed in Refs. citebib.bib44, bib.bib45, bib.bib46. In the present work, it only affects the ηc​Ξ0subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0etacXi0 channel for Λ∼600similar-toΛ600Lambdasim 600MeV since, for this channel, Λ2+m2+Λ2+M2=4489superscriptΛ2superscript𝑚2superscriptΛ2superscript𝑀24489sqrtLambda2+m2+sqrtLambda2+M2=4489MeV, which is close to the mass of the predicted pentaquark state, around ∼4500similar-toabsent4500sim 4500MeV. However, since at energies close to the pentaquark mass we are significantly above the ηc​Ξ0subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0etacXi0 threshold, the real part of its loop function must be small, with the imaginary part dominating the behavior. Therefore, for Λ∼600similar-toΛ600Lambdasim 600MeV, we consider only the imaginary part in the ηc​Ξ0subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0etacXi0 loop function as a suitable approximation. This procedure is commonly used when one has a situation like this citebib.bib47. The high nonlinear dynamics involved in Eq. leads to the emergence of poles in the ti​jsubscript𝑡𝑖𝑗tij scattering amplitudes on the second Riemann sheet. From the position of these poles, the value of the mass and width of the generated resonances can be inferred. In the present case, for I​(JP)=12​(12−)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃12superscript12I(JP)=frac12(frac12-), a pole was found in Ref. citebib.bib23 corresponding to a state of mass 4535MeV and a width of 9MeV for Λ=600Λ600Lambda=600MeV, and M=4479𝑀4479M=4479MeV, Γ=12Γ12Gamma=12MeV, for Λ=800Λ800Lambda=800MeV. The difference between the results for both cutoff values is to be considered as a measure of the uncertainty in our model. Additionally, from the residue of the amplitudes at the poles, the couplings of the generated resonances to the different channels can be obtained. We found in Ref. citebib.bib23 that this pentaquark couples mostly to D¯​Ωc¯𝐷subscriptΩ𝑐barDOmegac and D¯s​Ξc′subscript¯𝐷𝑠subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐barDsXiprimec and very weakly to ηc​Ξsubscript𝜂𝑐ΞetacXi (explicit values of the couplings can be found in Table IV of Ref. citebib.bib23). However, since ηc​Ξsubscript𝜂𝑐ΞetacXi is the only open channel for the generated state, it is the only source of the width of the pentaquark. The generated pentaquark described in this section is to be understood as dynamically arising from the coupled-channel effects, and the present study aims to investigate its production in Ξb0→η​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0𝜂subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Xib0toetaetacXi0 and Ωb−→K−​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscript𝐾subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Omegab-to K-etacXi0 decays.
content_sections.II Formalism II.2 Formalism for Ξb0→η​ηc: In this section we explain the mechanism for the Ξb0→η​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0𝜂subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Xib0toetaetacXi0 process for which we follow an analogous formalism to that used in previous studies such as the calculation of Ξb−→J/Ψ​K−​Λ→superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝐽Ψsuperscript𝐾ΛXib-to J/Psi K-Lambda citebib.bib29, Λc+→π+​M​B→superscriptsubscriptΛ𝑐superscript𝜋𝑀𝐵Lambdac+topi+MB citebib.bib48 (with M​B𝑀𝐵MB a meson-baryon pair), or Λb→J/Ψ​Λ​→subscriptΛ𝑏𝐽ΨΛ1405Lambdabto J/PsiLambda citebib.bib49, which are based on a methodology initially proposed in citebib.bib50. The reaction mechanism, prior to the final state interaction (FSI), is diagrammatically depicted in Fig. , and can be understood by dividing it into three parts. Weak decay: First the b𝑏b quark within the Ξb0superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0Xib0 state undergoes a weak transition to form a c​c¯𝑐¯𝑐cbarc pair and an s𝑠s-quark, as illustrated in the left part of Fig. , resulting in a final three-quark system s​u​s𝑠𝑢𝑠sus. This transition is characterized by the matrix elements of the Cabibbo-Kobayashi-Maskawa (CKM) matrix Vc​b​Vc​s∗subscriptsuperscript𝑉absent𝑐𝑏subscriptsuperscript𝑉𝑐𝑠Vphantom*cbV*cs. This process is commonly referred to as an internal W-emission diagram. External W-emission, which is color favored, does not work for this decay but it is possible in Ωb−→K−​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscript𝐾subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Omegab-to K-etacXi0, as will be discussed later. Further discussion and justification of the suppression of alternative topologies for weak mechanisms at the quark level can be found in Refs. citebib.bib48, bib.bib51. Hadronization: The c​c¯𝑐¯𝑐cbarc pair combines to create the ηcsubscript𝜂𝑐etac meson, while the virtual s​u​s𝑠𝑢𝑠sus three-quark state undergoes hadronization to form a meson-baryon pair. This hadronization proceeds via the generation of a q¯​q¯𝑞𝑞barqq pair with vacuum quantum numbers, in a philosophy in the line to the P03superscriptsubscript𝑃033P0 model citebib.bib52, bib.bib53. In the present process, the formation of a q¯​q¯𝑞𝑞barqq pair between the lower u𝑢u and s𝑠s lines is not possible. Indeed, initially strong correlation between the u𝑢u and s𝑠s quarks of the original ΞbsubscriptΞ𝑏Xib state is assumed, with the quarks moving independently within a potential well. Since the ΞbsubscriptΞ𝑏Xib (JP=1/2+superscript𝐽𝑃1superscript2JP=1/2+) resides in the ground state of the three-quark (u​s​b)𝑢𝑠𝑏(usb) configuration, the relative angular momenta between the individual quarks are all zero. Following the weak transition, but before the hadronization process, the three-quark state (s​u​s)𝑠𝑢𝑠(sus) must be in a p-wave to produce a pentaquark with JP=1/2−superscript𝐽𝑃1superscript2JP=1/2-. As the initial u𝑢u and s𝑠s quarks are regarded as spectators and initially possess L=0𝐿0L=0, the only possible scenario is for the upper s𝑠s quark to carry an angular momentum of L=1𝐿1L=1. Since the final-state mesons and baryons are in their respective ground states and in S-wave in relation to each other, all angular momenta in the final state are zero. Therefore, the q¯​q¯𝑞𝑞barqq pair is constrained to be produced solely between the upper s𝑠s and u𝑢u quarks, as depicted in Fig. . To evaluate the scattering amplitude for the process depicted in Fig. , let us start by considering the wave function of the initial Ξb0superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0Xib0 in flavor space citebib.bib54, bib.bib55, bib.bib56 : |Ξb0⟩=12​|b​(u​s−s​u)⟩,ketsuperscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏012ket𝑏𝑢𝑠𝑠𝑢displaystyle|Xib0=frac1sqrt2|b(us-su),, which is antisymmetric in the lighter quarks. After the weak process, but before the hadronization, the three-quark state turns into 12​|s​(u​s−s​u)⟩,12ket𝑠𝑢𝑠𝑠𝑢displaystylefrac1sqrt2|s(us-su),, which preserves the same antisymmetric flavor and spin correlation given that the initial u𝑢u and s𝑠s quarks are considered as spectators. content_sections.II Formalism II.3 Formalism for Ωb−→K−​η: In this section, we focus solely on the differences and particularities of the Ωb−→K−​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscript𝐾subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Omegab-to K-etacXi0 decay with respect to the process Ξb0→η​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0𝜂subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Xib0toetaetacXi0, as its formalism parallels that of the preceding one. In the latter case, the q¯​q¯𝑞𝑞barqq production of the hadronization process must occur between the c¯¯𝑐barc and s𝑠s quarks in order to have the opportunity to produce any of the final states of Table. Therefore, the amplitude for the external emission process of the Ωb−→K−​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscript𝐾subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Omegab-to K-etacXi0 decay is ℳe​e​(Minv)=Nc​𝒞​GD¯0​Ωc​(Minv)​tD¯0​Ωc,ηc​Ξ0​(Minv). subscriptℳ𝑒𝑒subscript𝑀invsubscript𝑁𝑐𝒞subscript𝐺superscript¯𝐷0subscriptΩ𝑐subscript𝑀invsubscript𝑡superscript¯𝐷0subscriptΩ𝑐subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0subscript𝑀invdisplaystyleMee(Mrm inv)=Nccal CGbarD0Omegac(Mrm inv),tbarD0Omegac,etacXi0(Mrm inv),. Note that the external W-emission is enhanced by a factor of the number of colors, Nc=3subscript𝑁𝑐3Nc=3, compared to the internal W-emission. This arises from the need to sum over the three possible quark colors in the hadronization process in Fig. (b), unlike in Fig. (a) where the color is fixed in the final meson. There is indeed an ambiguity in the relative sign when compared to the internal emission process, Eq. , which affects the interference between these two terms. However, as we will see in the results section, the Ωb−→K−​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscript𝐾subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Omegab-to K-etacXi0 decay spectrum is largely determined by the external-emission mechanism, rendering this ambiguity in sign rather inconsequential.
