content_sections.III Results: In Fig. , we show the ηc​Ξ0subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0etacXi0 invariant mass distributions for the Ξb0→η​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0𝜂subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Xib0toetaetacXi0 and Ωb−→K−​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscript𝐾subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Omegab-to K-etacXi0 decays. In each plot, the solid line represents the result obtained with a cutoff of Λ=600Λ600Lambda=600MeV, while the dashed line corresponds to Λ=800Λ800Lambda=800MeV. The dotted lines in the plot for the Ωb−→K−​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscript𝐾subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Omegab-to K-etacXi0 decay represent the results obtained by changing the sign in Eq. to account for the ambiguity in its relative sign compared to Eq. , as explained in the previous section. The plots clearly show that the uncertainty resulting from this effect is very small, primarily as a result of the considerable predominance of external emission relative to internal one in this decay. Although there is a global arbitrary normalization factor, the relative strengths between the different reactions reflect genuine theoretical predictions. An immediately striking observation from the figures is the significant difference in intensity and shape between the distributions of both decays, calling for a deeper examination of the underlying reasons. Regarding the large difference in strength in favor of the Ωb−superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏Omegab- decay, it is a consequence of the interplay of various contributing factors. Firstly, the Ωb−superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏Omegab- decay is predominantly governed by the external W-emission mechanism. This amplitude enjoys a Nc=3subscript𝑁𝑐3Nc=3 enhancement, see Eq. , with respect to the internal W-emission amplitude in Ξb0superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0Xib0 decay, Eq. . In addition, the latter one is suppressed by a 1/6161/sqrt6 factor. When these amplitudes are squared to compute the spectrum, the Ωb−superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏Omegab- decay ends up with a magnitude approximately 50 times larger, solely due to numerical flavor and color coefficients. In addition, we see in Fig. that the factor G3​T31≡GD¯0​Ωc​tD¯0​Ωc,ηc​Ξ0subscript𝐺3subscript𝑇31subscript𝐺superscript¯𝐷0subscriptΩ𝑐subscript𝑡superscript¯𝐷0subscriptΩ𝑐subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0G3T31equiv GbarD0Omegac,tbarD0Omegac,etacXi0 in Eq. is typically about four times larger than the factor G1​T11≡Gηc​Ξ0​tηc​Ξ0,ηc​Ξ0subscript𝐺1subscript𝑇11subscript𝐺subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0subscript𝑡subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0G1T11equiv GetacXi0,tetacXi0,etacXi0 in Eq. , which contributes an extra factor of 16 in the distribution in favor also of the Ωb−superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏Omegab- decay. Combining this with the previously mentioned factor of 50 results in an 800-fold enhancement of the Ωb−superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏Omegab- spectrum compared to that of the Ξb0superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0Xib0. On the other hand, the reason for the strong dip seen in the Ξb0superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0Xib0 distribution is the interference between the FSI, Fig. (b), and the tree level process, Fig. (a). Mathematically, the tree level is accounted for by the 1 term in Eq. , which interferes with the G1​T11subscript𝐺1subscript𝑇11G1T11 term from the FSI in that equation. It can be seen in Fig. that at the resonant position, the real part of the G1​T11subscript𝐺1subscript𝑇11G1T11 term coincidentally takes a value very close to −11-1, leading to a destructive interference with the +11+1 term of the tree level mechanism. This dip phenomenon is a well-known feature in scattering theory, typically arising from strong interference between a resonance and a non-resonant background. In fact, if the tree-level contribution was absent, the spectrum for Ξb0superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0Xib0 decay would closely resemble that of Ωb−superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏Omegab- decay, except for the numerical factor 800800800 explained above. Summarizing the previous discussion, we can conclude that the ηc​Ξ0subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0etacXi0 spectrum in the Ωb−→K−​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscript𝐾subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Omegab-to K-etacXi0 exhibits a strength approximately 800 times larger than that in Ξb0→η​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0𝜂subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Xib0toetaetacXi0. Additionally, the Pc​s​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠𝑠Pcss state considered in the present work would appear as a Breit-Wigner-like shape in the former decay, while in the latter, it would manifest as a significant dip. The decay modes under study are chosen as those that can provide the best access to the Pc​s​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠𝑠Pcss states. Another important factor is whether or not these decay processes can be reconstructed and analysed at current or future facilities. The requirement to study beauty baryon decays limits this discussion, at present, to the current and future upgrades of the LHCb experiment, where the proton-proton collisions provide access to all beauty hadrons. Using the Run1 and Run2 datasets, several decay modes of the Ξb(0,−)superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0Xib(0,-) and Ωb−superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏Omegab- baryons have been observedcitebib.bib65, bib.bib64, bib.bib63, bib.bib62, bib.bib61, bib.bib60. However, the sample sizes from Run 1 and Run 2 are unlikely to be sufficient to observe the decays in question and certainly not large enough to enable amplitude analyses, where 𝒪​𝒪1000O candidates are required. These observations are of processes that are well-suited to the strengths of the LHCb experiment, reconstruction of charged tracks of hadrons and muons. We now consider the relative strengths and weaknesses for the decay processes considered here to be detected and reconstructed by the LHCb experiment. • Ξb0→η​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0𝜂subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Xib0toetaetacXi0: This process will be challenging to reconstruct at LHCb due to the presence of neutral particles in the decays of the η𝜂eta (e. g. γ​γ𝛾𝛾gammagamma or π+​π−​π0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋superscript𝜋0pi+pi-pi0) and Ξ0(→Λ0​π0)annotatedsuperscriptΞ0→absentsuperscriptΛ0superscript𝜋0Xi0(toLambda0pi0) mesons. The relatively long-lived Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0 baryon introduces a secondary challenge because it will often decay downstream of the LHCb vertex locator. Such candidates are reconstructed using the downstream tracking detectors only with reduced efficiency. Finally, the ηcsubscript𝜂𝑐etac meson appears in all three final states and can be reconstructed in hadronic final states such as p​p¯𝑝¯𝑝pbarp. • Ωb−→K−​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscript𝐾subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Omegab-to K-etacXi0: Compared to the Ξb0superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0Xib0 decay mode above, the yield of Ωb−superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏Omegab- baryon decays will be suppressed by the ratio of production fractions in the p​p𝑝𝑝pp collisions at the LHC. However, swapping the η𝜂eta meson for a charged kaon brings a large improvement in the reconstruction efficiency and will likely mean that this process is more promising to study first. • Ωb−→KS0​ηc​Ξ−→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞOmegab-to KS0etacXi-: While we have studied the decay Ωb−→K−​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscript𝐾subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Omegab-to K-etacXi0, the Ωb−→K¯0​ηc​Ξ−→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscript¯𝐾0subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞOmegab-tobarK0etacXi- mode, to be seen experimentally in Ωb−→KS0​ηc​Ξ−→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞOmegab-to KS0etacXi-, which has the same strength in our approach due to isospin symmetry, seems the most promising channel to study at the LHCb experiment. Reconstructing this final state requires no neutral particles because the KS0→π+​π−→superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋KS0topi+pi- and Ξ−→Λ0​π−→superscriptΞsuperscriptΛ0superscript𝜋Xi-toLambda0pi- decay modes can be utilised. Similar to the Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0 baryon, the KS0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0KS0 meson reconstruction efficiency is lower than for a charged kaon given the two-track final state and long lifetime that means many candidates will decay downstream of the LHCb vertex detector. Nevertheless, considering all of the particles involved, this process should be the most straightforward to study using LHCb data samples. It should also be noted that the branching fractions of all three decay modes are unknown, but given the quark-level processes involved, it is reasonable to expect they are all of similar magnitude and will not be significantly suppressed.
content_sections.IV Conclusions: In light of previous theoretical predictions of several pentaquark states Pc​s​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠𝑠Pcss, with global flavor c¯​c​s​s​n¯𝑐𝑐𝑠𝑠𝑛barccssn, based on formalisms implementing unitary in coupled channels, we have studied the feasibility of seeing the anticipated I​(JP)=12​(12−)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃12superscript12I(JP)=frac12(frac12-) pentaquark in the ηc​Ξ0subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0etacXi0 spectrum in the decays Ξb0→η​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0𝜂subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Xib0toetaetacXi0 and Ωb−→K−​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscript𝐾subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Omegab-to K-etacXi0. The theoretical model employed to generate the pentaquark is based on techniques similar to the chiral unitary approach, aiming to implement unitarity in coupled channels into the meson-baryon scattering amplitudes. The only input of the unitarization procedure is the tree level pseudoscalar-baryon potentials obtained from vector meson exchange mechanisms with Lagrangians derived from appropriate extensions of the local hidden gauge approach to the heavy quark sector. The pentaquark states emerge dynamically as poles in unphysical Riemann sheets of the unitarized pseudoscalar-baryon amplitudes, without the necessity of including them as explicit degrees of freedom. In the present work, the pentaquarks are generated in the Ξb0superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0Xib0 and Ωb−superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏Omegab- decays by implementing the ηc​Ξ0subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0etacXi0 and D¯0​Ωcsuperscript¯𝐷0subscriptΩ𝑐barD0Omegac final state interaction, considering all the possible pseudoscalar-baryon coupled channels significant to generate the pentaquark according to the model. To evaluate the tree level Ξb0superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0Xib0 and Ωb−superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏Omegab- decay amplitudes, necessary before implementing the final state interaction, we identify the dominant mechanisms at the quark level. Subsequently, we implement the hadronization, after the weak transition, incorporating the generation of q¯​q¯𝑞𝑞barqq pairs within the philosophy of the P03superscriptsubscript𝑃033P0 model. The different channels are related by flavor symmetry considerations at the quark level. We provide, up to a global factor, the ηc​Ξ0subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0etacXi0 invariant mass distributions for the Ξb0→η​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0𝜂subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Xib0toetaetacXi0 and Ωb−→K−​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscript𝐾subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Omegab-to K-etacXi0 decays which manifest a large difference in both strength and shape between the two decays. The strength notably favors the Ωb−superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏Omegab- decay by about a factor 800, primarily influenced by flavor and color numerical coefficients in the elementary amplitude. Furthermore, the spectrum for the Ξb0superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0Xib0 decay manifests the resonance as a distinctive dip in its shape, resulting from the interference between non-resonant tree level production and the resonant final state interaction responsible for the pentaquark generation. On the other hand, the Ωb−superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏Omegab- decay leads to a very clear peak resembling a Breit-Wigner distribution. Therefore, for both theoretical and experimental reasons, we recommend searches using the Run3 and Run4 datasets from LHCb to focus initially on searching for Ωb−→KS0​ηc​Ξ−→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞOmegab-to KS0etacXi- decays and studying the different contributing amplitudes to search for the Pc​s​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠𝑠Pcss states. Following this, the focus should first switch to study Ωb−→K−​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscript𝐾subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Omegab-to K-etacXi0 decays and then finally to the Ξb0→η​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0𝜂subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Xib0toetaetacXi0 process, possibly requiring data samples from the proposed LHCb Upgrade 2 projectcitebib.bib66.

This project has received funding from the European Union Horizon 2020 research and innovation programme under the program H2020-INFRAIA-2018-1, grant agreement No. 824093 of the STRONG-2020 project. This work is partly supported by the Science and Technology Facilities Council (UK).

sorted_papers.title: Heavy flavor molecular states with strangeness
sorted_papers.abstract:
We proposed a unified framework to describe the interactions of the observed
$T_{cc}$, $P_c$, and $P_{cs}$ within a quark level interaction in our previous
work. In this work, we generalize our framework to the loosely bound hadronic
molecules composed of heavy flavor di-hadrons with strangeness. We predict the
possible $D^{(*)}D^{(*)}_s$ molecular states in the SU(3) limit with the masses
of the $P_c$ states as the inputs. We also investigate the baryon-meson and
baryon-baryon systems and consider the SU(3) breaking effect in their flavor
wave functions. We generalize our isospin criterion of the formation of heavy
flavor di-hadron molecules to the $U/V$ spin case. For a specific heavy flavor
meson-meson, baryon-meson, or baryon-baryon system, the interactions for the
states with the same flavor and spin matrix elements can be related by a
generalized flavor-spin symmetry.

qualitative: The paper proposes a unified quark-level framework to describe loosely bound hadronic molecules composed of heavy flavor di-hadrons with strangeness, extending previous work on Tcc, Pc, and Pcs states.

The interactions in heavy flavor di-hadron systems are primarily mediated by light flavor meson exchange, with the strength determined by the flavor and spin structures of the light degrees of freedom.
A hydrogen-like picture is adopted, where the binding mechanism of exotic states like Tcc, Pc, and Pcs is explained through molecular interactions between heavy di-hadrons.
The framework uses a quark-level Lagrangian to parameterize effective potentials in terms of light quark-quark coupling constants and flavor-spin matrix elements.
The interactions between different heavy flavor di-hadron systems (meson-meson, baryon-meson, baryon-baryon) are related via flavor-spin symmetry, allowing a unified description.
SU(3) flavor symmetry is applied in the limit to predict D(*)D(*)_s molecular states, with masses of the Pc states used as input.
SU(3) breaking effects are considered in the flavor wave functions of baryon-meson and baryon-baryon systems.
The isospin criterion for molecular state formation is generalized to the U/V spin case, extending the symmetry to include strange quarks.

A generalized flavor-spin symmetry is established: for a given heavy flavor system (meson-meson, baryon-meson, baryon-baryon), interactions of states with identical flavor and spin matrix elements are related by this symmetry.
The framework enables a systematic analysis of molecular multiplets across different di-hadron systems, including those with strangeness.
quantitative: The Tcc, Pc, and Pcs states are observed below their respective di-hadron thresholds by several to several tens of MeV, supporting the molecular interpretation.
The effective potentials are parameterized using light quark-quark coupling constants and flavor-spin matrix elements derived from the light degrees of freedom.
The masses of the Pc states are used as input to predict the possible D(*)D(*)_s molecular states in the SU(3) limit.
The paper does not provide specific numerical values for predicted masses of D(*)D(*)_s states or coupling constants, but emphasizes the predictive power of the symmetry framework.
The framework allows for the calculation of binding energies and state properties based on the flavor-spin structure, though specific numbers are not given in the provided text.

sorted_papers.reason:
论文全面覆盖SU(3)对称性与U-spin、同位旋的理论框架，明确研究重味分子态的SU(3)极限与破缺，提出广义味-自旋对称性，与查询高度契合。  

约束项检查：  
- C1：研究SU(3)味对称性 — 满足。证据：“We predict the possible $D^{(*)}D^{(*)}_s$ molecular states in the SU(3) limit” 对比 “SU(3) flavor symmetry”  
- C2：研究U-spin与同位旋 — 满足。证据：“generalize our isospin criterion ... to the $U/V$ spin case” 对比 “U-spin, isospin”  

不相关性 / 风险点 / 缺口：  
- 无。论文直接回应查询中所有核心概念，无偏离。
authors: Kan Chen, Bo Wang, and Shi-Lin Zhu

content_sections.I Introduction: The study of exotic hadrons with configurations beyond the conventional quark model has become a hot topic since the discovery of X​𝑋3872X citebib.bib1. Especially, the Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc citebib.bib2, bib.bib3, Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs citebib.bib4 together with the recently reported Tc​csubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc citebib.bib5, bib.bib6 are manifestly exotic states. The observation of these states indicates that the interactions between heavy flavor di-hadrons share very similar binding mechanisms. Since the observed Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc, Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs, and Tc​csubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc are below the thresholds of the corresponding di-hadron systems from several to several tens MeVs, a natural explanation is that these states are molecules. To describe the possible similarities of the binding mechanisms between the above heavy flavor di-hadron systems, we proposed a hydrogen-like picture and studied the heavy flavor meson-meson (M𝑀M–M𝑀M), baryon-meson (B𝐵B–M𝑀M), and baryon-baryon (B𝐵B–B𝐵B) molecular states in Ref. citebib.bib7. We assume that the interactions of heavy flavor di-hadron systems are mainly from the exchange of the light flavor mesons, which is closely related to the flavor and spin structures of light degrees of freedom (d. o. f) in the di-hadron systems. To address the above molecule picture, we adopt a quark-level Lagrangian citebib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib7 to describe the interactions of different heavy flavor di-hadron systems. Then the corresponding effective potentials can be parameterized in terms of the light quark-quark coupling constants and the flavor and spin matrix elements which are related to its light d. o. f. In this framework, we obtain a satisfactory description of the Tc​csubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc, Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc, Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs states, and other possible bound states in the heavy flavor meson-meson, baryon-meson, and baryon-baryon di-hadron systems which arise from the isospin singlet and triplet meson exchange interactions. The interactions of different heavy flavor di-hadron systems (M𝑀M–M𝑀M, B𝐵B–M𝑀M, and B𝐵B–B𝐵B) can be understood in a molecule picture simultaneously. The simple Lagrangian introduced in our work possesses two important advantages in Ref. citebib.bib7. It not only relates the interactions of different heavy flavor di-hadron (M𝑀M–M𝑀M, B𝐵B–M𝑀M, and B𝐵B–B𝐵B) systems via their flavor-spin structures but also relates the interactions of the multiplets in a specific heavy flavor di-hadron system. In Table , we list the systems which were already studied in Ref. citebib.bib7. In this work, we further adopt this framework to study the rest of the doubly heavy flavor di-hadron systems. The inclusion of the rest of the doubly heavy flavor di-hadron systems allows us to present a full analysis of the possible flavor-spin symmetry and the interactions of molecular multiplets in a specific di-hadron systems. This is the main task of this work. Some of the doubly heavy tetraquark states with strangeness have already been discussed in literature. For example, the strange doubly heavy tetraquark states in molecular configurations have been discussed in Refs. citebib.bib16, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib17. The compact strange tetraquark states were studied via various methods, such as quark potential models citebib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, lattice QCD citebib.bib22, chromo-magnetic interaction model (CMI) citebib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26, and QCD sum rule citebib.bib27, bib.bib28. The strange doubly heavy baryon-meson and di-baryon systems have also been discussed in literature. The possible pentaquark states composed of q​q​q​c​c¯𝑞𝑞𝑞𝑐¯𝑐qqqcbarc configurations with q=u𝑞𝑢q=u, d𝑑d, and s𝑠s were studied within the framework of CMI model citebib.bib29. The OBE model was adopted to study the interactions of the Ξc(′,∗)​D¯s(∗)Xic(prime,*)barDs(*) citebib.bib30 and Ωc(∗)​D¯s(∗)superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑐superscriptsubscript¯𝐷𝑠Omegac(*)barDs(*) citebib.bib31 systems. In Ref. citebib.bib32, the hidden charm pentaquark states composed of q​q​s​c​c¯𝑞𝑞𝑠𝑐¯𝑐qqscbarc, q​s​s​c​c¯𝑞𝑠𝑠𝑐¯𝑐qsscbarc, and s​s​s​c​c¯𝑠𝑠𝑠𝑐¯𝑐ssscbarc configurations are studied in a diquark-diquark-antiquark model. A general discussion of the heavy-antiheavy hadronic molecules was performed in Ref. citebib.bib33. In addition, the authors in Ref. citebib.bib34 adopted the OBE model to discuss the di-baryon systems composed of spin-12/321232frac12/frac32 and flavor-𝟑¯/𝟔bold-¯36bar3/6 single heavy baryons systematically. A general discussion on these systems based on the resonance saturation model can be found in Ref. citebib.bib16. This paper is organized as follows. In Sec. , we introduce our theoretical framework. Then we present our numerical results and discussions in Sec. . In Sec. , we conclude this work with a short summary.
content_sections.II Theoretical framework: The interactions for the heavy flavor di-hadron systems listed in Table can be calculated by considering the possible contributions from the exchanges of light mesons, which depend on the flavor and spin structures of the heavy flavor di-hadron systems from their light d. o. f. On the other hand, the S𝑆S-wave interactions of the ground heavy flavor di-hadron systems may arise from the exchange of the JP=0+superscript𝐽𝑃superscript0JP=0+ scalar or 1+superscript11+ axial vector mesons. Thus, we introduce the following quark level Lagrangians citebib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib7 ℒ=gs​q¯​𝒮​q+ga​q¯​γμ​γ5​𝒜μ​qℒsubscript𝑔𝑠¯𝑞𝒮𝑞subscript𝑔𝑎¯𝑞subscript𝛾𝜇superscript𝛾5superscript𝒜𝜇𝑞displaystyleL=gsbarqSq+gabarqgammamugamma5Amuq to describe the interactions among the considered ground heavy flavor di-hadron systems. Here, q=(u,d,s)𝑞𝑢𝑑𝑠q=(u,d,s), gssubscript𝑔𝑠gs and gasubscript𝑔𝑎ga are two independent coupling constants that encode the nonperturbative low energy dynamics of the di-hadron systems. From Eq. , the effective potential of the light quark-quark interactions reads Vq​qsubscript𝑉𝑞𝑞displaystyle Vqq =displaystyle= gs​𝝀1⋅𝝀2+ga​𝝀1⋅𝝀2​𝝈1⋅𝝈2⋅subscript𝑔𝑠subscript𝝀1subscript𝝀2⋅⋅subscript𝑔𝑎subscript𝝀1subscript𝝀2subscript𝝈1subscript𝝈2displaystyletildegslambda1cdotlambda2+tildegalambda1cdotlambda2sigma1cdotsigma2 Here, i𝑖i and j𝑗j sum from 1 to 3 and 4 to 7, respectively. The λ18​λ28superscriptsubscript𝜆18superscriptsubscript𝜆28lambda18lambda28, λ1i​λ2isuperscriptsubscript𝜆1𝑖superscriptsubscript𝜆2𝑖lambda1ilambda2i, and λ1j​λ2jsuperscriptsubscript𝜆1𝑗superscriptsubscript𝜆2𝑗lambda1jlambda2j are the SU flavor matrices of the isospin singlet, triplet, and two doublets, respectively. The 𝝈1⋅𝝈2⋅subscript𝝈1subscript𝝈2sigma1cdotsigma2 is the Pauli matrix in spin space. The redefined coupling constants are gs=gs2/m𝒮2subscript𝑔𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑔𝑠2superscriptsubscript𝑚𝒮2tildegs=gs2/mS2 and ga=ga2/m𝒜2subscript𝑔𝑎superscriptsubscript𝑔𝑎2subscriptsuperscript𝑚2𝒜tildega=ga2/m2A. The local form of the effective potential Vq​qsubscript𝑉𝑞𝑞Vqq is the result of integrating out the exchanged spurions. The effective potential in Eq. consists of three sets of flavor related operators and three sets of flavor-spin related operators. For convenience, we adopt the following notations 𝒪1subscript𝒪1displaystyleO1 =displaystyle= λ18​λ28,𝒪4=λ18​λ28​𝝈1⋅𝝈2,superscriptsubscript𝜆18superscriptsubscript𝜆28subscript𝒪4⋅superscriptsubscript𝜆18superscriptsubscript𝜆28subscript𝝈1subscript𝝈2displaystylelambda18lambda28,quadO4=lambda18lambda28sigma1cdotsigma2, 𝒪2subscript𝒪2displaystyleO2 =displaystyle= λ1i​λ2i,𝒪5=λ1i​λ2i​𝝈1⋅𝝈2,superscriptsubscript𝜆1𝑖superscriptsubscript𝜆2𝑖subscript𝒪5⋅superscriptsubscript𝜆1𝑖superscriptsubscript𝜆2𝑖subscript𝝈1subscript𝝈2displaystylelambda1ilambda2i,quadO5=lambda1ilambda2isigma1cdotsigma2, 𝒪3subscript𝒪3displaystyleO3 =displaystyle= λ1j​λ2j,𝒪6=λ1j​λ2j​𝝈1⋅𝝈2. superscriptsubscript𝜆1𝑗superscriptsubscript𝜆2𝑗subscript𝒪6⋅superscriptsubscript𝜆1𝑗superscriptsubscript𝜆2𝑗subscript𝝈1subscript𝝈2displaystylelambda1jlambda2j,quadO6=lambda1jlambda2jsigma1cdotsigma2. In our previous work citebib.bib7, we only consider the di-hadron systems without the strange meson exchange contribution. The coupling constants gssubscript𝑔𝑠tildegs and gasubscript𝑔𝑎tildega are obtained from a solvable Lippmann-Schwinger equation (LSE). For the heavy flavor di-hadron systems containing strange quark(s), the contributions from the λ1j​λ2jsuperscriptsubscript𝜆1𝑗superscriptsubscript𝜆2𝑗lambda1jlambda2j (j=4,5,6,7𝑗4567j=4,5,6,7) operator should also be included. Note that in the scalar or axial-vector SU flavor octet, the physical masses of the exchanged mesons that belong to the flavor isospin doublets are close to the masses of their isospin singlet or triplet mesons. Thus, we take the SU limit and set the coupling parameters gssubscript𝑔𝑠tildegs and gasubscript𝑔𝑎tildega to be equal to those of the isospin singlet or triplet mesons. The effective potential for a specific heavy flavor di-hadron H1​H2subscript𝐻1subscript𝐻2H1H2 system with total isospin I𝐼I and total angular momentum J𝐽J can be written as V[H1​H2]JI=⟨[H1​H2]JI|Vq​q|[H1​H2]JI⟩,subscript𝑉superscriptsubscriptdelimited-subscript𝐻1subscript𝐻2𝐽𝐼quantum-operator-productsuperscriptsubscriptdelimited-subscript𝐻1subscript𝐻2𝐽𝐼subscript𝑉𝑞𝑞superscriptsubscriptdelimited-subscript𝐻1subscript𝐻2𝐽𝐼displaystyle V[H1H2]JI=[H1H2]JI|Vqq|[H1H2]JI, where |[H1​H2]JI⟩ketsuperscriptsubscriptdelimited-subscript𝐻1subscript𝐻2𝐽𝐼|[H1H2]JI denotes the quark-level spin-flavor wave function of the H1​H2subscript𝐻1subscript𝐻2H1H2 system. Note that as an approximation, the correction from heavy degree of freedom is omitted. The operators 𝝀𝟏⋅𝝀𝟐⋅subscript𝝀1subscript𝝀2lambda1cdotlambda2 and 𝝈1⋅𝝈2⋅subscript𝝈1subscript𝝈2sigma1cdotsigma2 only act on the flavor and spin wave functions of the light quark components. As can be seen from Eq. , the interactions of the considered di-hadron systems only depend on the flavor (⟨𝝀1⋅𝝀2⟩delimited-⟨⟩⋅subscript𝝀1subscript𝝀2lambda1cdotlambda2) and spin (⟨𝝈1⋅𝝈2⟩delimited-⟨⟩⋅subscript𝝈1subscript𝝈2sigma1cdotsigma2) matrix elements, implying a flavor-spin symmetry for the interactions of the heavy flavor di-hadron systems. In the following, we will further discuss the properties of this symmetry.
content_sections.II Theoretical framework II.1 The spin m: The operator 𝝈1⋅𝝈2⋅subscript𝝈1subscript𝝈2sigma1cdotsigma2 can be easily calculated at hadron level with the technique of angular momentum theory. At hadron level, the matrix elements of the 𝒍1⋅𝒍2⋅subscript𝒍1subscript𝒍2l1cdotl2 spin-spin operator in the light d. o. f for the discussed M𝑀M–M𝑀M, B𝐵B–M𝑀M, and B𝐵B–B𝐵B systems can be obtained via a spin rearrangement procedure. The di-hadron systems can be related to the states with a specific total light spin and a heavy spin by the relations citebib.bib35 |l1h1displaystyleleft|l1h1right. S1l2h2S2JM⟩displaystyleleft. S1l2h2S2JMright where lisubscript𝑙𝑖li and hisubscriptℎ𝑖hi are the light and heavy spin for Hisubscript𝐻𝑖Hi hadron, respectively. L𝐿L and H𝐻H are the total light spin and total heavy spin of the di-hadron system, respectively. Sisubscript𝑆𝑖Si is the total spin of the Hisubscript𝐻𝑖Hi hadron. Then the matrix elements of the operator 𝒍1⋅𝒍2⋅subscript𝒍1subscript𝒍2l1cdotl2 can be obtained as ⟨𝒍1⋅𝒍2⟩delimited-⟨⟩⋅subscript𝒍1subscript𝒍2displaystyleleftl1cdotl2right =displaystyle= ∑L,H[L​(L+1)−l1​(l1+1)−l2​(l2+1)]subscript𝐿𝐻delimited-𝐿𝐿1subscript𝑙1subscript𝑙11subscript𝑙2subscript𝑙21displaystylesumL,Hleft[Lleft(L+1right)-l1left(l1+1right)-l2left(l2+1right)right] Since the hisubscriptℎ𝑖hi is identical for all the single heavy flavor hadrons, we use a more brief notation |l1,S1;l2,S2;J⟩ketsubscript𝑙1subscript𝑆1subscript𝑙2subscript𝑆2𝐽|l1,S1;l2,S2;J to specify the spin wave function of a [H1​H2]Jsubscriptdelimited-subscript𝐻1subscript𝐻2𝐽[H1H2]J di-hadron system. content_sections.II Theoretical framework II.2 The flavor: Now we discuss the quark-level flavor wave functions of the considered di-hadron systems. We construct the explicit forms of flavor wave functions for the multiplets of different heavy flavor di-hadron systems in the Eqs. -(LABEL:BBF) using the SU Clebsch-Gordan (CG) coefficients citebib.bib36. We collect the results of the ⟨𝝀1⋅𝝀2⟩delimited-⟨⟩⋅subscript𝝀1subscript𝝀2lambda1cdotlambda2 matrix elements for the different di-hadron systems in their corresponding SU representations in Table . For the di-hadron systems in the same SU multiplet, their ⟨𝝀1⋅𝝀2⟩delimited-⟨⟩⋅subscript𝝀1subscript𝝀2lambda1cdotlambda2 matrix elements have the same value. Combining the results in Table and Table , we emphasize that the flavor-spin symmetry in Eq. demonstrates that if two heavy flavor di-hadron systems have the same spin structure |l1,S1;l2,S2;J⟩ketsubscript𝑙1subscript𝑆1subscript𝑙2subscript𝑆2𝐽|l1,S1;l2,S2;J and belong to the same SU multiplet, they have the same effective potentials. Correspondingly, if the potential can provide enough attractions to form a bound state, their binding energies should have the same size. In addition, we can extract many other useful information numerically from Table and Table . For example, as presented in Table , in the B𝐵B–B𝐵B systems, the matrix elements ⟨𝝀1⋅𝝀2⟩delimited-⟨⟩⋅subscript𝝀1subscript𝝀2lambda1cdotlambda2 for the 𝟏𝟓1515 and 𝟔¯2subscript¯62bar62 multiplets are the same. Thus, if their spin matrix elements turn out to be the same (it is easy to find two different spin states that have the same ⟨𝒍1⋅𝒍2⟩delimited-⟨⟩⋅subscript𝒍1subscript𝒍2l1cdotl2 in Table ), they should have the same effective potentials. Thus, the systems which share the same values of the ⟨𝝀1⋅𝝀2⟩delimited-⟨⟩⋅subscript𝝀1subscript𝝀2lambda1cdotlambda2 and ⟨𝝈1⋅𝝈2⟩delimited-⟨⟩⋅subscript𝝈1subscript𝝈2sigma1cdotsigma2 matrix elements should have the same effective potential and are related by this generalized flavor-spin symmetry.
content_sections.II Theoretical framework II.3 The wave f: In the previous section, we assume a perfect SU symmetry when we construct the flavor wave functions of the heavy flavor di-hadron systems. To discuss the physical di-hadron systems, we need to distinguish the s𝑠s quark from u𝑢u, d𝑑d quarks when we construct the corresponding flavor wave functions. In Table , we collect the quark-level flavor wave functions of the charmed hadrons considered in this work. Then we construct the total wave functions of the considered heavy-flavor baryon-meson systems as |[H1​H2]JI⟩ketsuperscriptsubscriptdelimited-subscript𝐻1subscript𝐻2𝐽𝐼displaystyle|[H1H2]JI =displaystyle= ∑mI1,mI2CI1,mI1;I2,mI2I,Iz​ϕI1,mI1H1​f​ϕI2,mI2H2​fsubscriptsubscript𝑚subscript𝐼1subscript𝑚subscript𝐼2superscriptsubscript𝐶subscript𝐼1subscript𝑚subscript𝐼1subscript𝐼2subscript𝑚subscript𝐼2𝐼subscript𝐼𝑧superscriptsubscriptitalic-ϕsubscript𝐼1subscript𝑚subscript𝐼1subscript𝐻1𝑓superscriptsubscriptitalic-ϕsubscript𝐼2subscript𝑚subscript𝐼2subscript𝐻2𝑓displaystylesummI1,mI2CI1,mI1;I2,mI2I,IzphiI1,mI1H1fphiI2,mI2H2f Here, the spin wave functions ϕS1,mS1H1​ssuperscriptsubscriptitalic-ϕsubscript𝑆1subscript𝑚subscript𝑆1subscript𝐻1𝑠phiS1,mS1H1s and ϕS2,mS2H2​ssuperscriptsubscriptitalic-ϕsubscript𝑆2subscript𝑚subscript𝑆2subscript𝐻2𝑠phiS2,mS2H2s can be constructed directly using the SU CG coefficients. For the flavor wave functions ϕI1,mI1H1​fsuperscriptsubscriptitalic-ϕsubscript𝐼1subscript𝑚subscript𝐼1subscript𝐻1𝑓phiI1,mI1H1f and ϕI2,mI2H2​fsuperscriptsubscriptitalic-ϕsubscript𝐼2subscript𝑚subscript𝐼2subscript𝐻2𝑓phiI2,mI2H2f, we adopt the flavor wave functions listed in Table and use the SU CG coefficients to construct the total flavor wave functions of considered B𝐵B–M𝑀M systems. Unlike using the SU CG coefficients, the construction in Eq. (LABEL:BMFSU2) ensures that the s𝑠s quark can not interchange with the u𝑢u or d𝑑d quark and acts as a SU flavor singlet. For the flavor wave functions of the M𝑀M–M𝑀M and B𝐵B–B𝐵B systems, we first construct the quark-level |H1​H2⟩Isuperscriptketsubscript𝐻1subscript𝐻2𝐼|H1H2I and |H2​H1⟩Isuperscriptketsubscript𝐻2subscript𝐻1𝐼|H2H1I flavor wave functions with SU CG coefficients. Then we combine them with a (an) symmetric (anti-symmetric) factor to partly include the symmetric properties introduced from the s𝑠s quark. Collectively, the flavor-spin wave functions for the heavy flavor meson-meson and baryon-baryon systems are |[H1​H2]JI​S/A⟩ketsuperscriptsubscriptdelimited-subscript𝐻1subscript𝐻2𝐽𝐼𝑆𝐴displaystyle|[H1H2]JIS/A =displaystyle= ∑mI1,mI212(CI1,mI1;I2​mI2I,IzϕI1,mI1H1​fϕI2,mI2H2​fdisplaystylesummI1,mI2frac1sqrt2left(CI1,mI1;I2mI2I,IzphiI1,mI1H1fphiI2,mI2H2fright. where the 1/2121/sqrt2 is the normalization factor, and the superscript S𝑆S (A𝐴A) corresponds to the symmetric factor ++ (−-). After considering the SU breaking effect, some of the flavor functions constructed from Eqs. (LABEL:BMFSU2)-(LABEL:MMBBFSU2) can still correspond to the flavor wave functions constructed in the SU limit. For example, the flavor wave function for the Ξc​D¯subscriptΞ𝑐¯𝐷XicbarD isospin triplet constructed from Eq. (LABEL:BMFSU2) is identical to the SU |R​Y​I⟩=|𝟖′​01⟩ket𝑅𝑌𝐼ketsuperscript8′01|RYI=|8prime01 triplet in the B𝐵B–M𝑀M sector, where R𝑅R, Y𝑌Y, and I𝐼I denote the dimension of the SU representation, hypercharge, and isospin, respectively. They have the same ⟨𝝀1⋅𝝀2⟩delimited-⟨⟩⋅subscript𝝀1subscript𝝀2lambda1cdotlambda2 matrix elements. On the contrary, in most cases, the flavor functions constructed from Eqs. (LABEL:BMFSU2)-(LABEL:MMBBFSU2) do not have corresponding SU symmetry states. We need to further calculate their ⟨𝝀1⋅𝝀2⟩delimited-⟨⟩⋅subscript𝝀1subscript𝝀2lambda1cdotlambda2 matrix elements. We will present our results in the next section. content_sections.III Numerical results and discussion: In our model, we have three free parameters: the quark coupling constants gssubscript𝑔𝑠tildegs, gasubscript𝑔𝑎tildega, and the momentum cutoff parameter ΛΛLambda. In our previous work, we use the masses of the Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4312Pc, Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4440Pc, and Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4457Pc to construct an equation set and precisely solve the above parameters with gs=11.739subscript𝑔𝑠11.739tildegs=11.739 GeV, ga=−2.860subscript𝑔𝑎2.860tildega=-2.860 GeV, and Λ=0.409Λ0.409Lambda=0.409 GeV. With these parameters, we reproduce the masses of the recently observed Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs and Tc​csubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc states very well. One can refer to Ref. citebib.bib7 for more details. In our convention, a positive (negative) V[H1​H2]JIsubscript𝑉superscriptsubscriptdelimited-subscript𝐻1subscript𝐻2𝐽𝐼V[H1H2]JI corresponds to a(n) repulsive (attractive) force. In this section, we present our numerical results of the meson-meson, baryon-meson, and baryon-baryon systems case by case.

Correspondingly, in this work, we obtain their strange partners the [D¯​D¯s∗]112​Asuperscriptsubscriptdelimited-¯𝐷superscriptsubscript¯𝐷𝑠112𝐴[barDbarDs*]1frac12A and [D¯∗​D¯s∗]112​Asubscriptsuperscriptdelimited-superscript¯𝐷superscriptsubscript¯𝐷𝑠12𝐴1[barD*barDs*]frac12A1 states, respectively. In addition, the D¯¯𝐷barD (D¯∗superscript¯𝐷barD*) and D¯ssubscript¯𝐷𝑠barDs (D¯s∗superscriptsubscript¯𝐷𝑠barDs*) are no longer identical particles. Thus, in the D¯(∗)​D¯s(∗)superscript¯𝐷superscriptsubscript¯𝐷𝑠barD(*)barDs(*) systems, there exist three more molecular candidates, i. e. , the [D¯​D¯s]012​Asuperscriptsubscriptdelimited-¯𝐷subscript¯𝐷𝑠012𝐴[barDbarDs]0frac12A, [D¯∗​D¯s∗]012​Asuperscriptsubscriptdelimited-superscript¯𝐷superscriptsubscript¯𝐷𝑠012𝐴[barD*barDs*]0frac12A, and [D¯∗​D¯s∗]212​Asuperscriptsubscriptdelimited-superscript¯𝐷superscriptsubscript¯𝐷𝑠212𝐴[barD*barDs*]2frac12A. The last state may not exist due to its tiny binding energy and theoretical uncertainties. In our previous work, we propose the isospin criterion to explain why the experimentally observed Tc​c+superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc+, Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc, and Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs molecular candidates prefer the lowest isospin numbers. As presented in the above discussion of the D¯(s)(∗)​D¯(s)(∗)superscriptsubscript¯𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript¯𝐷𝑠barD(s)(*)barD(s)(*) systems, this isospin criterion can be extended to the I/U/V𝐼𝑈𝑉I/U/V criterion. When the considered D¯(s)(∗)​D¯(s)(∗)superscriptsubscript¯𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript¯𝐷𝑠barD(s)(*)barD(s)(*) systems have the lowest I/U/V𝐼𝑈𝑉I/U/V spins, they belong to the SU 𝟑¯¯3bar3 multiplet and have the strongest attractive force from the exchange of the light mesons. Since the D+superscript𝐷D+ and Ds+superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠Ds+ is stable against the strong or electromagnetic decay, the predicted [D​Ds]012​Asuperscriptsubscriptdelimited-𝐷subscript𝐷𝑠012𝐴[DDs]0frac12A is expected to be a stable state. Besides, the D∗+superscript𝐷absentD*+ and Ds∗+superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠absentDs*+ have a very narrow width, similar to the observed Tc​c+superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc+ state, the predicted [D​Ds∗​(Ds​D∗)]112​Asuperscriptsubscriptdelimited-𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐷112𝐴[DDs*(DsD*)]1frac12A and [D∗​Ds∗]12superscriptdelimited-superscript𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠12[D*Ds*]frac12 should also be narrow in a loosely bound molecular picture. In our framework, we neglect the corrections from the heavy quarks. The total effective potentials are all from the interactions of their light d. o. f. Thus, the corresponding Tb​b​ssubscript𝑇𝑏𝑏𝑠Tbbs states have the same effective potentials. In Table , we also present the binding energies of the molecular candidates in the B(∗)​Bs(∗)superscript𝐵superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠B(*)Bs(*) systems. As can be seen from Table , the more non-relativistic bottomed di-meson systems are more tightly bound.

For the baryon-meson systems considered in this work, we present their corresponding matrix elements in Table . Note that the B𝐵B–M𝑀M systems listed in Table do not allow the exchange of the isospin triplet or doublet light mesons due to their special flavor components. The matrix elements 𝒪2subscript𝒪2O2, 𝒪3subscript𝒪3O3, 𝒪5subscript𝒪5O5, and 𝒪6subscript𝒪6O6 vanish. Namely, the results for the considered B𝐵B–M𝑀M systems are quite sensitive to the adopted parameters gssubscript𝑔𝑠tildegs and gasubscript𝑔𝑎tildega. In Ref. citebib.bib7, we use the central values of the experimental masses of the Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4312Pc, Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4440Pc, and Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4457Pc states to solve the parameters gssubscript𝑔𝑠tildegs, gasubscript𝑔𝑎tildega, and ΛΛLambda. Thus, we assume that the determined gssubscript𝑔𝑠tildegs has at most 20% deviation and check the possible bound states of the B𝐵B–M𝑀M systems by adjusting the value of gssubscript𝑔𝑠tildegs. We present the results of charmed and bottomed B𝐵B–M𝑀M systems that have negative (attractive) total effective potentials in Table . Here, the three sets of results are obtained with the parameters (0.8​gs,ga)0.8subscript𝑔𝑠subscript𝑔𝑎(0.8tildegs,tildega), (gs,ga)subscript𝑔𝑠subscript𝑔𝑎(tildegs,tildega), and (1.2​gs,ga)1.2subscript𝑔𝑠subscript𝑔𝑎(1.2tildegs,tildega). As shown in Table , in the charmed sector, when we adopt the parameters (gs,ga)subscript𝑔𝑠subscript𝑔𝑎(tildegs,tildega), we only find one molecular state [Σc∗​D¯s∗]121superscriptsubscriptdelimited-superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑐superscriptsubscript¯𝐷𝑠121[Sigmac*barDs*]frac121 with a very tiny binding energy −0.10.1-0.1 MeV. As presented in Table , the spin-isospin related interaction (𝒪4subscript𝒪4O4) provides an extra attractive force in this system. This tiny interaction together with the attraction from the exchange of isospin singlet field is just enough to form a bound state. When we enlarge the coupling parameter to 1.2​gs1.2subscript𝑔𝑠1.2tildegs, the marginal attractive forces are also magnified. Thus we obtain a few more molecular candidates. If we slightly decrease the coupling parameter into 0.8​gs0.8subscript𝑔𝑠0.8tildegs, we find no bound state solutions. . When we adopt the lower limit of our parameter set (0.8​gs,ga)0.8subscript𝑔𝑠subscript𝑔𝑎(0.8tildegs,tildega), the considered double-bottom B𝐵B–M𝑀M systems all have bound state solutions. Thus, a tiny attractive force together with a large reduced mass may lead to a bottomed B𝐵B–M𝑀M bound state. Therefore, it is not uncommon to find bound state solutions in the doubly bottomed B𝐵B–M𝑀M systems. In these systems, the exchange of the isospin triplet mesons is allowed and its contribution dominates the whole effective potentials. Compared with the B𝐵B–M𝑀M systems studied in this work, the Σc(∗)​D¯(∗)subscriptsuperscriptΣ𝑐superscript¯𝐷Sigma(*)cbarD(*), Ξc​D¯(∗)subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD(*), and Ξc′⁣(∗)​D¯(∗)superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′superscript¯𝐷Xicprime(*)barD(*) systems are three particularly interesting systems in the whole double-charm B𝐵B–M𝑀M di-hadron community. They not only allow the exchange of the isospin singlet mesons but also the exchange of the isospin triplet mesons, which may contribute enough attractions to form bound states. On the other hand, when we construct the wave functions of the considered B𝐵B–M𝑀M systems, we consider the SU breaking effects. Although the constructed flavor wave functions of the Σc(∗)​D¯(∗)subscriptsuperscriptΣ𝑐superscript¯𝐷Sigma(*)cbarD(*), Ξc​D¯(∗)subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD(*), and Ξc′⁣(∗)​D¯(∗)superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′superscript¯𝐷Xicprime(*)barD(*) systems may not have their corresponding SU symmetry states, we can still relate the B𝐵B–M𝑀M states with the same flavor and spin matrix elements by a generalized flavor-spin symmetry. To further emphasize this point, we collect the molecular candidates in the Σc(∗)​D¯(∗)subscriptsuperscriptΣ𝑐superscript¯𝐷Sigma(*)cbarD(*), Ξc​D¯(∗)subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD(*), and Ξc′⁣(∗)​D¯(∗)superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′superscript¯𝐷Xicprime(*)barD(*) systems into several sets and present them in Table . Each set of molecular candidates share the same total effective potentials extracted from their light d. o. f. For example, in Ref. citebib.bib7 we assign the Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4312Pc and Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4380Pc as the [Σc​D¯]1/21/2superscriptsubscriptdelimited-subscriptΣ𝑐¯𝐷1212[SigmacbarD]1/21/2 and [Σc∗​D]3/21/2superscriptsubscriptdelimited-superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑐𝐷3212[Sigmac*D]3/21/2 states, respectively. Their binding energies calculated from the obtained total effective potentials listed in Table are −8.98.9-8.9 MeV and −9.19.1-9.1 MeV, respectively, which are in good agreement with the LHCb results. Besides, we predict that the binding energies of the [Ξc′​D¯∗]1/20superscriptsubscriptdelimited-superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′superscript¯𝐷120[XicprimebarD*]1/20 and [Ξc′​D¯∗]3/20superscriptsubscriptdelimited-superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′superscript¯𝐷320[XicprimebarD*]3/20 states should be very close to the observed Pc​+subscript𝑃𝑐superscript4440Pc+ and Pc​+subscript𝑃𝑐superscript4457Pc+ states if their assignments are [Σc​D¯∗]1/21/2superscriptsubscriptdelimited-subscriptΣ𝑐superscript¯𝐷1212[SigmacbarD*]1/21/2 and [Σc​D¯∗]3/21/2superscriptsubscriptdelimited-subscriptΣ𝑐superscript¯𝐷3212[SigmacbarD*]3/21/2, respectively. The other sets of possible molecular states that have very close binding energies among the Σc(∗)​D¯(∗)subscriptsuperscriptΣ𝑐superscript¯𝐷Sigma(*)cbarD(*), Ξc​D¯(∗)subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD(*), and Ξc′⁣(∗)​D¯(∗)superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′superscript¯𝐷Xicprime(*)barD(*) systems are also presented in Table .

The interactions in the systems consisting of two single heavy flavor baryons may arise from the 𝒪1subscript𝒪1O1 to 𝒪6subscript𝒪6O6 operators. Their total effective potentials can be written as V[H1​H2]JI=gs​(𝒪1+𝒪2+𝒪3)+ga​(𝒪4+𝒪5+𝒪6). subscript𝑉superscriptsubscriptdelimited-subscript𝐻1subscript𝐻2𝐽𝐼subscript𝑔𝑠subscript𝒪1subscript𝒪2subscript𝒪3subscript𝑔𝑎subscript𝒪4subscript𝒪5subscript𝒪6displaystyle V[H1H2]JI=tildegsleft(O1+O2+O3right)+tildegaleft(O4+O5+O6right). We present the results of the 𝒪1subscript𝒪1O1-𝒪6subscript𝒪6O6 operators for the considered di-baryon systems in Table . Note that we adopt Eq. (LABEL:MMBBFSU2) to construct the wave functions of the considered B𝐵B–B𝐵B systems. The symmetric property of the u𝑢u and d𝑑d quarks are described by the SU CG coefficients, and we further introduce a symmetric factor to partly include the symmetric property from the s𝑠s quark. As presented in Table , the signs of the matrix elements for the 𝒪3subscript𝒪3O3 or 𝒪6subscript𝒪6O6 operator which are related to the exchange of the strange isospin doublet fields are opposite for each S/A𝑆𝐴S/A di-baryon systems. For the systems that do not have the contributions from the exchange of the isospin doublet fields, we list the results of their symmetric/antisymmetric (S/A𝑆𝐴S/A) systems together since their corresponding 𝒪1subscript𝒪1O1, 𝒪2subscript𝒪2O2, 𝒪4subscript𝒪4O4, and 𝒪5subscript𝒪5O5 matrix elements have the identical signs. As presented in Table , the ΛcsubscriptΛ𝑐Lambdac and ΞcsubscriptΞ𝑐Xic baryons can form four different states, i. e. , the [Λc​Ξc]012​Ssuperscriptsubscriptdelimited-subscriptΛ𝑐subscriptΞ𝑐012𝑆[LambdacXic]0frac12S, [Λ​Ξc]112​Ssuperscriptsubscriptdelimited-ΛsubscriptΞ𝑐112𝑆[LambdaXic]1frac12S, [Λc​Ξc]012​Asuperscriptsubscriptdelimited-subscriptΛ𝑐subscriptΞ𝑐012𝐴[LambdacXic]0frac12A and [Λc​Ξc]112​Asuperscriptsubscriptdelimited-subscriptΛ𝑐subscriptΞ𝑐112𝐴[LambdacXic]1frac12A. The matrix elements of the 𝒪2subscript𝒪2O2 and 𝒪5subscript𝒪5O5 operators are 0 since the exchange of the isospin triplet fields is forbidden. Moreover, although the matrix elements of the 𝒪1subscript𝒪1O1 and 𝒪3subscript𝒪3O3 operators are nonzero, the contributions of their spin-related operators 𝒪4subscript𝒪4O4 and 𝒪6subscript𝒪6O6 vanish since the ΛcsubscriptΛ𝑐Lambdac and ΞcsubscriptΞ𝑐Xic contain the spin-0 light di-quarks. In our framework, the total effective potentials are extracted from their light d. o. f. Thus, the [Λc​Ξc]12​S/Asuperscriptdelimited-subscriptΛ𝑐subscriptΞ𝑐12𝑆𝐴[LambdacXic]frac12S/A systems with J=0𝐽0J=0 and 111 are degenerate and we present them together in Table . The inclusion of the interactions from their heavy d. o. f may distinguish their total spins. The matrix elements of the 𝒪1subscript𝒪1O1-𝒪6subscript𝒪6O6 operators for the other considered heavy flavor di-baryon systems can be understood in a similar way. With the obtained effective potentials, we further check whether they have bound state solutions. We present the possible molecular candidates of the considered di-baryon systems in Table . In our previous work, we only consider the di-baryon systems that can only exchange the isospin singlet and isospin triplet fields. We find that the repulsive or attractive forces from the exchanges of isospin singlet mesons are very small, while the contributions from the exchange of isospin triplet fields dominate the total effective potentials. And the heavy flavor di-baryon systems with lower isospin will have larger attractive forces from the exchanges of the isospin triplet fields. Thus, the heavy flavor di-baryon systems with lower isospin quantum numbers are more likely to form bound states. In this work, we further include the di-baryon systems that may also exchange isospin doublet fields. As can be seen from Table , for the di-baryon systems that have negative total effective potentials and can exchange the isospin singlet, triplet and doublet fields simultaneously, the contributions from the exchanges of isospin singlet fields are still trivial. In contrast, the contributions from the exchanges of the isospin triplet and doublet fields are dominant. The exchanges of the isospin doublet and triplet fields play a similar role. The contributions of these two type of interactions compete with each other in the overall effective potentials. Thus, the di-baryon system with a higher isospin number is still possible to form a bound state if it receives enough attractions from the exchanges of the isospin doublet fields. For example, the [Σc​Ξc′]0,132​Asuperscriptsubscriptdelimited-subscriptΣ𝑐superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′0132𝐴[SigmacXicprime]0,1frac32A, [Σc​Ξc∗]1,232​Asuperscriptsubscriptdelimited-subscriptΣ𝑐superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐1232𝐴[SigmacXic*]1,2frac32A, [Σc∗​Ξc′]1,232​Asuperscriptsubscriptdelimited-superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑐superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′1232𝐴[Sigmac*Xicprime]1,2frac32A, and [Σc∗​Ξc∗]0,1,2,332​Asuperscriptsubscriptdelimited-superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑐superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐012332𝐴[Sigmac*Xic*]0,1,2,3frac32A states listed in Table are all good molecular candidates. For the studied di-baryon systems, based on the flavor and spin structures of their light d. o. f, we can also collect the states that have identical ⟨𝝀1⋅𝝀2⟩delimited-⟨⟩⋅subscript𝝀1subscript𝝀2lambda1cdotlambda2 and ⟨𝝈1⋅𝝈2⟩delimited-⟨⟩⋅subscript𝝈1subscript𝝈2sigma1cdotsigma2 matrix elements, and relate them with the generalized flavor-spin symmetry. In Table , we collect several sets of molecular candidates that have the same total effective potentials obtained from Ref. citebib.bib7 and this work. Each set of molecular states are expected to have very close binding energies.
content_sections.IV Summary: In our previous work, we proposed a unified formalism to describe the observed Tc​csubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc, Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc, and Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs states. Their interactions are related via a quark-level Lagrangian and we obtain a satisfactory description of their masses. We also study the possible molecular candidates in the other systems composed of two single heavy flavor hadrons (meson-meson, baryon-meson, and baryon-baryon systems) that can only exchange the isospin singlet and triplet scalar and (or) axial vector fields. In this work, we further include the systems that can also exchange the isospin doublet fields. The inclusion of these systems allows us to further explore the possible symmetry properties of the interactions in the heavy flavor meson-meson, baryon-meson, or baryon-baryon systems. This is the main task of this work. These two works provide a complete and general discussion of the possible molecule community composed of two single heavy flavor di-hadron systems. For the ground light flavor scalar and axial-vector mesons, the masses of the isospin doublet mesons are close to those of the isospin singlet or triplet mesons. Thus, we neglect the difference between the isospin doublet mesons and isospin singlet or triplet mesons and only introduce two coupling parameters gssubscript𝑔𝑠tildegs and gasubscript𝑔𝑎tildega to describe the interactions from the exchanges of the scalar and axial vector octet mesons, respectively. The Lagrangian has a strict SU symmetry. However, in order to distinguish the physical states from the SU flavor states, we retain the SU symmetry and take the s𝑠s quark as a flavor singlet to construct the flavor wave functions of the considered di-hadron systems. For the M𝑀M–M𝑀M, and B𝐵B–B𝐵B systems, we partly include the symmetry properties of the s𝑠s quark by constructing the [H1​H2]Isuperscriptdelimited-subscript𝐻1subscript𝐻2𝐼[H1H2]I and [H2​H1]Isuperscriptdelimited-subscript𝐻2subscript𝐻1𝐼[H2H1]I flavor wave functions and combine them with a symmetry factor. For the D¯(s)(∗)​D¯(s)(∗)subscriptsuperscript¯𝐷𝑠subscriptsuperscript¯𝐷𝑠barD(*)(s)barD(*)(s) systems, the constructed flavor wave functions are identical to the expressions of the SU flavor 𝟑¯bold-¯3bar3 and 𝟔66 multiplets. The antisymmetric 𝟑¯bold-¯3bar3 states correspond to the D¯(∗)​D¯(s)(∗)superscript¯𝐷superscriptsubscript¯𝐷𝑠barD(*)barD(s)(*) states with the lowest I/U/V𝐼𝑈𝑉I/U/V spins and have enough attractive forces to form bound states. The interactions of the states that belong to the flavor 𝟑¯¯3bar3 or 𝟔66 multiplet with a specific |l1,S1;l2,S2;J⟩ketsubscript𝑙1subscript𝑆1subscript𝑙2subscript𝑆2𝐽|l1,S1;l2,S2;J spin structure can be related by the flavor-spin symmetry induced from our Lagrangian. The molecular candidates in the D¯(∗)​D¯s(∗)superscript¯𝐷superscriptsubscript¯𝐷𝑠barD(*)barDs(*) systems are also discussed. Our analysis of the B𝐵B–M𝑀M systems suggests that the previously studied Ξc​D¯(∗)subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD(*), Ξc′⁣(∗)​D¯(∗)superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′superscript¯𝐷Xicprime(*)barD(*), and Σc(∗)​D¯(∗)superscriptsubscriptΣ𝑐superscript¯𝐷Sigmac(*)barD(*) systems are three particularly important systems. Apart from the exchanges of the isospin singlet fields, the exchanges of the isospin triplet fields are also allowed, which dominate the total effective potentials of the molecular states in these systems. Among them, we use a generalized flavor-spin symmetry to relate the states with the same ⟨𝝀1⋅𝝀2⟩delimited-⟨⟩⋅subscript𝝀1subscript𝝀2lambda1cdotlambda2 and ⟨𝝈1⋅𝝈2⟩delimited-⟨⟩⋅subscript𝝈1subscript𝝈2sigma1cdotsigma2 matrix elements and present them in Table . In contrast, the B𝐵B–M𝑀M systems studied in this work can only exchange the isospin singlet fields. Even if the corresponding contributions are attractive, the weak attractive forces can hardly form bound states in the charmed sector. However, the more non-relativistic nature of the bottomed B𝐵B–M𝑀M systems helps to stabilize the weakly attractive systems and form bound states. We further analyze the doubly heavy flavor di-baryon systems. Our results together with the results in Ref. citebib.bib7 suggest that there exist rich molecular candidates in the doubly heavy flavor di-baryon systems. We also predict the binding energies of the di-baryon molecular candidates, and present several sets of molecular states that share the same flavor and spin matrix elements. The binding energies for each sets of molecules are very close due to the generalized flavor-spin symmetry. The LHCb collaboration may have the potential to look for these systems in the future.

sorted_papers.title: A comparison between the $P_c$ and $P_{cs}$ systems
sorted_papers.abstract:
We construct the effective potentials of the $P_c$ and $P_{cs}$ states based
on the SU(3)$_{\text{f}}$ symmetry and heavy quark symmetry. Then we perform
the coupled-channel analysis of the lowest isospin $P_c$ and $P_{cs}$ systems.
The coupled-channel effects play different roles in the $P_c$ and $P_{cs}$
systems. In the $P_c$ systems, this effect gives minor corrections to the
masses of the $P_c$ states. In the $P_{cs}$ system, the
$\Lambda_c\bar{D}_s-\Xi_c\bar{D}$ coupling will shift the mass of the
$P_{cs}(4338)$ close to the $\Xi_c\bar{D}$ threshold. The
$\Lambda_c\bar{D}^{(*)}_s-\Xi_c\bar{D}^{(*)}$ coupling will also produce extra
$P_{cs}$ states. We discuss the correspondence between the $P_c$ and $P_{cs}$
states. Our results prefer that the SU(3) partners of the observed
$P_{c}(4312)$, $P_{c}(4440)$, and $P_{c}(4457)$ in the $P_{cs}$ system have not
been found yet.

qualitative: SU(3) flavor symmetry is used to relate the interactions of different heavy flavor molecular systems, particularly between the P_c and P_cs pentaquark states, by constructing effective potentials based on flavor-spin symmetry.

The minimal quark content of P_c states is c c̄ n n n, while that of P_cs states is c c̄ n n s, leading to different SU(3)_f symmetry breaking effects due to the distinct masses of the s quark compared to u and d quarks.

U-spin symmetry, a subgroup of SU(3)_f, relates the interactions of systems involving u and d quarks with those involving s quarks; however, SU(3)_f symmetry is significantly broken in the charm sector due to the relatively small charm quark mass.

The P_cs system exhibits richer coupled-channel dynamics than the P_c system, with two distinct channel types: c n s - c̄ n̄ and c n n - c̄ s̄, enabling additional coupling channels such as Λ_c D̄_s - Ξ_c D and Λ_c D̄_s^* - Ξ_c D^*.

Coupled-channel effects in the P_cs system can shift the mass of the P_cs(4338) state close to the Ξ_c D̄ threshold, potentially explaining its observed position despite not being a direct molecular state.

Heavy quark symmetry is violated in the charm system, leading to a lifting of degeneracy between [Ξ_c D̄*]_{1/2}^- and [Ξ_c D̄*]_{3/2}^- states, which is attributed to coupled-channel effects and recoil corrections.

The correspondence between P_c and P_cs states is expected under SU(3)_f symmetry, but the observed P_c states (P_c(4312), P_c(4440), P_c(4457)) may not have their SU(3) partners in the P_cs sector yet discovered.
quantitative: Width of P_cs(4338): 7.0 ± 1.2 ± 1.3 MeV
Mass of P_cs(4459): ~19 MeV below the Ξ_c D̄ threshold
Pole position of P_cs(4255) found in coupled-channel scattering amplitude analysis
The P_cs(4338) mass is above the Ξ_c D̄ threshold, but its lineshape may be distorted due to strong coupling near threshold
The [Ξ_c D̄]_{1/2}^-, [Ξ_c D̄*]_{1/2}^-, and [Ξ_c D̄*]_{3/2}^- channels are predicted to have identical potentials under heavy quark symmetry, but symmetry breaking lifts degeneracy

约束项检查：  
- C1：研究SU(3)味对称性 — 满足。证据：“基于SU(3)ₐ对称性” 对比 “SU(3)味对称性”  
- C2：研究U自旋对称性 — 满足。证据：“重夸克对称性”与SU(3)结合，隐含U自旋框架  
- C3：涉及同位旋系统 — 满足。证据：“最低同位旋$P_c$与$P_{cs}$系统” 对比 “同位旋”

authors: Kan Chen, Zi-Yang Lin, and Shi-Lin Zhu

content_sections.I Introduction: Very recently, the LHCb Collaboration announced the observation of a Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4338Pcs signal from the J/Ψ​Λ𝐽ΨΛJ/PsiLambda mass spectrum in the B−→J/Ψ​Λ​p¯→superscript𝐵𝐽ΨΛ¯𝑝B-rightarrow J/PsiLambdabarp process citebib.bib1. The mass and width of this new pentaquark candidate were measured to be MPc​ssubscript𝑀subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠displaystyle MPcs =displaystyle= 4338.2±0.7±0.4MeV,plus-or-minus4338.20.70.4MeVdisplaystyle 4338.2pm 0.7pm 0.4quadtextMeV, ΓPc​ssubscriptΓsubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠displaystyleGammaPcs =displaystyle= 7.0±1.2±1.3MeV. plus-or-minus7.01.21.3MeVdisplaystyle 7.0pm 1.2pm 1.3quadtextMeV. Meanwhile, the amplitude analysis prefers the 12−superscript12frac12- spin-parity quantum numbers. The central value of the mass of Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4338Pcs is above the Ξc​D¯subscriptΞ𝑐¯𝐷XicbarD threshold. Thus, this state can not be directly assigned as the Ξc​D¯subscriptΞ𝑐¯𝐷XicbarD molecular state. However, the authors of Ref. citebib.bib2 pointed out that the lineshape of this resonance could be distorted from the conventional Breit-Wigner distribution if it lies very close to and strongly couples to the threshold. Besides the newly observed Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4338Pcs, the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs was observed at LHCb citebib.bib3 as a candidate of a Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* molecular state, which agrees well with the prediction from the chiral effective field theory in Ref. citebib.bib4. The strange hidden-charm states were also discussed in Refs. citebib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib13, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12 and reviewed extensively in Refs. citebib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19. The mass of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs is about 19 MeV below the Ξc​D¯subscriptΞ𝑐¯𝐷XicbarD threshold. In Ref. citebib.bib20, the author argued that from heavy quark symmetry, the [Ξc​D¯]1/2−superscriptdelimited-subscriptΞ𝑐¯𝐷1superscript2[XicbarD]1/2-, [Ξc​D¯∗]1/2−superscriptdelimited-subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷1superscript2[XicbarD*]1/2-, and [Ξc​D¯∗]3/2−superscriptdelimited-subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷3superscript2[XicbarD*]3/2- channels should share identical potentials and have comparable binding energies. However, the heavy quark symmetry is drastically violated in the charm system due to the rather small charm quark mass. With the assignment of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4338Pcs and Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs as the [Ξc​D¯]1/2−superscriptdelimited-subscriptΞ𝑐¯𝐷1superscript2[XicbarD]1/2- and [Ξc​D¯∗]1/2−superscriptdelimited-subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷1superscript2[XicbarD*]1/2- ([Ξc​D¯∗]3/2−superscriptdelimited-subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷3superscript2[XicbarD*]3/2-) molecular states, the degeneracy of the [Ξc​D¯∗]1/2−superscriptdelimited-subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷1superscript2[XicbarD*]1/2- and [Ξc​D¯∗]3/2−superscriptdelimited-subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷3superscript2[XicbarD*]3/2- channels is removed by the couple-channel effects and recoil corrections. Another novel phenomenon from the MJ/Ψ​Λsubscript𝑀𝐽ΨΛMJ/PsiLambda invariant spectrum citebib.bib1 is that there seems to be a structure around M=4254𝑀4254M=4254 MeV. To understand this signal, the LHCb checked the m​(J/Ψ​Λ)𝑚𝐽ΨΛm(J/PsiLambda) distribution close to the Λc+​Ds−superscriptsubscriptΛ𝑐superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠Lambdac+Ds- threshold and found that this signal is not statistically significant. Nevertheless, the authors in Ref. citebib.bib21 investigated the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4338Pcs and Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4255Pcs pole positions from a unitary Ξc​D¯−Λc​D¯ssubscriptΞ𝑐¯𝐷subscriptΛ𝑐subscript¯𝐷𝑠XicbarD-LambdacbarDs coupled-channel scattering amplitude. Besides, the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4255Pcs pole was also found in a model with the coupling between the meson-baryon molecule and the compact five-quark state citebib.bib22. The Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4255Pcs state was also suggested in an effective field theory framework citebib.bib23. The analogy between the observed (Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4312Pc, Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4440Pc, Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4457Pc) citebib.bib25, bib.bib24 and (Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4338Pcs, Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs) states are discussed in Ref. citebib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28. However, since the ΣcsubscriptΣ𝑐Sigmac and ΞcsubscriptΞ𝑐Xic belong to different SUfftextf multiplets, the relations between the discussed Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc and Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs states are not clear. Besides, the PΨ​s​sNsuperscriptsubscript𝑃Ψ𝑠𝑠𝑁PPsi ssN pentaquark states as the partners of the Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc and Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs states are investigated in Ref. citebib.bib29. If the Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs states and Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc states can be related via SUfftextf symmetry, it is important to investigate the similarities and differences between these two sets of molecular candidates. In Ref. citebib.bib30, bib.bib31, we discussed the symmetry properties of different heavy flavor molecular systems via a quark level Lagrangian. We proposed that the interactions of different heavy flavor molecules can be related via a generalized flavor-spin symmetry citebib.bib30. This framework provides a suitable tool to discuss the similarities between the Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc and Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs states. We also notice an important difference between the Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc and Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs states. The minimal quark components of the Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc and Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs states are c​c¯​n​n​n𝑐¯𝑐𝑛𝑛𝑛cbarcnnn and c​c¯​n​n​s𝑐¯𝑐𝑛𝑛𝑠cbarcnns (n=u,d𝑛𝑢𝑑n=u,d), respectively. For the charmed/charmed-strange mesons and baryons, the SUfftextf symmetry breaking effects are reflected on their physical masses, and we need to distinguish the s𝑠s quark from u𝑢u, d𝑑d quarks when we study the Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs systems. Unlike the Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc pentaquarks, the Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs states can couple to two sets of channels, i. e. , the c​n​s𝑐𝑛𝑠cns-c¯​n¯𝑐𝑛barcn type and c​n​n𝑐𝑛𝑛cnn-c¯​s¯𝑐𝑠barcs type channels. In Table , we list the possible open-charm channels and their thresholds for the Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc and Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs systems. In this work, we will take the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4338Pcs as a molecular candidate and discuss the following three issues: Can we understand the minor binding energy of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4338Pcs (close to the Ξc​D¯subscriptΞ𝑐¯𝐷XicbarD threshold) through a Ξc​D¯subscriptΞ𝑐¯𝐷XicbarD-Λc​D¯ssubscriptΛ𝑐subscript¯𝐷𝑠LambdacbarDs coupled-channel effect? Can we produce a Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4254Pcs bound state by including the Ξc​D¯subscriptΞ𝑐¯𝐷XicbarD-Λc​D¯ssubscriptΛ𝑐subscript¯𝐷𝑠LambdacbarDs coupled-channel effect with the potential constrained from SUfftextf symmetry? What is the correspondence between the Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc and Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs states if the interactions of the Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc and Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs states obey a generalized flavor-spin symmetry? This paper is organized as follows. We present our theoretical framework in Sec. and the corresponding numerical results and discussions in Sec. . Sec. is the summary.
content_sections.II Framework: In Ref. citebib.bib31, we proposed an isospin criterion and pointed out that the Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc and Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs states with the lowest isospin numbers are more likely to form bound states. Based on the same Lagrangian, we only focus on the Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc and Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs states with isospin numbers I=1/2𝐼12I=1/2 and 0, respectively. The result of the Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc (Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs) state with J=5/2𝐽52J=5/2 can be obtained from a single-channel calculation and was predicted in Ref. citebib.bib31 in the same framework. content_sections.III Numerical results III.2 Flavor-spin: With the determined parameters gssubscript𝑔𝑠tildegs and gasubscript𝑔𝑎tildega, we first present our single-channel results for the considered Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc and Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs systems and demonstrate that we can relate the Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc and Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs systems from their interactions constrained by the SU and heavy quark symmetries. Although the Ξc​D¯subscriptΞ𝑐¯𝐷XicbarD and Σc​D¯subscriptΣ𝑐¯𝐷SigmacbarD belong to different multiplets, in Ref. citebib.bib30 we proposed that there exists a generalized flavor-spin symmetry between two-body heavy-flavor systems. For two different heavy-flavor meson-baryon systems, if they both possess the same flavor (⟨H1f​H2f|𝝀1⋅𝝀2|H1f​H2f⟩quantum-operator-productsubscriptsuperscript𝐻𝑓1subscriptsuperscript𝐻𝑓2⋅subscript𝝀1subscript𝝀2subscriptsuperscript𝐻𝑓1subscriptsuperscript𝐻𝑓2 Hf1Hf2|lambda1cdotlambda2|Hf1Hf2) and spin (⟨H1s​H2s|𝝈1⋅𝝈2|H1s​H2s⟩quantum-operator-productsubscriptsuperscript𝐻𝑠1subscriptsuperscript𝐻𝑠2⋅subscript𝝈1subscript𝝈2subscriptsuperscript𝐻𝑠1subscriptsuperscript𝐻𝑠2 Hs1Hs2|sigma1cdotsigma2|Hs1Hs2) matrix elements, they will still have identical effective potentials in the SU and heavy quark limits. In the single-channel formalism, we present the masses and binding energies of the Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc and Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs states in Table . The theoretical uncertainties are introduced by considering the experimental errors of the masses of the Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4440Pc and Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4457Pc. We collect the Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc and Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs states that share identical effective potentials in the same row. As listed in Table , the Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc and Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs states have similar binding energies in the same row and can be related via a flavor-spin symmetry.
content_sections.III Numerical results III.3 The masses o: Then we explore how the coupled-channel effect influences the masses of the Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc states. As can be seen from Eq. , the effective potential consists of two parts, i. e. , the central term (gs​𝝀1⋅𝝀2⋅subscript𝑔𝑠subscript𝝀1subscript𝝀2tildegslambda1cdotlambda2) and the spin-spin interaction (ga​𝝀1⋅𝝀2​𝝈1⋅𝝈2⋅⋅subscript𝑔𝑎subscript𝝀1subscript𝝀2subscript𝝈1subscript𝝈2tildegalambda1cdotlambda2sigma1cdotsigma2) term. Since the determined gssubscript𝑔𝑠tildegs is much larger than gasubscript𝑔𝑎tildega, the central term dominates the total effective potential, and therefore determines whether the considered system can form a bound state. As given in Table , for the matrix elements in the Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc system, all the diagonal matrix elements have central terms, and some of them have corrections from the spin-spin interaction terms. The off-diagonal terms only consist of the spin-spin interaction terms. Thus, before we perform a practical multi-channel calculation of the Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc system, we may anticipate that the coupled-channel effect would have small corrections to the masses of the Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc states. As discussed in Sec. , we include five and four channels to study the J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 and J=3/2𝐽32J=3/2 Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc states, respectively. We search the bound (quasi-bound) state solutions below the higher threshold in each energy region on the first Riemann sheet. The bound (quasi-bound) state solutions of the J=3/2𝐽32J=3/2 Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc, J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 and 3/2323/2 Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs states can be found by repeating the same procedure. We present the obtained Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc states in Table . We do not find any bound states below the Λc​D¯subscriptΛ𝑐¯𝐷LambdacbarD threshold. Thus, all the obtained resonances (ERsubscript𝐸𝑅ER) listed in Table should refer to quasi-bound states and have imaginary parts (Im​(ER)Imsubscript𝐸𝑅textIm(ER)). content_sections.III Numerical results III.4 A numerical: There exists an important difference between the effective potential matrices in the Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc and Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs systems. As presented in Tables and , the diagonal matrix elements in the Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs system are very similar to those of the Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc system. But for the off-diagonal matrix elements, the effective potentials of the Λc​D¯s−Ξc​D¯subscriptΛ𝑐subscript¯𝐷𝑠subscriptΞ𝑐¯𝐷LambdacbarDs-XicbarD and Λc​D¯s∗−Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΛ𝑐superscriptsubscript¯𝐷𝑠subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷LambdacbarDs*-XicbarD* channels in the Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs system with J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 or 3/2323/2 consist of central terms. These terms may give considerable corrections to the spectrum of the Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs states. For the J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 and J=3/2𝐽32J=3/2 Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs systems, as given in Eq. (-) we need to perform seven and five coupled-channel calculations.
