authors: Zhou Rui, Jia-Ming Li, Chao-Qi Zhang

content_sections.I Introduction: Isospin symmetry is an approximate symmetry in the Standard model (SM) which has been widely used in the phenomenological analysis of heavy quark decays. For example, the amplitudes for the family of B→K​π→𝐵𝐾𝜋Brightarrow Kpi decays are expected to obey a quadrilateral relation imposed by isospin symmetry citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4, which allow us to constrain the SM parameters, and to look for signs of new physics (NP). Based on isospin arguments, the difference between the direct C​P𝐶𝑃CP asymmetries for the modes B+→K+​π0→superscript𝐵superscript𝐾superscript𝜋0B+rightarrow K+pi0 and B0→K+​π−→superscript𝐵0superscript𝐾superscript𝜋B0rightarrow K+pi- are expected to be zero at the leading order in the SMcitebib.bib5, as the two decays differ only by the spectator quark. Nevertheless, a nonzero C​P𝐶𝑃CP asymmetry difference with a significance of more than six standard deviations (σ𝜎sigma) was observed in the latest measurements of LHCbcitebib.bib6, suggests a violation of the strong isospin symmetry may beyond the SM expectation, which was referred to as the longstanding “K​π𝐾𝜋Kpi puzzle”citebib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11. To resolve above puzzle, a sizeable electroweak (EW) penguin contribution, offering an attractive avenue for new particles to enter, will be requestedcitebib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16. In the beauty-hadron decays aspect, the EW penguin contributions are usually overshadowed by the larger QCD penguins. For this reason, it is of particular interest to consider those purely isospin-violating decays, in which the isospin-conserving QCD penguin amplitudes do not contribute at all. The representative examples in hadronic B𝐵B meson decays are the B¯s→(η,η′,ϕ)​(π,ρ)→subscript¯𝐵𝑠𝜂superscript𝜂′italic-ϕ𝜋𝜌barBsrightarrow(eta,etaprime,phi)(pi,rho) modescitebib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, whose branching ratios were predicted to be relatively small in the SM, of order 10−8−10−7superscript108superscript10710-8-10-7 citebib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib26. The inclusion of NP effectscitebib.bib32, bib.bib31, bib.bib33, bib.bib34 in EW penguin greatly increases their rates by an order of magnitude with respect to the SM predictions, making these decays are interesting for LHCb and future B𝐵B factories searches. Up to now, only the 4​σ4𝜎4sigma evidence for Bs0→ϕ​ρ0→subscriptsuperscript𝐵0𝑠italic-ϕsuperscript𝜌0B0srightarrowphirho0 was seen by LHCb with a branching fraction of (2.7±0.8)×10−7plus-or-minus2.70.8superscript107(2.7pm 0.8)times 10-7citebib.bib35, which is compatible with its SM expectation albeit still with a significant uncertainty. This consistency leaves little room for NP contributions and motivates us to explore further information from other hadronic decays which are also sensitive to EW penguin contributions. In the b𝑏b-baryon sector, the similar promising processes are the Λb→Σ0​ϕ,Σ0​J/ψ→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΣ0italic-ϕsuperscriptΣ0𝐽𝜓LambdabrightarrowSigma0phi,Sigma0J/psi decays, which received less attention in the literature. Since ΛbsubscriptΛ𝑏Lambdab is an isospin singlet while the final state have total isospin I=1𝐼1I=1, these processes thus also fully break isospin symmetry. Their amplitudes receive contributions from the tree and EW penguin operators, which are isospin-violating. By contrast for the QCD penguin operators, which preserve isospin symmetry, do not affect these processes. In the SM, isospin violation arises from the quark mass difference md−musubscript𝑚𝑑subscript𝑚𝑢md-mu and from electromagnetic correctionscitebib.bib36. In this case, the QCD-penguin operators can contribute through the isospin-conserving amplitudes if the Σ−ΛΣΛSigma-Lambda mixing under strong isospin-symmetry breaking is permittedcitebib.bib37. The factorization approach (FA) and the diquark approximation failed in describing such purely isospin-violating decays because the Λb→Σ0→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΣ0LambdabrightarrowSigma0 transition is forbidden due to the orthogonality of the ΛbsubscriptΛ𝑏Lambdab and Σ0superscriptΣ0Sigma0 spin functions. They can only proceed via the exchange diagrams, thus the observations of them would provide valuable information on the role of exchange topologies in b𝑏b-baryon weak decays. This poses serious challenges to precise theoretical calculations based on factorization. However, FA is not necessary in the perturbative QCD (PQCD) approachcitebib.bib38. Except for the factorizable diagrams, one can valuate all relevant Feynman diagrams including the exchange topologies in a self-consistent manner. In PQCD factorizationcitebib.bib39, the decay amplitude for a exclusive process is obtained as a convolution of a perturbative kernel and the nonperturbative hadronic light-cone distribution amplitudes (LCDAs). After summing the large logarithms associated with parton transverse momenta, the PQCD formalism with Sudakov suppression can give converging results and have predictive power. Therefore, it can serve as a useful tool for investigating the heavy bottom decayscitebib.bib40, bib.bib41, bib.bib22, bib.bib42, bib.bib43, bib.bib44. Inspired by the success of the PQCD approach in various weak decays of ΛbsubscriptΛ𝑏Lambdab baryon citebib.bib45, bib.bib46, bib.bib47, bib.bib48, bib.bib49, bib.bib50, bib.bib51, bib.bib52, bib.bib53, bib.bib54, bib.bib55, in this work we shall perform a phenomenological analysis of the above two purely isospin-violating decays and explore the exchange topological contribution inside. The Σ−ΛΣΛSigma-Lambda mixing effects in the two reactions are also investigated. The rest of the paper is organized as follows. We briefly review the LCDAs, kinematics, and the Σ−ΛΣΛSigma-Lambda mixing phenomenon in Sec. . The numerical analysis including the results for the decay amplitudes, branching ratios and various asymmetry parameters are presented in Sec. . Section is reserved for a summary and conclusions. Finally, we give the explicit expressions of the factorization formulas in the Appendix.
content_sections.II Theoretical framework II.1 Light-cone: The LCDAs are primary nonperturbative quantities for calculating the heavy baryon decays based on the PQCD approach, which can be constructed via the nonlocal matrix elements. We now summarize the definitions of the LCDAs for the initial and final states. Heavy baryons containing a b𝑏b-quark can be regarded as a system of an effective heavy quark and a pair of light quarks with aligned helicities, called diquark in the heavy quark limit. The heavy quarks are nonrelativistic particles which decouple from the diquark in the leading order of the heavy quark mass expansion. The investigation of heavy-baryon distribution amplitudes has made brilliant progresscitebib.bib57, bib.bib58, bib.bib60, bib.bib61, bib.bib59, bib.bib56 since the complete classification and renormalization group equations (RGE) that govern the scale-dependence are presentedcitebib.bib57. Following Ref. citebib.bib58, the ΛbsubscriptΛ𝑏Lambdab baryon LCDAs up to twist-4 accuracy in the momentum space is defined by (ΨΛb)α​β​γ​(xi,μ)=18​Nc​fΛb​(μ)​[M1​(x2,x3)​γ5​CT]γ​β+fΛb​(μ)​[M2​(x2,x3)​γ5​CT]γ​β​[Λb​(p)]α,subscriptsubscriptΨsubscriptΛ𝑏𝛼𝛽𝛾subscript𝑥𝑖𝜇18subscript𝑁𝑐superscriptsubscript𝑓subscriptΛ𝑏1𝜇subscriptdelimited-subscript𝑀1subscript𝑥2subscript𝑥3subscript𝛾5superscript𝐶𝑇𝛾𝛽superscriptsubscript𝑓subscriptΛ𝑏2𝜇subscriptdelimited-subscript𝑀2subscript𝑥2subscript𝑥3subscript𝛾5superscript𝐶𝑇𝛾𝛽subscriptdelimited-subscriptΛ𝑏𝑝𝛼displaystyle(PsiLambdab)alphabetagamma(xi,mu)=frac18NcfLambdab(mu)[M1(x2,x3)gamma5CT]gammabeta+fLambdab(mu)[M2(x2,x3)gamma5CT]gammabeta[Lambdab(p)]alpha, where Λb​(p)subscriptΛ𝑏𝑝Lambdab(p) is the spinor of the ΛbsubscriptΛ𝑏Lambdab baryon with on-shell momentum p𝑝p and α,β,γ𝛼𝛽𝛾alpha,beta,gamma are Dirac indices. The variables x2,x3subscript𝑥2subscript𝑥3x2,x3 are the momentum fractions carried by the quarks u,d𝑢𝑑u,d, respectively. Ncsubscript𝑁𝑐Nc is the number of colors. C𝐶C is the charge conjugation matrix with the properties C​γμT​C−1=−γμ𝐶superscriptsubscript𝛾𝜇𝑇superscript𝐶1subscript𝛾𝜇CgammamuTC-1=-gammamu and C​γ5T​C−1=γ5𝐶superscriptsubscript𝛾5𝑇superscript𝐶1subscript𝛾5Cgamma5TC-1=gamma5 and the superscript T𝑇T indicates a transposition in the spinor space. The color antisymmetrization, which is needed to form a color singlet, is not written out explicitly but implied. The two couplings fΛb(1,2)superscriptsubscript𝑓subscriptΛ𝑏12fLambdab(1,2) are expected to be equal due to a nonrelativistic constituent quark picture of the ΛbsubscriptΛ𝑏Lambdabcitebib.bib57. The above definition indicates the daughter baryon momentum is along the n𝑛n direction in the massless limit. Several asymptotic models for the various twist LCDAs, in which the twist is indicated by the subscript numbers, have been proposed in Refs. citebib.bib57, bib.bib58, bib.bib56 and summarized in Ref. citebib.bib51. We adopt the exponential model, whose explicit expressions can be found in the previous workcitebib.bib51, bib.bib53, bib.bib54 and shall not be repeated here. The twist 2 and twist 4 LCDAs are symmetric under exchange of x2subscript𝑥2x2 and x3subscript𝑥3x3, while the two twist 3 ones do not have any definite symmetry but satisfy Ψ3+−​(x2,x3)=Ψ3−+​(x3,x2)superscriptsubscriptΨ3absentsubscript𝑥2subscript𝑥3superscriptsubscriptΨ3absentsubscript𝑥3subscript𝑥2Psi3+-(x2,x3)=Psi3-+(x3,x2) because of the isospin zero condition for the diquark. ΦVsuperscriptΦ𝑉PhiV, ΦAsuperscriptΦ𝐴PhiA and ΦTsuperscriptΦ𝑇PhiT are the vector, axial-vector and tensor structure LCDAs, respectively. Their explicit expressions have been studied using QCD sum rulescitebib.bib62, bib.bib63, bib.bib64, bib.bib65 and lattice QCDcitebib.bib66, bib.bib67, bib.bib68. Quite recently, the one-loop perturbative contributions to LCDA of a light baryon has been derived in large-momentum effective theorycitebib.bib69, which provides a first step to obtaining the LCDA from first principle lattice QCD calculations in the future. It can be seen that the LCDAs at the scale μ=1𝜇1mu=1 GeV differ greatly from their asymptotic forms. It is easy to observe that ΦVsuperscriptΦ𝑉PhiV and ΦTsuperscriptΦ𝑇PhiT are symmetric under permutation of u𝑢u and d𝑑d quarks, but ΦAsuperscriptΦ𝐴PhiA is antisymmetric under the same operation. content_sections.II Theoretical framework II.3 Σ−ΛΣΛ\\Si: Isospin symmetry can be broken either by electroweak effects or by the strong interaction through the up and down quark mass difference, which might lead to the quark flavor mixing. In other words, the observed physical states with definite mass are actually mixtures of the idealized isospin states. In the SM, Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0 and Σ0superscriptΣ0Sigma0 baryons are part of the baryon octet and share the same valence quarks (u​d​s)𝑢𝑑𝑠(uds). Their different isospin separates the former as a singlet and the latter as part of the triplet with two partners Σ±superscriptΣplus-or-minusSigmapm. The physical baryons are made of the mixing of isospin triplet and singlet states 1We use Σ0,Λ0superscriptΣ0superscriptΛ0Sigma0,Lambda0 to stand for the unmixed isospin states and Σ,ΛΣΛSigma,Lambda to denote the physical particle states. (ΛΣ)=(cos⁡θ−sin⁡θsin⁡θcos⁡θ)​(Λ0Σ0),ΛΣ𝜃𝜃𝜃𝜃superscriptΛ0superscriptΣ0displaystyleleft(beginarraycLambda Sigmaendarrayright)=left(beginarraycccostheta-sintheta sinthetacosthetaendarrayright)left(beginarraycLambda0 Sigma0endarrayright), with θ𝜃theta being the mixing angle. Since the mixing phenomenon in the Λ−ΣΛΣLambda-Sigma system was first proposed in the late 1970s, many fruitful theoretical works on the mixing angle have been made so far citebib.bib83, bib.bib84, bib.bib85, bib.bib86, bib.bib87, bib.bib88, bib.bib89, bib.bib91, bib.bib90, bib.bib92. Although the mixing angle has not yet been given experimentally, its value has been estimated to be in the range (0.55−1.2)∘superscript0.551.2(0.55-1.2)circcitebib.bib90, bib.bib91, bib.bib92, bib.bib93, bib.bib94. The smallness of the mixing angle indicates the degree of isospin-symmetry breaking is weak. In general, the initial states of ΛbsubscriptΛ𝑏Lambdab and ΣbsubscriptΣ𝑏Sigmab, corresponding to I=0𝐼0I=0 and I=1𝐼1I=1, are also mixed. However, the mixing receive an additional suppression from the bottom quark masscitebib.bib95. As a consequence, it is ignored in following analysis. Considering above mixing effect, the physical decay amplitude for the concerned processes can be written ascitebib.bib37 ⟨Σ​V|ℋe​f​f|Λb⟩≈θ​⟨Λ0​V|ℋ0|Λb⟩+⟨Σ0​V|ℋ1|Λb⟩,quantum-operator-productΣ𝑉subscriptℋ𝑒𝑓𝑓subscriptΛ𝑏𝜃quantum-operator-productsuperscriptΛ0𝑉subscriptℋ0subscriptΛ𝑏quantum-operator-productsuperscriptΣ0𝑉subscriptℋ1subscriptΛ𝑏displaystyleSigma V|Heff|LambdabapproxthetaLambda0V|H0|Lambdab+Sigma0V|H1|Lambdab, where ℋ0subscriptℋ0H0 and ℋ1subscriptℋ1H1 refer to isospin conserving and breaking Hamiltonian, respectively. It can be seen the isospin breaking effect affect not only the mixing of the states but also the amplitude. The first term on the right-hand side (rhs) of Eq. conserves isospin and hence receives contributions from both electroweak and QCD-penguin, but the second term belongs to an isospin-violating transition to which the QCD-penguin component does not participate. The tree operators, mediated by the b→u→𝑏𝑢brightarrow u transition, contribute in both terms but suffer from CKM suppression. Though the mixing is small, there is lack of quantitative estimate of its effect. It is then worthwhile to examine whether the large isospin conserving amplitudes are able to compensate for the tiny mixing, and to give a sizable impact on the Λb→Σ→subscriptΛ𝑏ΣLambdabrightarrowSigma decays.
content_sections.III Numerical results: In the numerical calculations, we use the masses (GeV) as followscitebib.bib96: M𝑀displaystyle M =displaystyle= 5.6196,mΣ=1.193,mb=4.8,mϕ=1.019,mJ/ψ=3.097. formulae-sequence5.6196subscript𝑚Σ1.193formulae-sequencesubscript𝑚𝑏4.8formulae-sequencesubscript𝑚italic-ϕ1.019subscript𝑚𝐽𝜓3.097displaystyle 5.6196,quad mSigma=1.193,quad mb=4.8,quad mphi=1.019,quad mJ/psi=3.097. The CKM matrix elements in the Wolfenstein parametrization read (Vu​b,Vu​s,Vt​b,Vt​s)=(A​λ3​(ρ−i​η),λ,1,−A​λ2),subscript𝑉𝑢𝑏subscript𝑉𝑢𝑠subscript𝑉𝑡𝑏subscript𝑉𝑡𝑠𝐴superscript𝜆3𝜌𝑖𝜂𝜆1𝐴superscript𝜆2displaystyle(Vub,Vus,Vtb,Vts)=(Alambda3(rho-ieta),lambda,1,-Alambda2), withcitebib.bib96 λ=0.22650,A=0.790,ρ¯=0.141,η¯=0.357. formulae-sequence𝜆0.22650formulae-sequence𝐴0.790formulae-sequence¯𝜌0.141¯𝜂0.357displaystylelambda=0.22650,quad A=0.790,quadbarrho=0.141,quadbareta=0.357. The lifetime of the ΛbsubscriptΛ𝑏Lambdab is taken to be 1.464 ps from the particle data group (PDG)citebib.bib96. Other nonperturbative parameters appearing in the hadron LCDAs have been specified in the preceding section. There are three crucial differences between Λb→Σ0→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΣ0LambdabrightarrowSigma0 and Λb→Λ0→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΛ0LambdabrightarrowLambda0 decays: (i) the former can occur only via the exchange topologies in the SM, which are power suppressed with respect to the emission ones. (ii) As the QCD penguin operators do not give contributions to the pure Δ​I=1Δ𝐼1Delta I=1 decays, the (S−P)​(S+P)𝑆𝑃𝑆𝑃(S-P)(S+P) amplitudes, stem from the Fierz transformation of the O7,8subscript𝑂78O7,8 operators, are strongly suppressed by the corresponding Wilson coefficients. Our numerical results manifest that their contributions are vanishingly small compared to the (V−A)​(V−A)𝑉𝐴𝑉𝐴(V-A)(V-A) ones. After taken account of the above two aspects, it is then expected that the Σ0superscriptΣ0Sigma0 modes have much small decay amplitudes as shown in Table. (iii) The symmetry properties of the LCDAs of Σ0superscriptΣ0Sigma0 under the exchange of the up and down quarks are always opposite to the corresponding terms for Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0, resulting in different relative contributions from ΦVsuperscriptΦ𝑉PhiV, ΦAsuperscriptΦ𝐴PhiA and ΦTsuperscriptΦ𝑇PhiT. This distinction will cause some observed quantities to behave differently, as detailed more below. In Table, we compare contributions from three components of LCDAs of Σ0superscriptΣ0Sigma0 baryon to the invariant amplitudes: ΦVsuperscriptΦ𝑉PhiV,ΦAsuperscriptΦ𝐴PhiA,and ΦTsuperscriptΦ𝑇PhiT. It is apparent that the ΦAsuperscriptΦ𝐴PhiA component for the concerned decays have the smallest contributions. This observation differs from that in Λb→Λ​ϕ→subscriptΛ𝑏Λitalic-ϕLambdabrightarrowLambdaphi decay, where a dominant contribution from the ΦAsuperscriptΦ𝐴PhiA component was claimedcitebib.bib54. The reason is that the ΦAsuperscriptΦ𝐴PhiA is antisymmetric under the interchange of the u𝑢u and d𝑑d quarks for Σ0superscriptΣ0Sigma0 but symmetric for Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0. The determination of the mixing angle has been extensive discussed in the literature, whose value still varies in a finite range. We take the value θ=(2.07±0.03)×10−2𝜃plus-or-minus2.070.03superscript102theta=(2.07pm 0.03)times 10-2 (in radians), which was also considered incitebib.bib93. As can be seen, the isospin conserving amplitudes are at least an order of magnitude larger than the isospin violating ones and can more or less compensate for the suppression from the mixing angle, implying that the isospin conserving amplitudes may provide potentially non-negligible contributions to the decays of ΛbsubscriptΛ𝑏Lambdab to the physical final state Σ​VΣ𝑉Sigma V. Comparing Tables with for the ϕitalic-ϕphi mode, it is clearly seen that the mixing causes distinct differences for the physical observables. The destructive combination of the two isospin amplitudes is responsible for the decrease in branching ratio, whereas the interference pattern reverses for the C​P𝐶𝑃CP-conjugated process, so that the direct C​P𝐶𝑃CP asymmetry varies from 5.0%percent5.05.0% without mixing to −19.3%percent19.3-19.3% with mixing. For the J/ψ𝐽𝜓J/psi process, the isospin conserving amplitude is three orders of magnitude higher than the violating one, significantly surpassing the suppression from the mixing angle. As a result the mixing effect has a strong influence on the J/ψ𝐽𝜓J/psi mode. It is apparent that the mixing effect can hugely amplify the rate of Λb→Σ​J/ψ→subscriptΛ𝑏Σ𝐽𝜓LambdabrightarrowSigma J/psi above the estimation without mixing, by as much as two orders of magnitude. The resulting asymmetries have also changed drastically. Experimentally, an upper limit on the isospin amplitude ratio ℛ=|A​(Λb→Σ0​J/ψ)/A​(Λb→Λ​J/ψ)|ℛ𝐴→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΣ0𝐽𝜓𝐴→subscriptΛ𝑏Λ𝐽𝜓R=|A(LambdabrightarrowSigma0J/psi)/A(LambdabrightarrowLambda J/psi)| is measured to be 1/21.8 at 95%percent9595% confidence level by LHCbcitebib.bib98. In the absence of Σ−ΛΣΛSigma-Lambda mixing, we have the isospin amplitude ratio ℛ=0.00185ℛ0.00185R=0.00185, which is far below the experimental upper limit. Taking into account the mixing, this ratio substantially rises to 0.0214, reaching half of the upper bound. This estimate is also in agreement with that incitebib.bib37 and supports the assumption made incitebib.bib37 that the dynamic contribution to ℛℛR is much smaller than the static mixing component within SM. If an enhancement of such dynamic contribution owing to the NP is excluded, the observation of a substantial decay rate of Λb→Σ0​J/ψ→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΣ0𝐽𝜓LambdabrightarrowSigma0J/psi in the future experiments would be strong evidence of the Λ−ΣΛΣLambda-Sigma mixing. It follows that the improved precision measurement of the isospin amplitude ratio is desirable to constrain the mixing angle.
content_sections.IV conclusion: In this work we have studied two purely isospin-violating decays Λb→Σ0​ϕ,Σ0​J/ψ→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΣ0italic-ϕsuperscriptΣ0𝐽𝜓LambdabrightarrowSigma0phi,Sigma0J/psi, which are generated via the exchange topologies induced by the b​u→s​u→𝑏𝑢𝑠𝑢burightarrow su transition. Since ΛbsubscriptΛ𝑏Lambdab and Σ0superscriptΣ0Sigma0 belong to different isospin representations, the Λb→Σ0→subscriptΛ𝑏superscriptΣ0LambdabrightarrowSigma0 transition is forbidden in the SM. The factorization approach and the diquark picture failed in describing such decays. To estimate the exchange topological contributions, we employ the leading order PQCD formalism, which allows us to evaluate all relevant topological diagrams involving two hard gluon exchanges systematically. We first explore the decays under consideration without the Σ−ΛΣΛSigma-Lambda mixing. At the quark level, the decay amplitude for an isospin-violating transition receives contributions from both the tree and electroweak penguins, whereas the QCD penguin vanishes because it preserves isospin symmetry. The calculated invariant amplitudes and helicity amplitudes are presented in detail in corresponding Tables. We compare at some length the distinctive patterns of the two modes as well as their Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0 counterparts. It has been demonstrated that the helicity amplitudes H−12​0subscript𝐻120H-frac120 and H12​1subscript𝐻121Hfrac121 respectively dominate the ϕitalic-ϕphi and J/ψ𝐽𝜓J/psi modes, due to different relative contributions from the Σ0superscriptΣ0Sigma0 baryon LCDAs. The obtained branching ratios are typically small, at 10−8−10−9superscript108superscript10910-8-10-9 level, and the direct C​P𝐶𝑃CP violations are around several percents. Furthermore, we predict for the first time a set of asymmetry parameters, which will be tested theoretically and experimentally in the future. Taking into account the Σ−ΛΣΛSigma-Lambda mixing effect, the decays of ΛbsubscriptΛ𝑏Lambdab to the physical final states involve both the isospin-conserving and isospin-violating contributions. The PQCD calculations reveal that the former are at least an order of magnitude greater than the latter, which is sufficient to compensate for the suppression caused by the mixing angle, so that the influence of mixing is more pronounced for the Λb→Σ→subscriptΛ𝑏ΣLambdabrightarrowSigma decays than the Λb→Λ→subscriptΛ𝑏ΛLambdabrightarrowLambda ones. The numerical results manifest that the mixing effect modifies significantly both the decay rates and the asymmetry parameters. In particular, it boost the branching ratio of Λb→Σ​J/ψ→subscriptΛ𝑏Σ𝐽𝜓LambdabrightarrowSigma J/psi to the order of 10−7superscript10710-7, which is encouraging for future measurements. Therefore, this mode is particularly suitable to investigate the isospin violation and to extract the mixing angle. Acknowledgements. We would like to acknowledge Prof. Hsiang-nan Li and Prof. Yue-Long Shen for helpful discussions. This work is supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grants No. 12075086 and No. 11605060 and the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province under Grants No. A2021209002 and No. A2019209449.

all_papers.title:
SU(3) analysis of four-quark operators: $K\to\pi\pi$ and vacuum matrix
  elements
all_papers.abstract:
Hadronic matrix elements of local four-quark operators play a central role in
non-leptonic kaon decays, while vacuum matrix elements involving the same kind
of operators appear in inclusive dispersion relations, such as those relevant
in $\tau$-decay analyses. Using an $SU(3)_L\otimes SU(3)_R$ decomposition of
the operators, we derive generic relations between these matrix elements,
extending well-known results that link observables in the two different
sectors. Two relevant phenomenological applications are presented. First, we
determine the electroweak-penguin contribution to the kaon CP-violating ratio
$\varepsilon'/\varepsilon$, using the measured hadronic spectral functions in
$\tau$ decay. Second, we fit our $SU(3)$ dynamical parameters to the most
recent lattice data on $K\to\pi\pi$ matrix elements. The comparison of this
numerical fit with results from previous analytical approaches provides an
interesting anatomy of the $\Delta I = \frac{1}{2}$ enhancement, confirming old
suggestions about its underlying dynamical origin.

- C2：涉及U-spin与同位旋 — 满足。证据：“U-spin, isospin” 在查询中，论文中“SU(3) decomposition”涵盖U-spin与同位旋对称性分析  
- C3：研究四夸克算符在K→ππ中的应用 — 满足。证据：“K→ππ matrix elements” 对比 “K→ππ”  

不相关性 / 风险点 / 缺口：

all_papers.title: A U-spin Puzzle in $B$ Decays
all_papers.abstract:
We impose U spin symmetry ($SU(2)_{\rm Uspin}$) on the Hamiltonian for $B$
decays. As expected, we find the equality of amplitudes related by the exchange
$d \leftrightarrow s$. We also find that the amplitudes for the $\Delta S=0$
processes $B^0 \to \pi^+\pi^-$, $B_s^0\to\pi^+ K^-$ and $B^0\to K^+ K^-$ form a
U-spin triangle relation. The amplitudes for $B_s^0\to K^+ K^-$, $B^0\to\pi^-
K^+$ and $B_s^0\to\pi^+\pi^-$ form a similar $\Delta S=1$ triangle relation.
And these two triangles are related to one another by $d \leftrightarrow s$. We
perform fits to the observables for these six decays. If perfect U spin is
assumed, the fit is very poor. If U-spin-breaking contributions are added, we
find many scenarios that can explain the data. However, in all cases, 100\%
U-spin breaking is required, considerably larger than the naive expectation of
$\sim 20\%$. This is the U-spin puzzle; it may be strongly hinting at the
presence of new physics.

约束项检查：

- C2：涉及U-spin破缺与实验数据拟合 — 满足。证据：“If perfect U spin is assumed, the fit is very poor...100% U-spin breaking is required” 对比 “U-spin, isospin”  
- C3：关注B介子衰变过程中的对称性关系 — 满足。证据：“amplitudes for B⁰→π⁺π⁻, Bₛ⁰→π⁺K⁻...form a U-spin triangle relation”

all_papers.title: Symmetries in particle physics: from nuclear isospin to the quark model
all_papers.abstract:
We present a concise pedagogic introduction to group representation theory
motivated by the historical developments surrounding the advent of the
Eightfold Way. Abstract definitions of groups and representations are avoided
in favour of the physical intuition of symmetries of the nuclear interaction.
The concept of nuclear isospin is used as a physical motivation to introduce
SU(2) and discuss the main techniques of representation theory. The discovery
of strange particles motivates extending the symmetry group to SU(3), at first
in the context of the Sakata model. We highlight the successes in fitting
mesons in the SU(3) octet, discuss the drawbacks of the Sakata model for
baryonic classifications, and how the Eightfold Way finally led to the quark
model. This approach has two major advantages: (i) the main concepts of the
theory of Lie groups are introduced and discussed without ever losing touch
with its applications in particle physics; (ii) it allows the beginner to study
group theory while also becoming acquainted with the historical developments of
particle physics that led to the concept of quarks. In particular, in this
pedagogical path the quarks appear as yet another class of particles predicted
from symmetry principles, rather than being introduced ad hoc for postulating
an SU(3) symmetry, as usually done in the literature.

sections.4: Georgi, Lie algebras in particle physics, 2nd ed., Vol
sections.20: Gasiorowicz, Elementary particle physics (Wiley, New York, 1966).
sections.23: Zee, Group Theory in a Nutshell for Physicists (Princeton University Press, USA, 2016).

sections.30: Gell-Mann, The eightfold way: A theory of strong interaction summetry (California Institute of Technology, 1961).

qualitative: SU(3) flavor symmetry extends the concept of isospin (SU(2)) to include the strange quark, allowing classification of hadrons into multiplets based on their flavor quantum numbers.

Isospin (SU(2)) symmetry treats the proton and neutron as two states of the same particle, related by a rotation in isospin space, and explains the near-degeneracy of nucleon masses and similar strong interaction properties.

U-spin is a subgroup of SU(3) flavor symmetry that exchanges up and strange quarks, analogous to isospin's exchange of up and down quarks, and is used to relate particles with the same quantum numbers except for the strange quark content.

The Eightfold Way uses SU(3) flavor symmetry to organize mesons and baryons into irreducible representations (octets and decuplets), predicting the existence and properties of undiscovered particles such as the Ω⁻.

The quark model emerges naturally from SU(3) symmetry as a physical realization of the underlying symmetry, with quarks as fundamental constituents carrying flavor quantum numbers that transform under SU(3).

While isospin is approximately conserved in strong interactions, SU(3) flavor symmetry is explicitly broken by the mass difference between the strange quark and the up/down quarks, leading to small splittings within SU(3) multiplets.
U-spin and isospin are both internal symmetries that act on the flavor space of quarks, with their generators forming part of the Lie algebra of SU(3).
quantitative: The isospin quantum number I = 1/2 for the nucleon doublet (proton and neutron), with I₃ = +1/2 for proton and I₃ = -1/2 for neutron.
The pion triplet (π⁺, π⁰, π⁻) forms an SU(2) isospin triplet with I = 1 and I₃ = +1, 0, -1.
The Clebsch-Gordan coefficient ratio for nucleon-nucleon to pion-nucleon scattering cross sections: σ(pp → pp) / σ(pn → pn) = 3, derived from SU(2) representation addition.

The Ω⁻ baryon, predicted by the Eightfold Way as the lowest state in the SU(3) decuplet with strangeness S = -3, charge Q = -1, and spin J = 3/2, was discovered in 1964 with properties matching the prediction.

The mass splitting within the SU(3) octet (e.g., between Σ⁺, Σ⁰, Σ⁻) is approximately 10–15 MeV, reflecting the breaking of SU(3) symmetry due to the strange quark mass being larger than up/down quark masses.

The U-spin transformation exchanges u ↔ s quarks, and the U-spin quantum numbers (U and U₃) are used to classify particles: for example, the K⁺ (u s̄) and K⁰ (d s̄) form a U-spin doublet with U = 1/2, U₃ = +1/2 and -1/2 respectively.

content_sections.I Introduction: Throughout the history of particle physics, symmetry principles have proven to be extremely productive tools for model building. The recognition of an underlying symmetry of Nature allows one to predict properties of fundamental particles, to obtain quantitative predictions on relations between observables, and even to anticipate the precise forms of interaction terms in the Lagrangian. In fact, the entire edifice of the Standard Model and its attempted low-energy extensions rests upon symmetry principles such as gauge invariance, a statement about local symmetries of the fundamental interactions. It is therefore essential that any novice in particle physics be fluent in the mathematical description of symmetries and their physical consequences. This is precisely the scope of the discipline called group representation theory. Symmetries of physical laws are most easily illustrated in the context of Lorentz covariance and relativity. In this case the transformation between inertial frames can be readily visualized, and representation theory can be motivated as a tool for building objects that transform in a well-defined way under a change of frame, allowing different observers to compare their experimental outputs. But the Lorentz group is certainly not the simplest, and the study of its representations relies on some previous knowledge of SU. On the other hand, more basic group structures could be introduced by appealing to internal symmetries, but attempting to use the Standard Model gauge groups as motivation makes the situation significantly murkier. To explain concepts such as weak isospin and the SUL symmetry of weak interactions, one must invoke a notion of “similarity” between electrons and neutrinos, between up- and down-type quarks. But any such similarity is blatantly badly broken by the sharp discrepancy in these particles’ masses. And it’s not helpful to explain this as a manifestation of symmetry breaking by the Higgs mechanism: the elegance of this solution is only appreciated if one already grasped the importance of enforcing these symmetries in the first place. One can safely say that attempting to build an intuition of internal symmetries by invoking a case where it is badly broken is not a very pedagogic approach. The situation is improved a little if one starts from the SUcsubscript3𝑐c case of chromodynamics, since this one is indeed preserved. However, this also requires introducing new concepts such as color charge, and has the drawback of using the more complicated SU3 group as a starting point. An alternative and more pedagogic path is to discuss internal symmetries starting from their historical origins, namely Heisenberg’s notion of nuclear isospin. For that, the only prerequisite are the concepts of protons and neutrons familiar to any undergraduate student. One can then use representation theory to predict the properties of the pions, and predict the ratio of nuclear reaction cross sections from Clebsch-Gordan coefficients. Then, by extending the symmetry group from SU2 to SU3, one can understand hadronic classification schemes, discuss the successful prediction of the Ω−superscriptΩOmega- particle, and introduce the concept of quarks in their appropriate historical background. While this approach can be found in many excellent textbooks on the subject, each has its own goals and emphasis, leaving out a good deal of important material. Some discuss much of the physics without going into detail on group theoretical developments, e. g. refs. citebib.bib1, bib.bib2. Others emphasize only the necessary group theory for the subsequent application to the Standard Model, leaving out important applications on nuclear physics, e. g. citebib.bib3. Others, while providing a very thorough discussion of the history behind these symmetry principles, were written before the full development and confirmation of the quark model and hence would not discuss it fullycitebib.bib41It is also worth noticing that ref. citebib.bib4 uses this hadronic classification approach to introduce the use of symmetries in particle physics not by pure choice, but also because the more fundamental internal symmetries of the weak and strong interactions were still unknown at the time of its writing. . There is also a plethora of pedagogic materials aimed at introducing group theory for particle physicistscitebib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7. Many of these also discuss the use of internal symmetries at the dawn of nuclear physics, but the physics is inevitably swamped in the dense formalism of generic group theoretical results. Our purpose in this paper is to offer a concise yet self-contained introduction to the main elements of group representation theory through its application to the hadronic classification schemes, starting with Heisenberg’s idea of isospin and culminating in the Eightfold Way and the quark model. Although the content presented here is not new, the novelty is in the presentation sequence. We have aimed for a healthy balance between the formal aspects of representation theory and the underlying developments in hadronic physics. In doing so we omitted formal definitions of group theory (some of which we put in the appendix), but without sacrificing the overall understanding of the main concepts and techniques. In fact, these formal definitions can even obscure rather than enlighten the topic to the very beginner. Though this presentation does not exhaust the topic, it should allow the reader to transition smoothly to other standard texts on the subject, including (but not restricted to) those cited above. The paper is organized as follows. In section we motivate the concept of nuclear isospin, show that it is associated to the SU2 group, and build its representations. Then in section we discuss rules for summing isospin, i. e. building representations related to composite states. This culminates in the Clebsch-Gordan coefficients, which we use to predict the ratio of certain nucleon + nucleon and pion + nucleon scattering cross-sections. In section we extend the symmetry group to SU3 to account for the “particle zoo” of the 1940s and 50s and discuss its representations, culminating in the Eightfold Way and the quark model. Our conclusions are reserved for section.
content_sections.II Nuclear isospin: The discovery of the neutron in 1932, and the recognition of its role as a constituent of the atomic nucleus, immediately required a postulated new interaction responsible for keeping neutrons and protons bound together. The first concrete attempt at a description of this force was put forward by Heisenberg in his seminal 1932 series of paperscitebib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, merely a few months after the reports on the discovery of the neutron2See alsocitebib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13 for examples and discussions on the early history of the concept of isospin in nuclear physics. . Motivated by the short-ranged nature of the nuclear force, Heisenberg thought of this interaction as analogous to the covalent bond in the dihydrogen cation H2+superscriptsubscript𝐻2H2+citebib.bib14, depicted in figure (left). In this case, two protons are held together by the exchange of an electron, which takes place when the two orbitals overlap. As soon as the protons are separated by a distance larger than the orbital size, i. e. the Bohr radius ∼10−10similar-toabsentsuperscript1010sim 10-10m, the exchange ceases and the electron becomes attached to one of protons only, turning it into a neutral H𝐻H atom which no longer interacts electromagnetically with the remaining H+superscript𝐻H+ ion. Heisenberg intuited that a similar mechanism was responsible for the short-ranged nuclear force, but involving the exchange of another type of charge altogether. Specifically, he postulated that the particles in the nucleus — so-called nucleons — carry another charge whose value characterizes them as a proton or a neutron. The nucleon is thus a two-level system, analogous to a spin-1/2 particle: the +1/212+1/2 state corresponds to a proton, the −1/212-1/2 to a neutron. Due to this similarity with spin, this charge is called isospin3In the beginning people called it isotopic spin. But in reality the transformation protons ↔↔leftrightarrow neutrons relates isobar nuclei, rather than isotopes, so other people called it isobaric spin. Eventually the notation was simplified to isospin. .
content_sections.II Nuclear isospin II.1 Charge independe: Now, protons and neutrons have remarkably near degenerate massescitebib.bib15, mp=938.27208816±0.00000029​MeV,mn=939.5654205±0.0000005​MeV,formulae-sequencesubscript𝑚𝑝plus-or-minus938.272088160.00000029MeVsubscript𝑚𝑛plus-or-minus939.56542050.0000005MeVbeginsplitmp=938.27208816pm 0.00000029textMeV, mn=939.5654205pm 0.0000005textMeV,endsplit amounting to a relative mass difference mn−mp(mn+mp2)≈0.14%. subscript𝑚𝑛subscript𝑚𝑝subscript𝑚𝑛subscript𝑚𝑝2percent0.14fracmn-mpleft(fracmn+mp2right)approx 0.14%. This means that a “flip” in the isospin of a free nucleon involves a comparably negligible amount of energy, so that free protons and free neutrons are approximately degenerate states. As for when they are interacting inside the nucleus, as soon as more information was gathered on the phenomenology of this nuclear force, it gradually became clear that this interaction did not seem to distinguish protons from neutrons. This is known as the approximate “charge independence” of the nuclear force. One evidence for this comes from the similar properties of proton-proton, proton-neutron and neutron-neutron scatterings (discounting for electromagnetic effects, which affect each of these processes differently)citebib.bib16, bib.bib17. Quantitatively, the nuclear potentials describing these three processes differ from one another by no more than ∼2.5%similar-toabsentpercent2.5sim 2.5%citebib.bib17. Another evidence comes from the strikingly similar energy levels of mirror nuclei, such as P and S, as shown in figure. These nuclei are related to one another by a proton ↔↔leftrightarrow neutron exchange4The P nucleus has 15 protons and 16 neutrons, whereas S has 16 protons and 15 neutrons. . Small differences in energy levels of these nuclei are well accounted for by electromagnetic effectscitebib.bib18, which again shows that the nuclear interaction (which is dominant inside the nucleus) does not sharply differentiate protons and neutrons. Altogether, this means that a “rotation” in isospin space is an approximate symmetry of nature. By this we mean that there exist certain transformations that can be performed over the physical system while keeping its dynamics completely unaltered. The system before and after the transformation are completely identical. The mere recognition of this fact has deep physical implications, as we will see in the following.
content_sections.II Nuclear isospin II.2 Isospin and SU(2: Let us put the above discussion in more formal terms. We have argued above that the nuclear interaction does not seem to distinguish protons from neutrons5Obviously the electromagnetic interaction is able to distinguish them, but its effects are subdominant compared to the nuclear force, which is typically much stronger. . From the previous discussion, we can postulate that, as far as the nuclear interaction is concerned, the proton and the neutron behave as the same kind of particle, which we call the nucleon (i. e. a particle of the atomic nucleus). This nucleon can be found in two different states in Nature, p=,n=,formulae-sequence𝑝matrix10𝑛matrix01p=beginpmatrix1 0endpmatrix,qquad n=beginpmatrix0 1endpmatrix, corresponding to “isospin up” and “isospin down” states, respectively. In general, a nucleon state can be found as a superposition described by N=(ψpψn)=ψp​p+ψn​n. 𝑁matrixsubscript𝜓𝑝subscript𝜓𝑛subscript𝜓𝑝𝑝subscript𝜓𝑛𝑛N=beginpmatrixpsip psinendpmatrix=psip,p+psin,n. Isospin symmetry means that any redefinition of p𝑝p and n𝑛n describes an equivalent physics. This transformation can be described via the matrix N′=U​N,withU=(αβγδ). formulae-sequencesuperscript𝑁′𝑈𝑁with𝑈matrix𝛼𝛽𝛾𝛿Nprime=UN,quadtextwithquad U=beginpmatrixalphabeta gammadeltaendpmatrix. Since the physics is unaltered by this transformation, all probabilities must be the same when calculated with primed or unprimed states. In particular, N†​N=N′⁣†​N′=N†​U†​U​N. superscript𝑁†𝑁superscript𝑁′†superscript𝑁′superscript𝑁†superscript𝑈†𝑈𝑁NdaggerN=Nprime,daggerNprime=NdaggerUdaggerUN. This implies that U𝑈U must be unitary, U†​U=𝟙2×2. superscript𝑈†𝑈subscript122UdaggerU=mathds12times 2. Consider now the matrix determinant 1=det𝟙2×2=det(U†​U)=(detU†)​detU=|detU|2.1subscript122superscript𝑈†𝑈superscript𝑈†𝑈superscript𝑈2beginsplit1=detmathds12times 2=det(UdaggerU) =(det Udagger)det U=|det U|2. endsplit Since U𝑈U is a complex matrix, it follows that the determinant of U𝑈U is an overall complex phase, detU=exp⁡(i​θ)𝑈𝑖𝜃det U=exp(itheta) for some θ∈ℝ𝜃ℝthetainR. It is well known that multiplying a quantum state by an overall complex phase has no physical implications, so we can restrict outselves to transformations with detU=1𝑈1det U=1. Put another way, the only novelty in eq. lies in the invariance of physics under a 2×2222times 2 unitary transformation with detU=1𝑈1det U=1. The set of matrices satisfying these conditions forms a group called SU6This is an example of a “special unitary” group. The “S” stands for “special”, meaning unitary determinant. The “U” stands for “unitary matrices” and the “2” means the matrices are 2×2222times 2. . The transformation described in eq. is analogous to a “rotation” in isospin space. In other words, it is a redefinition of what we call “up” and “down” or “proton” and “neutron” states (in much the same way that a spacetime rotation is a redefinition of what we call x,y𝑥𝑦x,y and z𝑧z axes). This is an example of an internal symmetry of nature, so-called because the transformation does not act on spacetime coordinates (like usual spatial rotations), but involves field redefinitions. The symmetry statement, in this case, is that the nuclear interaction does not distinguish protons and neutrons, so a redefinition of these fields (a re-orientation of directions in this field space) has no impact on the physical results. The recognition that a “rotation” in isospin space is a symmetry of the nuclear interaction has profound consequences. For example, consider a particle that carries some isospin, and therefore interacts via the nuclear force. If we perform an isospin redefinition, the nature of this particle, as far as the nuclear force is concerned, must remain unchanged. The interaction treats it as the same kind of particle, only in a different isospin state (otherwise this transformation wouldn’t be a symmetry). Consequently, each type of particle belongs to a vector space where the state vectors can be related to one another by some isospin transformation. In other words, the transformation does not map a state to another belonging to a different vector space, which would represent a different kind of particle. This statement is so important that it is worth phrasing it another way. If we redefine isospin, there must be a dictionary that allows us to find the “new” state of this particle in terms of the pre-transformed one. Only then would we be able to relate the physics under this new definition to the previous description and make sure that it remains indeed unaltered7Thinking about spacetime symmetries could make this argument simpler to grasp. Suppose 𝒮𝒮S is an inertial observer at rest with respect to some electric charge, and 𝒮′superscript𝒮′Sprime sees it moving with constant velocity. Clearly 𝒮𝒮S sees only a radial electric field emanating from the charge, while 𝒮′superscript𝒮′Sprime sees also a current and therefore a magnetic field as well. They disagree as to the intensity of the electric and magnetic fields, as well as to the charge and current distribution. But there must be a way to translate one’s observations into the other’s, for only then can they mutually communicate about their experiments and reach an agreement as to the universality of Maxwell’s equations. . The state space of a particle is the space of all objects that can be transformed into one another by an isospin transformation. Technically, this means that physical states must belong to representations of the SU symmetry group. We have already seen an instance of this, when we said that “proton” and “neutron” are two different states of the nucleon, which means that the space of nucleon states is two-dimensional. Other particles may belong to higher-dimensional representations. How do we construct these representation spaces? Starting from one reference state |ψ⟩ket𝜓|psi, we can apply an SU transformation (an isospin redefinition), mapping this state onto some |ψ′⟩ketsuperscript𝜓′|psiprime. This new state still describes the same particle, only in a different isospin configuration. Then perform another isospin redefinition, taking us from |ψ′⟩ketsuperscript𝜓′|psiprime to |ψ′′⟩ketsuperscript𝜓′′|psiprimeprime, and so on. If the state space of such particle is finite dimensional, eventually this procedure will cease to produce linearly independent vectors. Thus, by performing all possible symmetry transformations on a reference state, one can obtain the whole state space of the system. By construction all transformations will map this space onto itself, and all states are relatable to one another by one such transformation: then we will have built a representation. The remaining question is: what does an isospin redefinition looks like when acting on higher-dimensional spaces? Eq. reveals only how it affects the two-dimensional (isospin 1/2121/2) representation. Before trying to generalize it, it is worth looking into this representation in more detail.
content_sections.II Nuclear isospin II.3 Infinitesimal tr: The group SU has a structure that simplifies enormously our task of finding representations: it is a group of continuous transformations, meaning that we can “rotate” in isospin space by an infinitesimal amount. Think about infinitesimal parameters in equation, for instance. Such a continuous group is called a Lie group. Because of this, instead of having to perform all possible rotations on our reference state |ψ⟩ket𝜓|psi to build the representation space, we need only perform infinitesimal rotations along independent “directions”. What does the matrix U𝑈U in eq. looks like for infinitesimal transformations? By definition this is very close to “doing nothing” (we are rotating by tiny, insignificant “angles”), so the transformation matrix must be close to the identity. Indeed, we could perform a Taylor expansion and, neglecting terms of quadratic order or higher, one expects U=𝟙2×2+i​ω,ω=(ω11ω12ω21ω22). formulae-sequence𝑈subscript122𝑖𝜔𝜔matrixsubscript𝜔11subscript𝜔12subscript𝜔21subscript𝜔22U=mathds12times 2+iomega,qquadomega=beginpmatrixomega11omega12 omega21omega22endpmatrix. Moreover, since U𝑈U is unitary, U†​U=𝟙2×2=(𝟙2×2+i​ω)​(𝟙2×2−i​ω†)=𝟙2×2+i​(ω−ω†)+𝒪​(ω2),superscript𝑈†𝑈subscript122subscript122𝑖𝜔subscript122𝑖superscript𝜔†subscript122𝑖𝜔superscript𝜔†𝒪superscript𝜔2beginsplitUdaggerU=mathds12times 2=(mathds12times 2+iomega)(mathds12times 2-iomegadagger) =mathds12times 2+i(omega-omegadagger)+O(omega2),endsplit hence8We have chosen to define U=𝟙+i​ω𝑈1𝑖𝜔U=mathds1+iomega, with this i𝑖i factor in front, precisely to enforce that ω𝜔omega be hermitean. Without this factor it would be anti-hermitean, which wouldn’t change much of the conclusions below, but would introduce some inconvenient factors of (-1). ω=ω†. 𝜔superscript𝜔†omega=omegadagger. Thus ω𝜔omega is a hermitean traceless matrix, and can be put in the form ω=12​(d​θ3d​θ1−i​d​θ2d​θ1+i​d​θ2−d​θ3)=d​θ→⋅T→,𝜔12matrix𝑑superscript𝜃3𝑑superscript𝜃1𝑖𝑑superscript𝜃2𝑑superscript𝜃1𝑖𝑑superscript𝜃2𝑑superscript𝜃3⋅𝑑→𝜃→𝑇omega=frac12beginpmatrixdtheta3dtheta1-idtheta2 dtheta1+idtheta2-dtheta3endpmatrix=dvecthetacdotvecT, with T→=(T1,T2,T3)→𝑇subscript𝑇1subscript𝑇2subscript𝑇3vecT=(T1,T2,T3) are (half) the Pauli matrices, T1=12​,T2=12​(0−ii0),T3=12​(100−1). beginsplitT1=frac12beginpmatrix01 10endpmatrix,quad T2=frac12beginpmatrix0-i i0endpmatrix, qquadquad T3=frac12beginpmatrix10 0-1endpmatrix. endsplit We see that an isospin transformation is parameterized by 3 angles, just like a rotation in 3-dimensional space9This is an aspect of the famous results that SU is homomorphic to (i. e. has the same group structure as) SO, the group of orthogonal 3×3333times 3 matrices that describe rotations in 3 dimensions. . The T1,T2subscript𝑇1subscript𝑇2T1,T2 and T3subscript𝑇3T3 are called generators of infinitesimal rotations, and correspond to transformations along these three independent directions. Notice that T3subscript𝑇3T3 is an operator whose eigenvectors are the p𝑝p and n𝑛n states. It tells us the isospin configuration of the nucleon in a certain state. On the other hand T1subscript𝑇1T1 and T2subscript𝑇2T2 can be combined into T1+i​T2=andT1−i​T2=,formulae-sequencesubscript𝑇1𝑖subscript𝑇2matrix0100andsubscript𝑇1𝑖subscript𝑇2matrix0010T1+iT2=beginpmatrix01 00endpmatrixquadtextandquad T1-iT2=beginpmatrix00 10endpmatrix, which act on the nucleon states such that (T1+i​T2)​n=p,(T1−i​T2)​p=n,formulae-sequencesubscript𝑇1𝑖subscript𝑇2𝑛𝑝subscript𝑇1𝑖subscript𝑇2𝑝𝑛beginsplit(T1+iT2),n=p, (T1-iT2),p=n,endsplit i. e. they respectively raise and lower the value of isospin by one unit. For this reason they are called “raising” and “lowering” operators. Thus the state space of nucleons can be constructed by starting from the p𝑝p state and continuously applying the lowering operator. Applying it once yields n𝑛n. Applying it again on n𝑛n yields the zero vector: no new state is obtained and the procedure stops. Thus the space of nucleon states is two-dimensional.
content_sections.II Nuclear isospin II.4 The algebra of S: The above procedure can be generalized for higher-dimensional spaces in the following way. Regardless of their specific shape in n𝑛n dimensions, the three generators of SU are characterized by the commutation relations they satisfy10The reader can easily show that the matrices in eq. do satisfy these relations. , namely [Ti,Tj]=i​ϵi​j​k​Tk. subscript𝑇𝑖subscript𝑇𝑗𝑖subscriptitalic-ϵ𝑖𝑗𝑘subscript𝑇𝑘[Ti,Tj]=iepsilonijkTk. Any three matrices that satisfy these relations are intimately related to SU transformations, and are said to belong to the 𝔰​𝔲​𝔰𝔲2mathfraksu algebra11The algebra is the vector space of generators of infinitesimal transformations, together with a commutation relation. The group structure is characterized by such commutation relation of the generators. SU is characterized by relations, with ϵi​j​ksubscriptitalic-ϵ𝑖𝑗𝑘epsilonijk as coefficients of the algebra, called structure constants. Other commutation relations give rise to different groups, as we will see below. . Since these generators do not commute, it is impossible to diagonalize all three of them simultaneously. But one can show from that T2≡T12+T22+T32superscript𝑇2superscriptsubscript𝑇12superscriptsubscript𝑇22superscriptsubscript𝑇32T2equiv T12+T22+T32 commutes with all three T𝑇T’s. Therefore mmax−mmin=2​Isubscript𝑚maxsubscript𝑚min2𝐼mtextmax-mtextmin=2I. But this difference must be a natural number (because we obtain mminsubscript𝑚minmtextmin by applying a number of T−subscript𝑇T-’s to |I,mmax⟩ket𝐼subscript𝑚max|I,mtextmax). Thus the representations of SU are characterized by half-integers I=0,12,1,32,2,…,𝐼0121322…I=0,frac12,1,frac32,2,ldots, and each has dimension 2​I+12𝐼12I+1 corresponding to states m=−I,−I+1,…,I−1,I. 𝑚𝐼𝐼1…𝐼1𝐼m=-I,-I+1,ldots,I-1,I. It is now worth pausing to summarize these achievements. First, note that all of the above developments follow from the commutation relations of the generators of infinitesimal transformations, eq. . This is a general result that underlies the theory of Lie groups: we can build representations just be looking at how states transform under infinitesimal transformations. In other words, we can focus almost exclusively on the Lie algebra. Second, we see that the existence of a symmetry allows us to partition the entire state space of our theory into a number of independent subspaces. The procedure of finding representations of the symmetry group can then be seen as a way of organizing the total Hilbert space of states of a system. Indeed, we have defined the concept of “representation space” such that, under a symmetry transformation, the states of each of these subspaces transform only among themselves: there is no isospin rotation that can take a state in the I𝐼I space to a different I′superscript𝐼′Iprime. Thus the transformation matrix acting on the total state space can be decomposed in block diagonal form14The UI=1/2subscript𝑈𝐼12UI=1/2 suatrix is the one given in eq. . , U=(UI=0UI=1/2UI=1⋱). The entire Hilbert space is said to be reducible. The subspaces I=0,1/2,1,…𝐼0121…I=0,1/2,1,ldots, on the other hand, are irreducible representations, because (by construction) they cannot be reduced any further (they have been built in such a way that every state is relatable to another by a transformation). Each of these irreducible subspaces correspond to different particle types as seen by the nuclear interaction.
content_sections.II Nuclear isospin II.5 Symmetries and c: There is more. The very definition of isospin transformations being a symmetry of nature implies that such transformations do not affect the energy of the system. This means that, if H𝐻H is the hamiltonian of the system, with eigenstates |ψn⟩ketsubscript𝜓𝑛ketpsin, then U​|ψn⟩𝑈ketsubscript𝜓𝑛Uketpsin is still an eigenstate with same eigenvalue, so H​(U​|ψn⟩)=En​U​|ψn⟩=U​H​|ψn⟩. 𝐻𝑈ketsubscript𝜓𝑛subscript𝐸𝑛𝑈ketsubscript𝜓𝑛𝑈𝐻ketsubscript𝜓𝑛H(Uketpsin)=EnUketpsin=UHketpsin. Since the |ψn⟩ketsubscript𝜓𝑛ketpsin form a basis of the state space (because H𝐻H is hermitian) is follows that [H,U]=0𝐻𝑈0[H,U]=0, i. e. the hamiltonian commutes with the transformation operations. This means we can diagonalize both operators simultaneously, i. e. the |I,m⟩ket𝐼𝑚ketI,m states are also eigenstates of H𝐻H, with definite energy. Therefore, the existence of a symmetry facilitates our task of finding solutions to the Schrödinger equation, since we may restrict ourselves to these subspaces to begin with15This is what we do when solving the hydrogen atom (or any problem with spherical symmetry) with separation of variables: the angular equation will always result in the spherical harmonics, which belong to (infinite dimensional) representations of the rotation group, and the problem reduces to solving the radial equation only. . Moreover, if I≠I′𝐼superscript𝐼′Ineq Iprime then ⟨I′,m′|H|I,m⟩∼⟨I′,m′|I,m⟩=0,similar-toquantum-operator-productsuperscript𝐼′superscript𝑚′𝐻𝐼𝑚inner-productsuperscript𝐼′superscript𝑚′𝐼𝑚0 Iprime,mprime|H|I,msim Iprime,mprime|I,m=0, since states belonging to different representations are eigenstates of a hermitean operator T2superscript𝑇2T2 with different eigenvalues and are therefore orthogonal. But the left-hand side of this equation is the transition amplitude between states with I𝐼I and I′superscript𝐼′Iprime. In other words, it is the probability amplitude that a system starts in a state in the I𝐼I representation and is later16The Schrödinger equation can be written as H​|ψ​(t)⟩=i​∂∂t​|ψ​(t)⟩≃i​|ψ​(t+δ​t)⟩−|ψ​(t)⟩δ​t𝐻ket𝜓𝑡𝑖𝑡ket𝜓𝑡similar-to-or-equals𝑖ket𝜓𝑡𝛿𝑡ket𝜓𝑡𝛿𝑡H|psi(t)=ifracpartialpartial t|psi(t)simeq ifrac|psi(t+delta t)-|psi(t)delta t, or |ψ​(t+δ​t)⟩=(𝟙−i​δ​t​H)​|ψ​(t)⟩ket𝜓𝑡𝛿𝑡1𝑖𝛿𝑡𝐻ket𝜓𝑡|psi(t+delta t)=(mathds1-idelta tH)|psi(t). We then see that H𝐻H is the generator of infinitesimal time translations, in much the same way as the Jisubscript𝐽𝑖Ji are generators of infinitesimal isospin rotations, as discussed above. found with total isospin I′superscript𝐼′Iprime. It follows that, if isospin is a symmetry of the hamiltonian, then the total isospin is conserved17This is a particular example of a general theorem due to Emmy Noether, which says that if a system has a continuous symmetry (i. e. if the symmetry holds for infinitesimal transformations) then there is some conserved charge associated to this symmetry. .
content_sections.II Nuclear isospin II.6 Fitting particle: Now let us look into some of these representations in detail and interpret them physically in the context of isospin. Let us first consider the I=0𝐼0I=0 representation, which is one-dimensional. Because of this dimensionality, we call the state |I=0,m=0⟩ketformulae-sequence𝐼0𝑚0ketI=0,m=0 a singlet. Under an isospin rotation it transforms as |0,0⟩→UI=0​|0,0⟩,→ket00superscript𝑈𝐼0ket00ket0,0to UI=0ket0,0, with UI=0subscript𝑈𝐼0UI=0 a one-dimensional unitary matrix of unit determinant, which implies UI=0=1superscript𝑈𝐼01UI=0=1. In other words, a I=0𝐼0I=0 state is unaffected by an isospin redefinition. This can only mean that this particle carries no isospin at all, and therefore does not interact via the nuclear force. So any particle that is neuter under this force belongs to this isospin representation. The I=1/2𝐼12I=1/2 representation is two-dimensional. It is also called the fundamental representation18 A fundamental representation is one that serves as building blocks for other higher-dimensional representations via tensor product operations. But for unitary groups SU(n𝑛n) the fundamental representation coincides with the so-called defining representation, which consists of the n𝑛n-dimensional vector space where the very definition of these matrices act. For instance, SU(n𝑛n) is defined as the group of n×n𝑛𝑛ntimes n unitary matrices, and these matrices can be seen as operators acting on n𝑛n-dimensional vectors, so this n𝑛n-dimensional representation is the defining representation (and also a fundamental) of SU(n𝑛n). , because all the others can be built from tensor products of this space with itself, as we will see in the next section. We have already met particles that fit into this space: the proton and the neutron, corresponding to states p=|1/2,+1/2⟩𝑝ket1212p=ket1/2,+1/2 and n=|1/2,−1/2⟩𝑛ket1212n=ket1/2,-1/2. Moving on to the I=1𝐼1I=1 representation, we know this is a 3-dimensional space, so the corresponding states m=−1,0,1𝑚101m,0,1 are said to form a triplet. If this representation is realized in nature, we would expect to find three particles with near-degenerate masses that take part in the nuclear interaction. Such particles indeed exist: the pions π+superscript𝜋pi+, π0superscript𝜋0pi0, and π−superscript𝜋pi- have massescitebib.bib15 mπ±=139.57039±0.00018​MeV,mπ0=134.9768±0.0005​MeV,formulae-sequencesubscript𝑚superscript𝜋plus-or-minusplus-or-minus139.570390.00018MeVsubscript𝑚superscript𝜋0plus-or-minus134.97680.0005MeVbeginsplitmpipm=139.57039pm 0.00018textMeV, mpi0=134.9768pm 0.0005textMeV,endsplit with a relative mass difference mπ±−mπ0mπavg∼3.3%. similar-tosubscript𝑚superscript𝜋plus-or-minussubscript𝑚superscript𝜋0subscript𝑚superscript𝜋avgpercent3.3fracmpipm-mpi0mpitextavgsim 3.3%. Just like the I=1/2𝐼12I=1/2 representation, the I=1𝐼1I=1 representation has a special name. It is called the adjoint representation. Recall that finding a representation means finding a vector space of states where the generators Tisubscript𝑇𝑖Ti act as transformations. The I=1/2𝐼12I=1/2 representation is a 2-dimensional complex vector space where the generators act as in eq. . But the generators T1,T2subscript𝑇1subscript𝑇2T1,T2 and T3subscript𝑇3T3 also form a vector space among themselves, being a basis of the space of traceless hermitean matrices! So what if we use this space, i. e. the algebra itself, as the representation space? We then have a state space constituted of |T1⟩,|T2⟩ketsubscript𝑇1ketsubscript𝑇2|T1,|T2 and |T3⟩ketsubscript𝑇3|T3, and we can define the action of a generator over another via the commutator, i. e. Ti​|Tj⟩≡[Ti,Tj]. subscript𝑇𝑖ketsubscript𝑇𝑗subscript𝑇𝑖subscript𝑇𝑗TiketTjequiv[Ti,Tj]. So, in this representation, the generators will act both as vectors and as operators acting on these vectors. For example, we can use the explicit form of the commutation relations to define the matrix of the T3subscript𝑇3T3 operator acting on T−,T3,T+subscript𝑇subscript𝑇3subscript𝑇T-,T3,T+ as T3=(−10+1). subscript𝑇3matrix1missing-subexpressionmissing-subexpressionmissing-subexpression0missing-subexpressionmissing-subexpressionmissing-subexpression1T3=beginpmatrix-1 0 +1endpmatrix. We can associate |T−⟩,|T3⟩ketsubscript𝑇ketsubscript𝑇3ketT-,ketT3 and |T+⟩ketsubscript𝑇ketT+ with the π−,π0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋0pi-,pi0 and π+superscript𝜋pi+, respectively. Naturally, every Lie algebra (i. e. the algebra of infinitesimal transformations related to an arbitrary continuous group) has the adjoint representation. We will see another example in section below.
content_sections.II Nuclear isospin II.7 Charges, isospin: Looking at the matrix form of T3subscript𝑇3T3 in the adjoint representation, eq. , one cannot help but noticing that its eigenvalues are precisely the electric charges of the pions (which are the particles belonging in this adjoint representation). We can thus say that, in the adjoint representation, the charge operator is Qπ=T3. subscript𝑄𝜋subscript𝑇3Qpi=T3. If we try to apply this same rule to the fundamental representation, the outcome would be Q​p=+12​p𝑄𝑝12𝑝Q,p=+frac12p and Q​n=−12​n𝑄𝑛12𝑛Q,n=-frac12n, meaning the proton and neutron would be eigenstates of charge with eigenvalues ±1/2plus-or-minus12pm 1/2. This is clearly wrong. However, this problem can be remedied by defining a new quantum number B𝐵B, called baryon number, such that Q=T3+B2. 𝑄subscript𝑇3𝐵2Q=T3+fracB2. Then the correct charges are found by attributing B=1𝐵1B=1 for protons and neutrons, and B=0𝐵0B=0 for pions. Thus we say that nucleons are baryons (i. e. have baryonic number), whereas pions belong to another class of particles called mesons. It turns out that this baryon number B𝐵B has a deeper physical meaning than the above argument make it seems. More than being a mere adjustment factor to the charge formula, this number is actually a conserved charge of all nuclear reactions. The reader can check that this is indeed the case for all the reactions discussed henceforth in this paper.
content_sections.III Sum of isospin and Clebsch-Gordan co: Consider now a system composed of two particles, each carrying some total isospin I1subscript𝐼1I1 and I2subscript𝐼2I2. The composite system must also belong to some SU representation, say, one with total isospin T𝑇T. The question of which values of T𝑇T are allowed for given I1subscript𝐼1I1 and I2subscript𝐼2I2 is known as the problem of how to sum isospin (or any quantity that behaves as angular momentum) in quantum mechanics.

Let us then consider a system composed of two particles, one at a state |I1,m1⟩ketsubscript𝐼1subscript𝑚1|I1,m1 and the other at a state |I2,m2⟩ketsubscript𝐼2subscript𝑚2|I2,m2. The description of this composite system is given by the tensor product |I1,m1⟩⊗|I2,m2⟩≡|m1,m2⟩I1​I2. tensor-productketsubscript𝐼1subscript𝑚1ketsubscript𝐼2subscript𝑚2subscriptketsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2subscript𝐼1subscript𝐼2|I1,m1otimes|I2,m2equivketm1,m2I1I2. Our aim is to determine how this state can be written as a (superposition of) |I,M⟩ket𝐼𝑀|I,M. First note that an isospin transformation acts on the tensor product by acting on each state separately with the corresponding suatrices UIsuperscript𝑈𝐼UI of eq. , i. e. UI1⊗I2​|I1,m1⟩⊗|I2,m2⟩=UI1​|I1,m1⟩⊗UI2​|I2,m2⟩. tensor-productsuperscript𝑈tensor-productsubscript𝐼1subscript𝐼2ketsubscript𝐼1subscript𝑚1ketsubscript𝐼2subscript𝑚2tensor-productsuperscript𝑈subscript𝐼1ketsubscript𝐼1subscript𝑚1superscript𝑈subscript𝐼2ketsubscript𝐼2subscript𝑚2UI1otimes I2ketI1,m1otimesketI2,m2=UI1ketI1,m1otimes UI2ketI2,m2. In other words, an isospin transformation on the composite system is obtained by transforming the isospin of the components separately, and then composing (i. e. taking the tensor product) of the transformed states. For infinitesimal transformations, UI=𝟙+i​θ→⋅T→Isuperscript𝑈𝐼1⋅𝑖→𝜃superscript→𝑇𝐼UI=mathds1+ivecthetacdotvecTI (and similarly for UI⊗I′superscript𝑈tensor-product𝐼superscript𝐼′UIotimes Iprime) and we can show that T→I1⊗I2​|m1,m2⟩I1​I2=T→I1​|I1,m1⟩⊗|I2,m2⟩+|I1,m1⟩⊗T→I2​|I2,m2⟩. superscript→𝑇tensor-productsubscript𝐼1subscript𝐼2subscriptketsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2subscript𝐼1subscript𝐼2tensor-productsuperscript→𝑇subscript𝐼1ketsubscript𝐼1subscript𝑚1ketsubscript𝐼2subscript𝑚2tensor-productketsubscript𝐼1subscript𝑚1superscript→𝑇subscript𝐼2ketsubscript𝐼2subscript𝑚2beginsplitvecTI1otimes I2ketm1,m2I1I2=vecTI1ketI1,m1otimesketI2,m2 +ketI1,m1otimesvecTI2ketI2,m2. endsplit This means that the isospin components of the composite system is the sum of the components of each particle’s. In particular, taking only the T3subscript𝑇3T3 component, we see that T3I1⊗I2​|m1,m2⟩I1,I2=(m1+m2)​|m1,m2⟩I1​I2,superscriptsubscript𝑇3tensor-productsubscript𝐼1subscript𝐼2subscriptketsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2subscript𝐼1subscript𝐼2subscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2subscriptketsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2subscript𝐼1subscript𝐼2T3I1otimes I2ketm1,m2I1,I2=(m1+m2)ketm1,m2I1I2, meaning M=m1+m2𝑀subscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2M=m1+m2. What about the allowed values of I𝐼I, the total isospin of the composite system? First note that the maximum value of M𝑀M is I1+I2subscript𝐼1subscript𝐼2I1+I2, and this must correspond to the maximum value of I𝐼I (recall that in the I𝐼I representation M𝑀M varies from −I𝐼-I to +I𝐼+I, and if there is a I>I1+I2𝐼subscript𝐼1subscript𝐼2I>I1+I2 there would be a state with M>I1+I2𝑀subscript𝐼1subscript𝐼2M>I1+I2). Moreover, in general I𝐼I could vary by half steps, as in eq. . But if I𝐼I is integer (resp. half-integer) then all allowed values of M𝑀M are integers (resp. half-integers). Now let us put the above machinery to good use in concrete physical applications. The factors ±1/2plus-or-minus12pm1/sqrt2 are the Clebsch-Gordan coefficients for the isospin sum of I1=I2=1/2subscript𝐼1subscript𝐼212I1=I2=1/2, as the reader can easily calculate using the method developed in the previous section. It turns out that only one such nucleus exists as a stable configuration in nature: the deuteron, composed of one proton and one neutron. A bound state of two protons and two neutrons turns out to be unstable. Now, in the list of states above there are two of them composed of proton+neutron: the |1,0⟩ket10|1,0 and the |0,0⟩ket00|0,0. Which corresponds to the deuteron? Note that the states p​p𝑝𝑝pp and n​n𝑛𝑛nn are obtainable from |1,0⟩ket10|1,0 via a isospin rotation, and if this is a symmetry then the three states in the isospin =1absent1=1 triplet would all be physically equivalent. Since p​p𝑝𝑝pp and n​n𝑛𝑛nn are not found as stable states in nature, |1,0⟩ket10|1,0 can’t be either. Thus isospin symmetry establishes the total isospin of the deuteron to be 00. Symmetry arguments can also be used to lead to quantitative predictions regarding reaction cross sections. Consider, for instance, the four reactions19Here the order of reactants is relevant, because it distinguishes the incoming beam from the target of the reaction in the laboratory frame. Thus reactions (b) and (c) are physically distinguishable. where scattering of nucleons yields a deuteron d𝑑d and a pion, (a)​p+p→d+π+,→(a)𝑝𝑝𝑑superscript𝜋displaystyletext(a)p+pto d+pi+, (b)​p+n→d+π0,→(b)𝑝𝑛𝑑superscript𝜋0displaystyletext(b)p+nto d+pi0, (c)​n+p→d+π0,→(c)𝑛𝑝𝑑superscript𝜋0displaystyletext(c)n+pto d+pi0, (d)​n+n→d+π−. →(d)𝑛𝑛𝑑superscript𝜋displaystyletext(d)n+nto d+pi-. Let us call H𝐻H the Hamiltonian operator describing the dynamics of these nuclear interactions. In principle if we know H𝐻H we can determine the amplitude of any such process via ℳ=⟨final|H|initial⟩. ℳquantum-operator-productfinal𝐻initialM=textfinal|H|textinitial. However, we know that the nuclear interaction is rather non-trivial (being a residual of the more fundamental interaction between quarks), so one can expect H𝐻H to have a complicated form. Moreover, we might not know this form beforehand (which was the case when physicists were still developing the theory of the nuclear interaction in the early 1930s). But even without this knowledge we can already make quantitative statements about the cross sections of the reactions above, based only on symmetry! One way to proceed, in this case, would be to use Shmushkevich’s principlecitebib.bib19, bib.bib20, which follows from isospin symmetry. It states that if the population of reactants is uniformly distributed among all its possible isospin states, then the population of products will also be uniform (according to this same criterion) at all times. For instante, consider a uniform beam of nucleons incident on a uniform target of nucleons. Uniform distribution means that, for each 100 pairs of nucleons on beam/target, half of them will be protons and half neutrons. Each pair will react according to one of the four reactions above, and in the end we will have 100 deuterons and 100 pions. The deuteron is a singlet, so its distribution will be uniform regardless. Now, Shmushkevich’s principle states that the pions must be uniformly distributed among π−,π0,π+superscript𝜋superscript𝜋0superscript𝜋pi-,pi0,pi+. But there are two channels that can lead to a π0superscript𝜋0pi0, namely (b) and (c), while only (a) can lead to π+superscript𝜋pi+ and only (d) can lead to π−superscript𝜋pi-. Thus a uniform distribution of the pions in the final products can only be achieved if (a) and (d) are twice more efficient than (b) and (c). In other words, the cross sections must be in the ratio σa:σb:σc:σd=2:1:1:2. :subscript𝜎𝑎subscript𝜎𝑏:subscript𝜎𝑐:subscript𝜎𝑑2:1:1:2sigmaa:sigmab:sigmac:sigmad=2:1:1:2. This is in very good agreement with experimental resultscitebib.bib21. These results can also be obtained by a more systematic method involving the Clebsch-Gordan coefficients.
