content_sections.IV Extending the symmetry IV.1 Strange p: If the world was composed only of protons, neutrons and pions, it seems that an SU isospin symmetry would be an excellent explanation for the classification and behaviour of these particles. But in the late 1940s and early 1950s physicists were confronted with the discovery of a number of other particles. First, cloud chamber photographs of cosmic rays showed some unusually heavy particles decaying into pions citebib.bib22, bib.bib23, evidencing yet unknown mesons which later came to be known as the kaons. In 1950 a neutral baryon decaying into protons and pions was discovered in cosmic ray events, which later came to be dubbed the Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0 baryoncitebib.bib24. It did not take long to notice that there was something strange about these particles. For instance, Λ0+2​πsuperscriptΛ02𝜋Lambda0+2pi’s could be produced copiously when colliding proton + pions, from which one can infer the transition amplitude ⟨Λ0​π​π|H|p​π⟩quantum-operator-productsuperscriptΛ0𝜋𝜋𝐻𝑝𝜋Lambda0pipi|H|ppi. If one then used this amplitude to estimate the decay lifetime of Λ0→p+π−→superscriptΛ0𝑝superscript𝜋Lambda0to p+pi-, one would find ∼10−23similar-toabsentsuperscript1023sim 10-23s. However, observations showed that the correct lifetime was ∼10−10similar-toabsentsuperscript1010sim 10-10s: a discrepancy of 13 orders of magnitude! Clearly, production and decay were very different mechanisms for Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0 (and the same was true for the kaons). The way out of the problem involved two steps. At first, it was postulated that proton+pion collisions produce Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0 only in association with kaons, π−+p→Λ0→superscript𝜋𝑝superscriptΛ0displaystylepi-+ptoLambda0 +K0||superscript𝐾0displaystyle+underset|K0 →π−+π+. →absentsuperscript𝜋superscript𝜋displaystylequad,topi-+pi+. This is known as “associated production”, namely that these “exotic” particles would always be produced in pairs. For some time this was enough to explain all observations, but soon new reactions were observed involving an odd number of exotic particles, while other reactions involving pairs were never observed despite being allowed by the conjecture22The hypothesis of associated production came into crisis when the reaction π−+p→Ξ−+K0+K+→superscript𝜋𝑝superscriptΞsuperscript𝐾0superscript𝐾pi-+prightarrowXi-+K0+K+ was observed (with Ξ−superscriptΞXi- an exotic baryon heavier than the proton), while the reaction π−+p→Ξ−+K+→superscript𝜋𝑝superscriptΞsuperscript𝐾pi-+prightarrowXi-+K+, allowed by the conjecture, was not. These can be explained by assigning to Ξ−superscriptΞXi- a strangeness value of S=−2𝑆2S=-2 and assuming that strangeness is an approximately conserved charge. . Later it was recognized that there was another (approximately valid) conservation law involved: one can assign to these new particles a new quantum charge, which was (appropriately enough) called strangeness, such that SΛ0=−1subscript𝑆superscriptΛ01SLambda0 and SK0=+1subscript𝑆superscript𝐾01SK0=+1. This way, the production channel above conserves strangeness (Sbefore=Safter=0subscript𝑆beforesubscript𝑆after0Stextbefore=Stextafter=0), whereas the decays Λ0→p+π−→superscriptΛ0𝑝superscript𝜋Lambda0to p+pi- and K0→π−+π+→superscript𝐾0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋K0topi-+pi+ do not. This explains why the decays are much less favourable than the production. Now, if we were to fit these strange particles in isospin representations, we would be forced to place the Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0 in a singlet state, since no other particle looks like an SU partner23The discovery of other strange particles called Σ±superscriptΣplus-or-minusSigmapm could, upon first sight, lead to a tentative classification of (Σ−,Λ0,Σ+)superscriptΣsuperscriptΛ0superscriptΣ(Sigma-,Lambda0,Sigma+) as an isospin triplet. However, soon a new strange particle Σ0superscriptΣ0Sigma0 was discovered through the decay Σ0→Λ0+γ→superscriptΣ0superscriptΛ0𝛾Sigma0toLambda0+gamma, with a mass that was much closer to Σ±superscriptΣplus-or-minusSigmapm. So the true isospin triplet is composed of (Σ−,Σ0,Σ+)superscriptΣsuperscriptΣ0superscriptΣ(Sigma-,Sigma0,Sigma+), leaving Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0 as a singlet. of Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0. In this case the expression for the charge operator would again fail to give the correct charge of Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0, forcing us to modify it as Q=T3+B+S2. 𝑄subscript𝑇3𝐵𝑆2Q=T3+fracB+S2. If we now try to assign quantum numbers to the kaons to meet their correct charges, we would be led to assign T3=−1/2subscript𝑇312T3/2 to K0superscript𝐾0K0 (to match QK0=0subscript𝑄superscript𝐾00QK0=0 since BK0=0subscript𝐵superscript𝐾00BK0=0 and SK0=1subscript𝑆superscript𝐾01SK0=1), so that the T3=+1/2subscript𝑇312T3=+1/2 partner would be a K+superscript𝐾K+. The antiparticle of K+superscript𝐾K+ would be a K−superscript𝐾K-, with opposite strangeness charge S=−1𝑆1S, so it cannot belong to the same isospin multiplet as the latter (contrary to charge, strangeness is independent from isospin, and particles in the same isospin multiplet have the same strangeness). It follows that there must be another isospin 1/2121/2 multiplet where we fit the K+superscript𝐾K+ and another neutral kaon which we call K0¯¯superscript𝐾0overlineK0, which is the antiparticle of K0superscript𝐾0K0 and must therefore have the same mass. This particle was predicted this way, and later indeed observedcitebib.bib25, confirming the success of this scheme based on strangeness. By the late 1960s a number of additional heavy strange particles were discovered, such as ΣΣSigma and ΞΞXi baryons, as well as the (non-strange) ΔΔDelta’s already mentioned above. Due to this proliferation of particles, a classification based solely on SU representations became increasingly more cumbersome, since additional representations (often with the same isospin as others, yet fitting rather different particles) had to be introduced ad hoc to make the scheme work. This proliferation of particles and the difficulty in classifying them neatly under SU representations is colloquially known as the particle zoo.
content_sections.IV Extending the symmetry IV.2 Sakata mo: A major step towards a more elegant and more predictive classification of hadrons was made by japanese physicist Shoichi Sakata in 1956. Sakata proposed that the Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0 would be yet another state of a nucleon, which could then be found in three different states, p+=,n0=,Λ0=,formulae-sequencesuperscript𝑝matrix100formulae-sequencesuperscript𝑛0matrix010superscriptΛ0matrix001displaystyle p+=beginpmatrix1 0 0endpmatrix,quad n0=beginpmatrix0 1 0endpmatrix,quadLambda0=beginpmatrix0 0 1endpmatrix, or rather in a general superposition of these states. Sakata’s statement is that the nuclear interaction has an approximate symmetry under a redefinition of these states. So, similarly to what we discussed in section, one could do a redefinition N=(ψpψnψΛ)→N′=U​N,𝑁matrixsubscript𝜓𝑝subscript𝜓𝑛subscript𝜓Λ→superscript𝑁′𝑈𝑁N=beginpmatrixpsip psin psiLambdaendpmatrixto Nprime=UN, with U𝑈U a 3×3333times 3 unitary matrix with unit determinant. The group of such matrices is called SU. Notice that mΛ0=1115.683±0.006​MeV,subscript𝑚superscriptΛ0plus-or-minus1115.6830.006MeVmLambda0=1115.683pm 0.006textMeV, so that the relative mass difference between the Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0 and the average mass of proton and neutron is Δ​mΛ0,p​nmΛ0,p​navg≃17.2%. similar-to-or-equalsΔsubscript𝑚superscriptΛ0𝑝𝑛superscriptsubscript𝑚superscriptΛ0𝑝𝑛avgpercent17.2fracDelta mLambda0,pnmLambda0,pntextavgsimeq 17.2%. This already illustrates that this new symmetry is only approximately valid, but this shall not prevent us from proceeding. After all, SU isospin was also only approximate but still gave us many important and valid predictions.
content_sections.IV Extending the symmetry IV.3 Represent: Our task is again to build representations of this SU group, and then argue that each representation corresponds to a certain particle type. The method is the same as above: we build representations by performing infinitesimal SU transformations on a certain reference state. Following the same arguments as in section we find that the generators of infinitesimal SU transformations must be hermitean traceless matrices. We already know one representation of this group: the fundamental representation, a 3-dimensional space with basis given by eq. . In this representation, the infinitesimal transformations are given by 3×3333times 3 complex traceless hermitian matrices. How many generators are there? As a real vector space, the space of all complex 3×3333times 3 matrices has dimension24There are 32superscript3232 entries in the matrix, and each is described by two real numbers, namely the real and the imaginary parts of each complex entry. 2×322superscript322times 32. Making it hermitian decreases the dimension in half25The 333 diagonal elements must be real. Once we fix the 2×3232times 3 elements in the upper-triangular matrix (above the diagonal), those below the diagonal are also fixed by the hermiticity condition. So we only need 3+2×3=932393+2times 3=9 real numbers to describe the whole matrix. , leaving 32superscript3232. Finally the traceless condition lowers the dimension once more by 1, leaving us with a dimension26In general, the Lie algebra of 𝔰​𝔲​(n)𝔰𝔲𝑛mathfraksu(n) (i. e. the space of n×n𝑛𝑛ntimes n hermitean traceless matrices) has dimension n2−1superscript𝑛21n2-1. Applying this to 𝔰​𝔲​𝔰𝔲2mathfraksu one finds a dimension 22−1=3superscript221322-1=3, as we saw in section. of 32−1superscript32132-1. This means SU has 8 linearly independent generators. In SU, the usual generators are given by the Pauli Matrices. We also have, for 3 dimensions, an usual choice of generators, called the Gell-Mann matrices, given by λ1=,λ2=(0−i0i00000),λ3=(1000−10000),λ4=,λ5=(00−i000i00),λ6=,λ7=(00000−i0i0),λ8=13​(10001000−2). beginsplitlambda1=beginpmatrix010 100 000endpmatrix,quadlambda2=beginpmatrix0-i0 i00 000endpmatrix,quadlambda3=beginpmatrix100 0-10 000endpmatrix, lambda4=beginpmatrix001 000 100endpmatrix,quadlambda5=beginpmatrix00-i 000 i00endpmatrix, lambda6=beginpmatrix000 001 010endpmatrix,quadlambda7=beginpmatrix000 00-i 0i0endpmatrix,quadlambda8=dfrac1sqrt3beginpmatrix100 010 00-2endpmatrix. endsplit Notice that λ1,2,3subscript𝜆123lambda1,2,3 are simply the Pauli matrices inserted into a larger 3×3333times 3 paradigm. This means that the algebra 𝔰​𝔲​𝔰𝔲2mathfraksu is contained in 𝔰​𝔲​𝔰𝔲3mathfraksu. Consequently, representations of SU contain representations of SU. In other words, the classification scheme we will construct here will extend what we discussed above: all the successes of classification by SU isospin will be maintained. Actually, a glimpse at λ4subscript𝜆4lambda4 and λ5subscript𝜆5lambda5 shows that, if we remove the second row and column, we are left with two of the Pauli matrices. Similarly, from its other commutation relation, it is an eigenvector of Y𝑌Y with eigenvalue (y+1)𝑦1(y+1). Therefore all ladder operators map eigenvectors of (T3,Y)subscript𝑇3𝑌(T3,Y) to other such eigenvectors (unless they map |t3,y⟩ketsubscript𝑡3𝑦kett3,y to the zero vector). But to act on a state with these ladders is equivalent to performing arbitrary SU transformations on this state. Thus we will build irreducible representations of SU by repeatedly applying these operators on a reference state of the representation, resulting in a number of linearly independent states, until this iterative procedure exhausts itself and no new independent vectors are produced. As long as we are dealing with finite dimensional representations, this iteration must always cease to produce new independent vectors at some point. When this happens, we have found our representation: all states are related to each other by SU transformations. In the case of SU, only one generator T3subscript𝑇3T3 could be simultaneously diagonalized28The operator T2superscript𝑇2T2 is not one of the generators, although it is built from them. It is called the Casimir operator of the 𝔰​𝔲​𝔰𝔲2mathfraksu algebra. , whose eigenvalues m𝑚m label the states of the representation, and the other generators formed ladder operators, which move the states along both directions of this straight line (increasing and decreasing m𝑚m). Thus SU representations could be visualized as a series of points on a straight line, corresponding to the allowed values of m=−I,…,I𝑚𝐼…𝐼m=-I,ldots,I. In the case of SU each state in a representation space is labeled by two parameters, and we can illustrate this representation as points in a (t3,y)subscript𝑡3𝑦(t3,y) plane. Each point is called a “weight” and the collection of points belonging to a representation is called a “weight diagram”. Figure shows how each point (i. e. each weight) is mapped onto another by the ladder operators defined above.Let us consider first the fundamental or defining representation of SU, which is the representation where the transformations act as 3×3333times 3 unitary matrices (and infinitesimal SU transformations are represented by the Gell-Mann matrices themselves). Going along with the Sakata Model of SU, we can label the three vectors belonging to this representation space as p=(1,0,0),n=(0,1,0),Λ=(0,0,1)formulae-sequence𝑝100formulae-sequence𝑛010Λ001p=(1,0,0),n=(0,1,0),Lambda=(0,0,1). Since it is three dimensional, we usually refer to it as the 3 representation.It turns out that, in SU, there is another 3-dimensional representation which is inequivalent to the fundamental 𝟑33. Note that, if U𝑈U is an SU matrix, then so is its complex conjugate U∗superscript𝑈U*. The U∗superscript𝑈U*’s defines the complex-conjugate representation of the U𝑈U’s. Moreover, for an infinitesimal SU transformation29Recall that the parameters of our transformation (i. e. the “angles” by which we perform the “rotation”/transformation, dubbed αasuperscript𝛼𝑎alphaa below, are always real numbers. The reader may now pause and wonder: why was this not mentioned in the case of SU above? Where is the “antifundamental” of SU? It turns out that, for SU, any representation is equivalent to its conjugate! Graphically, all states of an SU representation lie along a line symmetric around the origin. Thus the above transformations (going from the fundamental to the antifundamental) correspond to a mere renaming of the states of the I𝐼I representation, which keeps the vector space itself unaltered. But for SU these two representations define different spaces. We can then put these states in a weight diagram (i. e. in a (t3,y)subscript𝑡3𝑦(t3,y) plane), which results in figure. Note how this parallels figure above. This is of course no accident, since the states in the adjoint representation are the ladder (and diagonal T3subscript𝑇3T3 and Y𝑌Y) operators themselves. One says that the roots are the weights of the adjoint representation. Notice also that both |T3⟩ketsubscript𝑇3ketT3 and |Y⟩ket𝑌ketY have the same eigenvalues (t3=0,y=0)formulae-sequencesubscript𝑡30𝑦0(t3=0,y=0). This does not mean that they are the same vector. Rather, it means that certain weights in an SU representation may have multiplicity larger than one. In other words, there may be more than one state sitting on the same point of the weight diagram31One may wonder why this happens in SU and not in SU (since in that case two vectors with the same labels j𝑗j and m𝑚m are necessarily linearly dependent). The reason for this difference stems from the fact that, in SU, there is essentially only one way to get from a state to another using the ladder operators: you can only ”climb up or down” using J±subscript𝐽plus-or-minusJpm. In SU, on the other hand, you can reach one point from another by different paths in weight space. In other words, we can use several different combinations of T±,U±subscript𝑇plus-or-minussubscript𝑈plus-or-minusTpm,Upm and V±subscript𝑉plus-or-minusVpm to reach one state from another, and these possibilities don’t always commute, and will in general result in linearly independent states. Indeed, notice for instance that there are at least three possibilities to go from a state (t3,y)subscript𝑡3𝑦(t3,y) to (t3−1,y)subscript𝑡31𝑦(t3-1,y), namely by applying T−subscript𝑇T-, U+​V−subscript𝑈subscript𝑉U+V- or V−​U+subscript𝑉subscript𝑈V-U+. From the commutation relations in eq. one sees that [U+,V−]=T−⟹U+​V−=T−+V−​U+,subscript𝑈subscript𝑉subscript𝑇subscript𝑈subscript𝑉subscript𝑇subscript𝑉subscript𝑈[U+,V-]=T-implies U+V-=T-+V-U+, so U+​V−​|t3,y⟩subscript𝑈subscript𝑉ketsubscript𝑡3𝑦U+V-kett3,y, T−​|t3,y⟩subscript𝑇ketsubscript𝑡3𝑦T-kett3,y and V−​U+​|t3,y⟩subscript𝑉subscript𝑈ketsubscript𝑡3𝑦V-U+kett3,y are linearly dependent, but any two of these are in general independent. . In the case of the adjoint representation, the point (0,0)00(0,0) has multiplicity 2. We represent this multiplicity in the weight diagram of figure by circling the central dot at the origin. Because T−,T3subscript𝑇subscript𝑇3T-,T3 and T+subscript𝑇T+ form an 𝔰​𝔲​𝔰𝔲2mathfraksu subalgebra of 𝔰​𝔲​𝔰𝔲3mathfraksu, the points along horizontal lines in SU weight diagrams form SU representations. Looking at figure we infer that: • |U+⟩,|V+⟩ketsubscript𝑈ketsubscript𝑉ketU+,ketV+ form an SU doublet, • |T−⟩,|T3⟩,|T+⟩ketsubscript𝑇ketsubscript𝑇3ketsubscript𝑇ketT-,ketT3,ketT+ form an SU triplet, • |U+⟩,|V+⟩ketsubscript𝑈ketsubscript𝑉ketU+,ketV+ form an SU doublet, • |Y⟩ket𝑌ketY forms an SU singlet. We know that the pions fit into an SU triplet, so we could naturally try to accommodate them as the |T−⟩,|T3⟩,|T+⟩ketsubscript𝑇ketsubscript𝑇3ketsubscript𝑇ketT-,ketT3,ketT+ in the octet. Moreover, recalling the discussion at the end of section, we know that (K0,K+)superscript𝐾0superscript𝐾(K0,K+) and (K−,K0¯)superscript𝐾¯superscript𝐾0(K-,overlineK0) fit into SU doublets, so we can associate them to (|U+⟩,|V+⟩)ketsubscript𝑈ketsubscript𝑉(ketU+,ketV+) and (|V−⟩,|U−⟩)ketsubscript𝑉ketsubscript𝑈(ketV-,ketU-). There remains the isospin singlet, which should correspond to another meson. In 1958, theorists who worked on the Sakata model predicted the existence of this new particlecitebib.bib26, bib.bib27, a new meson later dubbed η𝜂eta, which was indeed found with the properties anticipated from symmetry argumentscitebib.bib28. In particular, note that its mass mη=547.862±0.017​MeVsubscript𝑚𝜂plus-or-minus547.8620.017MeVmeta=547.862pm 0.017textMeV is very much in line with those of the kaons, mK±=493.677±0.016​MeV,mK0=mK¯0=497.611±0.013​MeV,formulae-sequencesubscript𝑚superscript𝐾plus-or-minusplus-or-minus493.6770.016MeVsubscript𝑚superscript𝐾0subscript𝑚superscript¯𝐾0plus-or-minus497.6110.013MeVbeginsplitmKpm=493.677pm 0.016textMeV, mK0=moverlineK0=497.611pm 0.013textMeV,endsplit in agreement with expectations from symmetry arguments (concerning their belonging to the same SU multiplet). Figure illustrates this fitting of the mesons in the octet representation. Note that the electric charge of the particles do not change as we move along the direction of the U±subscript𝑈plus-or-minusUpm roots (or as we apply the U±subscript𝑈plus-or-minusUpm ladders). Instead, charge increases or decreases as we move in the orthogonal direction, which is also shown in the figure as the Q𝑄Q axis, corresponding to the operator32Note that this is perpendicular to the direction U3∼λ3+3​λ8similar-tosubscript𝑈3subscript𝜆33subscript𝜆8U3simlambda3+sqrt3lambda8, which is another Pauli matrix that can be built out of the Gell-Mann matrices. We chose to work with T3subscript𝑇3T3 and Y𝑌Y as the diagonal generators, but we could with equal right have chosen U3subscript𝑈3U3 and Q𝑄Q. This is yet another illustration of the fact that 𝔰​𝔲​𝔰𝔲3mathfraksu is formed of 3 overlapping 𝔰​𝔲​𝔰𝔲2mathfraksu’s. Note also that the states lying on top of the U3subscript𝑈3U3 direction belong to 𝔰​𝔲​𝔰𝔲2mathfraksu representations, which are symmetric around the origin. Thus the weight diagram must be symmetric against reflections around Q𝑄Q, as can be indeed seen from figure. Q≡T3+Y2. 𝑄subscript𝑇3𝑌2Qequiv T3+fracY2. Comparing with eq. we see that Y=B+S𝑌𝐵𝑆Y=B+S, and since for mesons B=0𝐵0B=0 we see that in this case the hypercharge Y𝑌Y is just a quantification of strangeness. This is again in line with our discussions of section, where we concluded that (K0,K+)superscript𝐾0superscript𝐾(K0,K+) doublet had strangeness S=+1𝑆1S=+1 while their antiparticles (K0¯,K−)¯superscript𝐾0superscript𝐾(overlineK0,K-) have S=−1𝑆1S.Finally, another important SU representation is the decuplet, thus named because it is a 10-dimensional vector space. Its weight diagram is shown in figure. It can be constructed by starting at a state with largest value of t3subscript𝑡3t3 (this state must exist, otherwise the representation space would be infinite dimensional) and applying V−subscript𝑉V- transformations 3 times consecutively. By the fourth application of V−subscript𝑉V- the result is the null vector. This is akin to the case of SU, when a state with m=mmin𝑚subscript𝑚minm=mtextmin had to be annihilated by the lowering operator for consistency. Moreover, the decuplet is such that, after applying three times V−subscript𝑉V-, one cannot do other operations except go back up via V+subscript𝑉V+ or via U+subscript𝑈U+. It can be shown that U+subscript𝑈U+ can be applied only 3 times before annihilating the state, and then one can only apply T+subscript𝑇T+ to close the triangle, as shown in figurecitebib.bib4. The decuplet is important in particle physics because, up until 1963, we had detected nine spin 3/2323/2 baryons, namely: the four ΔΔDelta’s that we met in section, a triplet of baryons called Σ∗superscriptΣSigma*, and a doublet of Ξ∗superscriptΞXi*citebib.bib29. Their properties fit nicely into the decuplet representation. From eq. we see, for example, that the state at the top-right corner of the triangle has Y=1𝑌1Y=1 and T3=3/2subscript𝑇332T3=3/2, resulting in Q=2𝑄2Q=2. We did know a particle with this charge: the Δ++superscriptΔabsentDelta++ baryon, with isospin 3/2323/2 under SU. As discussed in section we expect four such baryons, which were indeed found and fit nicely into the top-most row of the triangle in figure. As we move to the left in the diagram along a horizontal line, the charge Q𝑄Q decreases by one unit. Likewise, descending in the V−subscript𝑉V- direction decreases Y𝑌Y by 111 and T3subscript𝑇3T3 by 1/2121/2, therefore decreasing Q𝑄Q by unit as well. So the Σ∗superscriptΣSigma*’s fit naturally in the row below the ΔΔDelta’s, and the Ξ∗superscriptΞXi*’s fit in the row below the Σ∗superscriptΣSigma*’s. Moreover, recalling that Y=B+S𝑌𝐵𝑆Y=B+S and that these particles are baryons, one has S=Y−1𝑆𝑌1S=Y-1 in this case. This prediction is also in agreement with the observations that SΔ=0subscript𝑆Δ0SDelta=0, SΣ∗=−1subscript𝑆superscriptΣ1SSigma* and SΞ∗=−2subscript𝑆superscriptΞ2SXi*=-2. However, symmetry arguments tell us that we should have 10 of these particles, and not just nine. This led many theorists in 1962 to conjecture that a tenth particle should existcitebib.bib30, bib.bib31, dubbed the ΩΩOmega baryon (since ΩΩOmega is the last letter of the greek alphabet, and this would be the last missing member of the decuplet). Moreover, the properties of this particle can be predicted from symmetry arguments: it should have spin 3/2323/2, charge −11-1 and strangeness −33-3. Not only that, the mass splitting between the consecutive rows of the diagram is approximately constant, since mΔsubscript𝑚Δdisplaystyle mDelta ≃1232​MeV,similar-to-or-equalsabsent1232MeVdisplaystylesimeq 1232textMeV, mΣ∗subscript𝑚superscriptΣdisplaystyle mSigma* ≃1385​MeV,similar-to-or-equalsabsent1385MeVdisplaystylesimeq 1385textMeV, mΞ∗subscript𝑚superscriptΞdisplaystyle mXi* ≃1532​MeV,similar-to-or-equalsabsent1532MeVdisplaystylesimeq 1532textMeV, allowing us to also predict mΩ−pred. ≃1680​MeV. similar-to-or-equalssuperscriptsubscript𝑚superscriptΩpred.1680MeVmOmega-textpred. simeq 1680textMeV. In February 1964 the discovery of a particle with exactly these properties was announcedcitebib.bib29, with mass mΩ−exp. =1672.45superscriptsubscript𝑚superscriptΩexp.1672.45mOmega-textexp. =1672.45MeV (within ∼0.5%similar-toabsentpercent0.5sim 0.5% of the predicted mass!). To this day the prediction and subsequent detection of the Ω−superscriptΩOmega- baryon is one of the greatest success stories in particle physics.
content_sections.IV Extending the symmetry IV.4 Tensor pr: We can also consider the analogous problem to what we studied in section: what happens when one deals with a composite system of two (or more) particles belonging to SU representations? The resulting composite system must also belong to SU representations. But which ones? Mathematically, this amounts to determining how tensor products of SU representations decompose as direct sums of SU representations. For this purpose, note that the developments that led to eqs. and are valid for any group. So also in SU the eigenvalues t3subscript𝑡3t3 and y𝑦y of a composite system |t3,y⟩⊗|t3,y⟩tensor-productketsuperscriptsubscript𝑡31superscript𝑦1ketsuperscriptsubscript𝑡32superscript𝑦2kett3,yotimeskett3,y are t3+t3superscriptsubscript𝑡31superscriptsubscript𝑡32t3+t3 and y+ysuperscript𝑦1superscript𝑦2y+y. In the (t3,y)subscript𝑡3𝑦(t3,y) weight diagram, this means that when we take the tensor product of two representations, the resulting figure is achieved by superimposing copies of one of these representations over each point belonging to the other representation (i. e. taking the weights of one representation as the origin of the other’s weight diagram). Figure illustrates this for the tensor product of the fundamental and the antifundamental representations. We see that the outer points form a hexagon, and there are three weights at the origin. The octuplet is an hexagon with multiplicity two at the origin, so we see that the 9-dimensional space obtained from this tensor product naturally decomposes as 𝟑⊗𝟑¯=𝟖⊕𝟏. tensor-product3¯3direct-sum813otimesoverline3=8oplus1. With the same construction we can perform the product of 𝟑⊗𝟑tensor-product333otimes3 and identify a 𝟑¯¯3overline3 as well as a triangle corresponding to a 6-dimensional representation which we call the 𝟔66. Likewise we can see that 𝟑⊗𝟔tensor-product363otimes6 results in an octet and a decupletcitebib.bib7. So we can write 𝟑⊗𝟑⊗𝟑=(𝟔⊕𝟑¯)⊗𝟑=(𝟔⊗𝟑)⊕(𝟑¯⊗𝟑)=𝟏𝟎⊕𝟖⊕𝟖⊕𝟏. tensor-product333tensor-productdirect-sum6¯33direct-sumtensor-product63tensor-product¯33direct-sum10881beginsplit3otimes 3otimes 3=(6oplusoverline3)otimes3 =(6otimes3)oplus(overline3otimes 3) =10oplus 8oplus 8oplus 1. endsplit
content_sections.IV Extending the symmetry IV.5 Sakatons: These results indicate that all SU representations can be recovered from tensor products of the fundamental and antifundamental. It is precisely because they act as building blocks of all representations that they are called “fundamental”. From figure we see that, in Sakata’s model, the octet + singlet could then be seen as composite states of protons/neutrons/ΛΛLambda’s with their antiparticles p¯¯𝑝overlinep/n¯¯𝑛overlinen/Λ¯¯ΛoverlineLambda. These fundamental constituents of the Sakata model are often denoted as sakatons. This perspective fits nicely with the meson octet in figure, since all quantum numbers would match if we identify π+=n¯​psuperscript𝜋¯𝑛𝑝pi+=overlinenp, K+=p​Λ¯superscript𝐾𝑝¯ΛK+=poverlineLambda, K0=n​Λ¯superscript𝐾0𝑛¯ΛK0=noverlineLambda, π0=(n¯​n+p¯​p)/2superscript𝜋0¯𝑛𝑛¯𝑝𝑝2pi0=(overlinenn+overlinepp)/sqrt2 (the m=0𝑚0m=0 state of the SU triplet), and η=(n¯​n−p¯​p−2​Λ¯​Λ)/6𝜂¯𝑛𝑛¯𝑝𝑝2¯ΛΛ6eta=(overlinenn-overlinepp-2overlineLambdaLambda)/sqrt6. The remaining mesons of the octed are antiparticles of these. Note, for instance, how in this scheme the S=+1𝑆1S=+1 kaons K0superscript𝐾0K0 and K+superscript𝐾K+ would be composed of a Λ¯¯ΛoverlineLambda, which is the antiparticle of an S=−1𝑆1S particle. One sees that the mesons fit in octets which we can interpret as stemming from 𝟑⊗𝟑¯tensor-product3¯33otimesoverline3. This product also contain a singlet (now called η′superscript𝜂′etaprime), predicted since 1957citebib.bib26, bib.bib32 and observed in 1964citebib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35 – yet another success for this mesonic classification scheme. However, this model is a lot less successful for a classification of baryons. When Sakata proposed this model in 1956, eight spin-1/2 baryons were known: p𝑝p, n𝑛n, ΛΛLambda, 3 ΣΣSigma’s and 2 ΞΞXi’s33Note that these are different particles from the spin-3/2 Σ∗superscriptΣSigma*’s and Ξ∗superscriptΞXi*’s appearing in the decuplet. The similar names come from similarities they share, e. g. the fact that both ΣΣSigma and Σ∗superscriptΣSigma* exist as isospin triplets under SU, i. e. there are three isospin partners (Σ−,Σ0,Σ+)Sigma-,Sigma0,Sigma+) and also three isospin partners (Σ∗−,Σ∗0,Σ∗+)Sigma*-,Sigma*0,Sigma*+). Similarly, both ΞΞXi and Ξ∗superscriptΞXi* form SU doublets. citebib.bib36, bib.bib37. Since the ΣΣSigma’s have strangeness SΣ=−1subscript𝑆Σ1SSigma, they would have to contain one ΛΛLambda. Clearly a Σ−superscriptΣSigma- would have to also include an antiproton p¯¯𝑝overlinep (since this is the only sakaton with negative charge), so we would need at least a 𝟑⊗𝟑¯=𝟖⊕𝟏tensor-product3¯3direct-sum813otimesoverline3=8oplus1. But there is no way to fit the ΣΣSigma triplet in the octet: the only t3subscript𝑡3t3-triplet in this representation lies on the t3subscript𝑡3t3 axis, and not all states in this axis would contain a ΛΛLambda (meaning not all states would have the correct strangeness for the ΣΣSigma’s), see fig. . We could then try to accommodate the ΣΣSigma’s and ΞΞXi’s using larger representations, for instance 𝟑⊗𝟑⊗𝟑¯tensor-product33¯33otimes 3otimesoverline3, which is 3×3×3=27333273times 3times 3=27-dimensional. However, the other 22 baryonic partners of the ΣΣSigma’s and ΞΞXi’s, which would be expected to belong to this large representation space, were never found. It was Gell-Mann citebib.bib38 and Ne’emann citebib.bib39, working independently, who first proposed a different baryonic classification scheme. Instead of taking p,n𝑝𝑛p,n and ΛΛLambda as belonging to the fundamental, they instead proposed that these should be classified together with the ΣΣSigma’s and ΞΞXi’s in the 𝟖88 representation. This is the famous “Eightfold Way” of particle physics, depicted in fig. . In this scenario, the spin-1/2 baryons fit in an octet while the spin-3/2 ones fit the decuplet. Looking at the developments of the last section, we can identify this 𝟏𝟎⊕𝟖direct-sum10810oplus 8 as a result34The reader may now look at the expression written at the end of section, namely 𝟑⊗𝟑⊗𝟑=𝟏𝟎⊕𝟖⊕𝟖⊕𝟏tensor-product333direct-sum108813otimes 3otimes 3=10oplus 8oplus 8oplus 1, and conclude that we should find one baryonic decuplet, two baryonic octets and one baryonic singlet. But only one decuplet and one octet are actually found! A detailed explanation of why the 𝟖⊕𝟏direct-sum818oplus 1 baryons do not exist is beyond the scope of this paper. It is related to the confinement mechanism of quarks. It turns out that quarks carry not only flavour (i. e. their characteristic of being an u𝑢u, d𝑑d or s𝑠s) but also spin 1/2 and a colour charge. Quark confinement means that only fully anti-symmetrized wavefunctions in the colour degree of freedom are allowed. By Pauli’s exclusion principle this means that the wavefunction must be fully symmetric in flavour and spin. It turns out that only the 𝟏𝟎⊕𝟖direct-sum10810oplus 8 satisfy these requirements, and are therefore the only SU baryonic representations that can be found in Naturecitebib.bib1. of the product 𝟑⊗𝟑⊗𝟑tensor-product3333otimes 3otimes 3. The remaining question is: what is the fundamental representation in this case? Which are the fundamental constituent particles of mesons and baryons? Gell-Mann dubbed these particles “quarks”citebib.bib4035George Zweig also had a similar idea, and dubbed these fundamental constituents “aces”citebib.bib41. Clearly it was Gell-Mann’s nomenclature that stuck. Incidentally, Gell-Mann took the idea of the name from a passage of James Joyce’s Finnegans Wake: “Three quarks for Muster Mark!”, inspired by the 𝟑⊗𝟑⊗𝟑tensor-product3333otimes 3otimes 3 construction of baryons. . The three states of the fundamental representation are called up, down and strange quarks, or, in terms of t3subscript𝑡3t3 and y𝑦y eigenvalues, u=|12,13⟩,d=|−12,13⟩,s=|0,−23⟩. formulae-sequence𝑢ket1213formulae-sequence𝑑ket1213𝑠ket023u=left|frac12,frac13right,quad d=left|-frac12,frac13right,quad s=left|0,-frac23right. A few important conclusions follow straightforwardly, namely: • applying eq. to the fundamental representation one sees that these quarks have charges Qu=2/3subscript𝑄𝑢23Qu=2/3, Qd=−1/3subscript𝑄𝑑13Qd/3 and Qs=−1/3subscript𝑄𝑠13Qs/3. Their charges are thus fractions of the elementary charge. • Since baryons are constituted of 3 quarks, these constituents must have fractional baryonic number B=1/3𝐵13B=1/3. Mesons are formed of quarks and anti-quarks and thus have B=1/3+(−1/3)=0𝐵13130B=1/3+(-1/3)=0. • Looking at the meson octet in fig. and how it stems from the product 𝟑⊗𝟑¯tensor-product3¯33otimesoverline3 as in fig. , one concludes that the strangeness charge of the mesons is nothing but a count of the number of strange quarks (or rather strange antiquarks, since s𝑠s has strangeness −11-1) that constitute them. Likewise for the baryons in the octet and in the decuplet. • The fact that strangeness is only approximately conserved can now be interpreted in the following manner: the strong interaction is not able to change the nature of a quark, but the weak interaction is. So, in a strangeness-violating decay such as Λ0→p+π−→superscriptΛ0𝑝superscript𝜋Lambda0to p+pi-, what happens is that the strange quark in Λ0=u​d​ssuperscriptΛ0𝑢𝑑𝑠Lambda0=uds is decaying as s→u+→𝑠limit-from𝑢displaystyle sto u+ W−||superscript𝑊displaystyleunderset|W- →d+u¯→absent𝑑¯𝑢displaystyle,rightarrow d+overlineu with W−superscript𝑊W- a mediator of the weak interaction. Then Λ0=u​d​s→u​u​d+d​u¯=p+π−. superscriptΛ0𝑢𝑑𝑠→𝑢𝑢𝑑𝑑¯𝑢𝑝superscript𝜋Lambda0=udstouud+doverlineu=p+pi-. Despite these predictive successes, for some time there was some skepticism as to whether quarks indeed exist, or whether they would just be mathematical artifacts of this symmetry constructioncitebib.bib4. For one, it is relatively easy to detect charged particles, but despite decades of successful experiments in particle physics no particles with fractional charges had ever been seen. It was then clear that quarks, if they existed, could never be seen in isolation, but only confined within hadrons – and this confinement mechanism was largely unknown at the time (and still is to a great extent!). By 1968, high-energy collisions of electrons and nuclei were finally able to probe the inner structure of hadrons, showing that they indeed behave as if constituted of point-like substructures. Throughout the early 1970s a series of other theoretical and experimental discoveries helped explain some aspects of quark confinement, and strengthened the conviction of the community on the existence of these fundamental hadronic constituentscitebib.bib42. Overall, this ultimate triumph of the quark model was another chapter in the long history of theoretical prediction of new particles followed by subsequent experimental confirmation.
content_sections.V Conclusions: Symmetry arguments and related techniques from group representation theory have always played a pivotal role in the development of particle physics. These techniques appear in the study of the Lorentz group, in classification schemes proposed since the earliest days of particle physics, and in the gauge principle that now lies at the heart of the Standard Model of elementary particles and their fundamental interactions. It is therefore essential that any beginner in this field be familiar with this language. In this paper we paved a pedagogic path that serves as an introduction to group representation theory for beginner particle physicists. Our approach focuses in the history of hadronic classification schemes that culminated in the quark model in the mid- to late 1960s. We argued that this is the most natural starting point to introduce group theory to this audience, since it allows one to start from the most basic SU group and progress naturally to the more challenging SU structure. Moreover, this allows students to get acquainted with some aspects of the early history of this field and some of its most successful predictions. Through the study of symmetries and groups we can tell the story of many particles that were predicted and later detected, a constant pattern that is still ongoing in the field of particle physics. This approach is also particularly interesting because it allows beginners to have a first contact with the history of the quark model. Most students are already familiar with the fact that protons and neutrons are composed of quarks. But as scientists it is important to not only know something as a fact, but also to know the details of how and why we came to know that as a fact. And for the quark model this inevitably means discussing SU representations and/or deep inelastic scattering experiments. In this sense, this work differs from most of the current literature that choose to present the SU paradigm already from an ad hoc postulate of the existence of 3 quark flavours. Here we instead culminate in the prediction of quarks as belonging to the fundamental representation of a (previously recognized) SU symmetry group. In this pedagogic path, quarks are yet another paradigmatic example of particles predicted from symmetry arguments and later confirmed by experiments. Finally, an important novelty in this presentation is a middle path we have found between a full presentation of mathematical tools and an emphasis on physical applications. The reader will find here an explanation of the concept of group representation, including the fundamental and the adjoint, as well as the main techniques underlying the highest weight construction which can be used to build general representations of Lie algebras. This allows for a reasonable understanding of the ideas being discussed, and provides the reader with tools to later explore more technical texts. On the other hand, we purposefully avoid any general results, sticking to the groups we are actually interested in. Abstract mathematical definitions of groups and representations are reserved for the appendix. Theorems on the geometry of SU weight diagrams are not stated nor proven. We have focused only on the results that are necessary for the reader to understand the “Eightfold Way” and the quark model. After studying the subject as presented here, the reader will hopefully be more prepared to tackle more thorough textbooks on group theory, with the benefit of knowing where some of these arid concepts will appear and how they can be applied. Should this text help in providing students with the toolkit to face this journey more lightly, it will have fulfilled its purpose.

all_papers.title: Production of pentaquarks with hidden charm and double strangeness in $\Xi_b$ and $\Omega_b$ decays
all_papers.abstract: Recently, several pentaquark states $P_{css}$, with global flavor $\bar c c s s n$, have been predicted within a theoretical framework based on unitary coupled channels. We study theoretically the feasibility to observe the $P_{css}$ with $I(J^P)=\frac{1}{2}(\frac{1}{2}^-)$ in the decays $\Xi_b^0\to \eta \eta_c \Xi^0$ and $\Omega_b^-\to K^- \eta_c \Xi^0$. Indeed, within the model, the $\eta_c \Xi^0$ channel is the lowest mass pseudoscalar-baryon channel to which this pentaquark state couples, thus we can expect to observe its signal in the $\eta_c \Xi^0$ invariant mass distribution of the mentioned decays. We identify the dominant weak decay processes and then implement the hadronization into the different meson-baryon channels in the final state, linked by flavor symmetry. The dominant meson-baryon final state interaction is then implemented to generate the full amplitude, implicitly accounting for the dynamical emergence of the pentaquark states. We obtain a clear Breit-Wigner-like resonant signal in the spectrum of the $\Omega_b^-$ decay, exceeding that in the $\Xi_b^0$ decay by two to three orders of magnitude. In the case of the latter decay, the resonant state would manifest as a significant dip in the spectrum. We study the feasibility of searching for these $b$-hadron decay modes and analysing their resonant components using the current and future data samples from the LHCb experiment.

sections.20: E Close An introduction to quarks and partons
sections.24: E Close An introduction to quarks and partons

qualitative: SU(3) flavor symmetry is used to relate different meson-baryon channels in the hadronization process of b-hadron decays, enabling the description of final states with different quark content through symmetry-based mappings.

U-spin symmetry, a subgroup of SU(3) flavor symmetry, is employed to connect decays involving strange and non-strange quarks, particularly in relating the Ξ_b^0 and Ω_b^- decays to the same pentaquark state with hidden charm and double strangeness.

The dominant meson-baryon final state interactions are implemented using unitary coupled channels, where SU(3) and U-spin symmetries guide the structure of the interaction potentials and the coupling between channels.

The η_c Ξ^0 channel is identified as the lowest-mass pseudoscalar-baryon channel to which the P_css pentaquark state with I(J^P) = 1/2(1/2^-) couples, making it the preferred channel for resonance detection in the decays studied.

The theoretical framework relies on the dynamical generation of pentaquark states via nonlinear dynamics in unitarized meson-baryon scattering, with SU(3) flavor symmetry ensuring consistent coupling patterns across different decay modes.
quantitative: The predicted mass of the P_css pentaquark state with I(J^P) = 1/2(1/2^-) is approximately 4500 MeV.
The predicted width of the P_css state is of the order of 10 MeV.
The resonant signal in the Ω_b^- → K^- η_c Ξ^0 decay is two to three orders of magnitude larger than in the Ξ_b^0 → η η_c Ξ^0 decay.
The dominant coupling of the P_css state is to the D̄ Ω_c̄ and D̄_s Ξ'_c channels, with weaker coupling to the η_c Ξ^0 channel.
The η_c Ξ^0 channel is open for the P_css state, while the D̄ Ω_c̄ and D̄_s Ξ'_c channels are closed, making η_c Ξ^0 the accessible final state for experimental observation.

content_sections.I Introduction: Pentaquark research has become highly active in the past decade, spurred by notable experimental findings from the LHCb collaboration, which reported hidden charm pentaquark candidates such as Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4380Pc, Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4312Pc, Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4440Pc, Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4457Pc, Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4337Pc without strangeness citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3, as well as containing a strange quark, Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs citebib.bib4 and Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4338Pcs citebib.bib5. Several theoretical models, in particular those based on meson-baryon molecular interpretations, have sought to elucidate their nature (see Refs. citebib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12 for reviews on the subject). Several states were indeed predicted citebib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21 before their observation by LHCb. A logical progression would be to explore the potential existence of hidden-charm pentaquarks with strangeness S=−2𝑆2S=-2, denoted as Pc​s​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠𝑠Pcss, for which there is currently no experimental evidence. This contrasts with theoretical predictions of models relying on implementing unitarity in coupled channels, based on potentials derived from t-channel vector meson exchange citebib.bib22, bib.bib23, which have predicted several Pc​s​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠𝑠Pcss states in the range about 4500-4700MeV. Other theoretical approaches are based on the meson exchange model citebib.bib24, sum rules citebib.bib25 and quark models citebib.bib26, bib.bib27. The states in Refs. citebib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24 emerge as generated from the intricate nonlinear dynamics involved in the unitarization of the meson-baryon scattering amplitudes. In particular, the lowest mass pole predicted in Ref. citebib.bib23, was found in the pseudoscalar-meson–baryon interaction with quantum numbers I​(JP)=12​(12−)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃12superscript12I(JP)=frac12(frac12-) and flavor c¯​c​s​s​n¯𝑐𝑐𝑠𝑠𝑛barccssn, which was associated to a molecular pentaquark state with mass about 4500MeV and width of the order of 10MeV, predominantly coupling to D¯​Ωc¯𝐷subscriptΩ𝑐barDOmegac and D¯s​Ξc′subscript¯𝐷𝑠subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐barDsXiprimec, and less strongly to ηc​Ξsubscript𝜂𝑐ΞetacXi. This theoretical backdrop prompts exploration of potential experimental reactions aimed at observing such a state. This endevour can draw inspiration from the success story of the pentaquark with hidden charm and single strangeness, Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs. Indeed this state was anticipated by coupled channel unitary models citebib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib28, and the Ξb−→J/Ψ​K−​Λ→superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝐽Ψsuperscript𝐾ΛXib-to J/Psi K-Lambda decay was proposed in Ref. citebib.bib29 to observe its signal. This reaction was later employed by the LHCb collaboration to experimentally observe this pentaquark for the first time citebib.bib4. Encouraged by this successful example, in the present work we study the possibility to see the Pc​s​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠𝑠Pcss pentaquark with I​(JP)=12​(12−)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃12superscript12I(JP)=frac12(frac12-) in the ηc​Ξ0subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0etacXi0 spectrum in the Ξb0→η​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0𝜂subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Xib0toetaetacXi0 and Ωb−→K−​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscript𝐾subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Omegab-to K-etacXi0 decays. The choice of ηc​Ξsubscript𝜂𝑐ΞetacXi for the observation of the Pc​s​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠𝑠Pcss state is justified since the D¯​Ωc¯𝐷subscriptΩ𝑐barDOmegac and D¯s​Ξc′subscript¯𝐷𝑠subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐barDsXiprimec channels are closed for decay. We also discuss if these reactions could be attainable in an experimental facility such as LHCb.
content_sections.II Formalism II.1 Summary of the unitary: We first provide a brief summary of the model from Ref. citebib.bib23 for generating the pentaquark state with flavor c¯​c​s​s​n¯𝑐𝑐𝑠𝑠𝑛barccssn and I​(JP)=12​(12−)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃12superscript12I(JP)=frac12(frac12-), further details can be found in that reference. The model follows the techniques of the chiral unitary approach citebib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32, bib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36 to sum up the coupled channels ηc​Ξsubscript𝜂𝑐ΞetacXi, D¯s​Ξc′subscript¯𝐷𝑠subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐barDsXiprimec and D¯​Ωc¯𝐷subscriptΩ𝑐barDOmegac, implementing unitarity, and starting from kernel potentials based on t-channel vector meson exchange mechanisms. The required vertices are obtained using a formalism based on heavy quark spin symmetry and the local hidden gauge approach citebib.bib37, bib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib41 properly extrapolated to the charm sector citebib.bib42, bib.bib43. Within this model, the S-wave pseudoscalar-baryon (P​B𝑃𝐵PB) tree level potentials are given by citebib.bib23 Vi​j=g2​Ci​j​(p0+p′⁣0),subscript𝑉𝑖𝑗superscript𝑔2subscript𝐶𝑖𝑗superscript𝑝0superscript𝑝′0displaystyle Vij=g2Cij(p0+pprime 0),, where the subindices denote specific pseudoscalar-baryon channels, p0​(p′⁣0)superscript𝑝0superscript𝑝′0p0(pprime 0) are the on-shell center of mass energy of the initial(final) meson, g𝑔g is a coupling constant defined in citebib.bib23, and the coefficients Ci​j=Cj​isubscript𝐶𝑖𝑗subscript𝐶𝑗𝑖Cij=Cji are given in Table. Note the dependence on the inverse of the squared masses of the vector mesons exchanged in the t-channel. Building upon the potentials of Eq. , within the framework of the coupled channels unitary approach, exact unitarity can be incorporated into the meson-baryon interaction. To this aim, we use the Bethe-Salpeter equation t=(1−V​G)−1​V,𝑡superscript1𝑉𝐺1𝑉t=(1-VG)-1V , where G𝐺G represents a diagonal matrix containing the meson-baryon loop functions. It is worth interpreting Eq. as the coupled channel resummation shown in Fig. , where each intermediate step includes all of the possible P​B𝑃𝐵PB states listed in Table. The loop function, Glsubscript𝐺𝑙Gl, for the meson-baryon channel l𝑙l, is regularized by means of a three-momentum cutoff, ΛΛLambda: Gl​(s)=∫q<Λsubscript𝐺𝑙𝑠subscript𝑞Λdisplaystyle Gl(sqrts)=intq<Lambda d3​q→(2​π)3​12​ωl​(q)​MlEl​(q)superscript𝑑3→𝑞superscript2𝜋312subscript𝜔𝑙𝑞subscript𝑀𝑙subscript𝐸𝑙𝑞displaystylefracd3vecq(2pi)3frac12omegal(q)fracMlEl(q) with q=|q→|𝑞→𝑞q=|vecq|, ωl​(q)=ml2+q2subscript𝜔𝑙𝑞subscriptsuperscript𝑚2𝑙superscript𝑞2omegal(q)=sqrtm2l+q2, El=Ml2+q2subscript𝐸𝑙subscriptsuperscript𝑀2𝑙superscript𝑞2El=sqrtM2l+q2, and ml​(Ml)subscript𝑚𝑙subscript𝑀𝑙ml(Ml) the mass of the meson(baryon). The value of the cutoff stands as the primary source of uncertainty within the model, naturally ranging between Λ=600Λ600Lambda=600MeV and 800800800MeV citebib.bib23. A potential issue with the cutoff method in the heavy hadron sector arises from the presence of a pole in the integrand of Eq. for s𝑠sqrts in the range [m+M,Λ2+m2+Λ2+M2]𝑚𝑀superscriptΛ2superscript𝑚2superscriptΛ2superscript𝑀2[m+M,sqrtLambda2+m2+sqrtLambda2+M2]. This means that near s∼Λ2+m2+Λ2+M2similar-to𝑠superscriptΛ2superscript𝑚2superscriptΛ2superscript𝑀2sqrtssimsqrtLambda2+m2+sqrtLambda2+M2, the result for the real part of the loop function may not be reliable, as the integrand above the pole cannot adequately compensate the sharp increase before the pole in the proper evaluation of the principal value. This concern with the cutoff method for heavy hadrons is also discussed in Refs. citebib.bib44, bib.bib45, bib.bib46. In the present work, it only affects the ηc​Ξ0subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0etacXi0 channel for Λ∼600similar-toΛ600Lambdasim 600MeV since, for this channel, Λ2+m2+Λ2+M2=4489superscriptΛ2superscript𝑚2superscriptΛ2superscript𝑀24489sqrtLambda2+m2+sqrtLambda2+M2=4489MeV, which is close to the mass of the predicted pentaquark state, around ∼4500similar-toabsent4500sim 4500MeV. However, since at energies close to the pentaquark mass we are significantly above the ηc​Ξ0subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0etacXi0 threshold, the real part of its loop function must be small, with the imaginary part dominating the behavior. Therefore, for Λ∼600similar-toΛ600Lambdasim 600MeV, we consider only the imaginary part in the ηc​Ξ0subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0etacXi0 loop function as a suitable approximation. This procedure is commonly used when one has a situation like this citebib.bib47. The high nonlinear dynamics involved in Eq. leads to the emergence of poles in the ti​jsubscript𝑡𝑖𝑗tij scattering amplitudes on the second Riemann sheet. From the position of these poles, the value of the mass and width of the generated resonances can be inferred. In the present case, for I​(JP)=12​(12−)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃12superscript12I(JP)=frac12(frac12-), a pole was found in Ref. citebib.bib23 corresponding to a state of mass 4535MeV and a width of 9MeV for Λ=600Λ600Lambda=600MeV, and M=4479𝑀4479M=4479MeV, Γ=12Γ12Gamma=12MeV, for Λ=800Λ800Lambda=800MeV. The difference between the results for both cutoff values is to be considered as a measure of the uncertainty in our model. Additionally, from the residue of the amplitudes at the poles, the couplings of the generated resonances to the different channels can be obtained. We found in Ref. citebib.bib23 that this pentaquark couples mostly to D¯​Ωc¯𝐷subscriptΩ𝑐barDOmegac and D¯s​Ξc′subscript¯𝐷𝑠subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐barDsXiprimec and very weakly to ηc​Ξsubscript𝜂𝑐ΞetacXi (explicit values of the couplings can be found in Table IV of Ref. citebib.bib23). However, since ηc​Ξsubscript𝜂𝑐ΞetacXi is the only open channel for the generated state, it is the only source of the width of the pentaquark. The generated pentaquark described in this section is to be understood as dynamically arising from the coupled-channel effects, and the present study aims to investigate its production in Ξb0→η​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0𝜂subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Xib0toetaetacXi0 and Ωb−→K−​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscript𝐾subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Omegab-to K-etacXi0 decays.
content_sections.II Formalism II.2 Formalism for Ξb0→η​ηc: In this section we explain the mechanism for the Ξb0→η​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0𝜂subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Xib0toetaetacXi0 process for which we follow an analogous formalism to that used in previous studies such as the calculation of Ξb−→J/Ψ​K−​Λ→superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝐽Ψsuperscript𝐾ΛXib-to J/Psi K-Lambda citebib.bib29, Λc+→π+​M​B→superscriptsubscriptΛ𝑐superscript𝜋𝑀𝐵Lambdac+topi+MB citebib.bib48 (with M​B𝑀𝐵MB a meson-baryon pair), or Λb→J/Ψ​Λ​→subscriptΛ𝑏𝐽ΨΛ1405Lambdabto J/PsiLambda citebib.bib49, which are based on a methodology initially proposed in citebib.bib50. The reaction mechanism, prior to the final state interaction (FSI), is diagrammatically depicted in Fig. , and can be understood by dividing it into three parts. Weak decay: First the b𝑏b quark within the Ξb0superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0Xib0 state undergoes a weak transition to form a c​c¯𝑐¯𝑐cbarc pair and an s𝑠s-quark, as illustrated in the left part of Fig. , resulting in a final three-quark system s​u​s𝑠𝑢𝑠sus. This transition is characterized by the matrix elements of the Cabibbo-Kobayashi-Maskawa (CKM) matrix Vc​b​Vc​s∗subscriptsuperscript𝑉absent𝑐𝑏subscriptsuperscript𝑉𝑐𝑠Vphantom*cbV*cs. This process is commonly referred to as an internal W-emission diagram. External W-emission, which is color favored, does not work for this decay but it is possible in Ωb−→K−​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscript𝐾subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Omegab-to K-etacXi0, as will be discussed later. Further discussion and justification of the suppression of alternative topologies for weak mechanisms at the quark level can be found in Refs. citebib.bib48, bib.bib51. Hadronization: The c​c¯𝑐¯𝑐cbarc pair combines to create the ηcsubscript𝜂𝑐etac meson, while the virtual s​u​s𝑠𝑢𝑠sus three-quark state undergoes hadronization to form a meson-baryon pair. This hadronization proceeds via the generation of a q¯​q¯𝑞𝑞barqq pair with vacuum quantum numbers, in a philosophy in the line to the P03superscriptsubscript𝑃033P0 model citebib.bib52, bib.bib53. In the present process, the formation of a q¯​q¯𝑞𝑞barqq pair between the lower u𝑢u and s𝑠s lines is not possible. Indeed, initially strong correlation between the u𝑢u and s𝑠s quarks of the original ΞbsubscriptΞ𝑏Xib state is assumed, with the quarks moving independently within a potential well. Since the ΞbsubscriptΞ𝑏Xib (JP=1/2+superscript𝐽𝑃1superscript2JP=1/2+) resides in the ground state of the three-quark (u​s​b)𝑢𝑠𝑏(usb) configuration, the relative angular momenta between the individual quarks are all zero. Following the weak transition, but before the hadronization process, the three-quark state (s​u​s)𝑠𝑢𝑠(sus) must be in a p-wave to produce a pentaquark with JP=1/2−superscript𝐽𝑃1superscript2JP=1/2-. As the initial u𝑢u and s𝑠s quarks are regarded as spectators and initially possess L=0𝐿0L=0, the only possible scenario is for the upper s𝑠s quark to carry an angular momentum of L=1𝐿1L=1. Since the final-state mesons and baryons are in their respective ground states and in S-wave in relation to each other, all angular momenta in the final state are zero. Therefore, the q¯​q¯𝑞𝑞barqq pair is constrained to be produced solely between the upper s𝑠s and u𝑢u quarks, as depicted in Fig. . To evaluate the scattering amplitude for the process depicted in Fig. , let us start by considering the wave function of the initial Ξb0superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0Xib0 in flavor space citebib.bib54, bib.bib55, bib.bib56 : |Ξb0⟩=12​|b​(u​s−s​u)⟩,ketsuperscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏012ket𝑏𝑢𝑠𝑠𝑢displaystyle|Xib0=frac1sqrt2|b(us-su),, which is antisymmetric in the lighter quarks. After the weak process, but before the hadronization, the three-quark state turns into 12​|s​(u​s−s​u)⟩,12ket𝑠𝑢𝑠𝑠𝑢displaystylefrac1sqrt2|s(us-su),, which preserves the same antisymmetric flavor and spin correlation given that the initial u𝑢u and s𝑠s quarks are considered as spectators. To incorporate the hadronization, we introduce the q¯​q¯𝑞𝑞barqq pairs, leading to the final quark flavor state: |H⟩ket𝐻displaystyle|H ≡12​ηc​|s​(u¯​u+d¯​d+s¯​s+c¯​c)​(u​s−s​u)⟩absent12subscript𝜂𝑐ket𝑠¯𝑢𝑢¯𝑑𝑑¯𝑠𝑠¯𝑐𝑐𝑢𝑠𝑠𝑢displaystyleequivfrac1sqrt2etac|s,(baruu+bardd+barss+barcc),(us-su) =12​ηc​∑i=14|P3​i​qi​(u​s−s​u)⟩,absent12subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptsubscript𝑖14ketsubscript𝑃3𝑖subscript𝑞𝑖𝑢𝑠𝑠𝑢displaystyle=frac1sqrt2etacsumi=14|P3iqi(us-su),, where q≡(udsc)​and​P≡q​q¯τ=(u​u¯u​d¯u​s¯u​c¯d​u¯d​d¯d​s¯d​c¯s​u¯s​d¯s​s¯s​c¯c​u¯c​d¯c​s¯c​c¯),𝑞𝑢𝑑𝑠𝑐and𝑃𝑞superscript¯𝑞𝜏𝑢¯𝑢𝑢¯𝑑𝑢¯𝑠𝑢¯𝑐𝑑¯𝑢𝑑¯𝑑𝑑¯𝑠𝑑¯𝑐𝑠¯𝑢𝑠¯𝑑𝑠¯𝑠𝑠¯𝑐𝑐¯𝑢𝑐¯𝑑𝑐¯𝑠𝑐¯𝑐qequivleft(beginarraycu d s cendarrayright),textandPequiv qbarqtau=left(beginarrayccccubaruubardubarsubarc dbarudbarddbarsdbarc sbarusbardsbarssbarc cbarucbardcbarscbarc endarrayright),, which corresponds to the quark-antiquark representation of the pseudoscalar meson matrix P=(π02+η3+η′6π+K+D¯0π−−12​π0+η3+η′6K0D−K−K¯0−η3+2​η′6Ds−D0D+Ds+ηc),𝑃superscript𝜋02𝜂3superscript𝜂′6superscript𝜋superscript𝐾superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝜋12superscript𝜋0𝜂3superscript𝜂′6superscript𝐾0superscript𝐷superscript𝐾superscript¯𝐾0𝜂32superscript𝜂′6superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐷0superscript𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝜂𝑐displaystyle P=left(beginarrayccccfracpi0sqrt2+fracetasqrt3+fracetaprimesqrt6pi+K+barD0 pi--frac1sqrt2pi0+fracetasqrt3+fracetaprimesqrt6K0D- K-barK0-fracetasqrt3+frac2etaprimesqrt6Ds- D0D+Ds+etacendarrayright),, where for the η𝜂eta and η′superscript𝜂′etaprime we have assumed the usual physical mixing between the singlet and octet S​U​𝑆𝑈3SU states citebib.bib57. The hadronized state |H⟩ket𝐻|H can now be written as |H⟩=ket𝐻absentdisplaystyle|H= 12ηc(K−u(us−su)+K¯0d(us−su)displaystylefrac1sqrt2etacbigg(K-u(us-su)+barK0d(us-su) +13(−η+2η′)s(us−su)+Ds−c(us−su)),displaystyle+frac1sqrt3left(-eta+sqrt2etaprimeright)s(us-su)+Ds-c(us-su)bigg), where we omit the bra-ket notation for simplicity. The ηc​η​s​(u​s−s​u)subscript𝜂𝑐𝜂𝑠𝑢𝑠𝑠𝑢etaceta s(us-su) term is the only one in Eq. that exhibits an overlap with one of the channels1Note that the last term in Eq. would generate the state Ds−​Ξc+superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐Ds-Xic+, not the Ds−​Ξc+′Ds-Xicprime+ of Table. responsible for generating the Pc​s​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠𝑠Pcss in our model, namely ηc​Ξ0subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0etacXi0. content_sections.II Formalism II.3 Formalism for Ωb−→K−​η: In this section, we focus solely on the differences and particularities of the Ωb−→K−​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscript𝐾subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Omegab-to K-etacXi0 decay with respect to the process Ξb0→η​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0𝜂subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Xib0toetaetacXi0, as its formalism parallels that of the preceding one. In Fig. (a), we see that the internal W-emission is analogous to Fig. , but now the external W-emission mechanism is also possible, Fig. (b). This leads to the final state in flavor space being |H⟩ket𝐻displaystyle|H ≡Ωc​|s​(u¯​u+d¯​d+s¯​s+c¯​c)​c¯⟩absentsubscriptΩ𝑐ket𝑠¯𝑢𝑢¯𝑑𝑑¯𝑠𝑠¯𝑐𝑐¯𝑐displaystyleequivOmegac|s,(baruu+bardd+barss+barcc),barc =Ωc​∑i=14P3​i​Pi​4=Ωc​P342absentsubscriptΩ𝑐superscriptsubscript𝑖14subscript𝑃3𝑖subscript𝑃𝑖4subscriptΩ𝑐subscriptsuperscript𝑃234displaystyle=Omegacsumi=14P3iPi4,=OmegacP234 =Ωc(K−D¯0+K¯0D−−13ηDs−displaystyle=Omegacbigg(K-barD0+barK0D--frac1sqrt3eta Ds- +23η′Ds−+Ds−ηc),displaystyle+sqrtfrac23etaprimeDs-+Ds-etacbigg), from where we can see that although there is no tree level decay into K−​ηc​Ξ0superscript𝐾subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0K-etacXi0, such a final state can be produced via coupled channels from the transition D¯0​Ωc→ηc​Ξ0→superscript¯𝐷0subscriptΩ𝑐subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0barD0OmegactoetacXi0, as illustrated in Fig. . Therefore, the amplitude for the external emission process of the Ωb−→K−​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscript𝐾subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Omegab-to K-etacXi0 decay is ℳe​e​(Minv)=Nc​𝒞​GD¯0​Ωc​(Minv)​tD¯0​Ωc,ηc​Ξ0​(Minv). subscriptℳ𝑒𝑒subscript𝑀invsubscript𝑁𝑐𝒞subscript𝐺superscript¯𝐷0subscriptΩ𝑐subscript𝑀invsubscript𝑡superscript¯𝐷0subscriptΩ𝑐subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0subscript𝑀invdisplaystyleMee(Mrm inv)=Nccal CGbarD0Omegac(Mrm inv),tbarD0Omegac,etacXi0(Mrm inv),. Note that the external W-emission is enhanced by a factor of the number of colors, Nc=3subscript𝑁𝑐3Nc=3, compared to the internal W-emission. This arises from the need to sum over the three possible quark colors in the hadronization process in Fig. (b), unlike in Fig. (a) where the color is fixed in the final meson. There is indeed an ambiguity in the relative sign when compared to the internal emission process, Eq. , which affects the interference between these two terms. However, as we will see in the results section, the Ωb−→K−​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscript𝐾subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Omegab-to K-etacXi0 decay spectrum is largely determined by the external-emission mechanism, rendering this ambiguity in sign rather inconsequential. Finally, the expression for the invariant mass distribution is the same as in Eq. but substituting the Ξb0superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0Xib0 by Ωb−superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏Omegab- and the η𝜂eta by K−superscript𝐾K-, and the amplitude is now ℳ=ℳi​e+ℳe​eℳsubscriptℳ𝑖𝑒subscriptℳ𝑒𝑒M=Mie+Mee.
content_sections.III Results: In Fig. , we show the ηc​Ξ0subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0etacXi0 invariant mass distributions for the Ξb0→η​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0𝜂subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Xib0toetaetacXi0 and Ωb−→K−​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscript𝐾subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Omegab-to K-etacXi0 decays. In each plot, the solid line represents the result obtained with a cutoff of Λ=600Λ600Lambda=600MeV, while the dashed line corresponds to Λ=800Λ800Lambda=800MeV. The dotted lines in the plot for the Ωb−→K−​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscript𝐾subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Omegab-to K-etacXi0 decay represent the results obtained by changing the sign in Eq. to account for the ambiguity in its relative sign compared to Eq. , as explained in the previous section. The plots clearly show that the uncertainty resulting from this effect is very small, primarily as a result of the considerable predominance of external emission relative to internal one in this decay. Although there is a global arbitrary normalization factor, the relative strengths between the different reactions reflect genuine theoretical predictions. An immediately striking observation from the figures is the significant difference in intensity and shape between the distributions of both decays, calling for a deeper examination of the underlying reasons. Regarding the large difference in strength in favor of the Ωb−superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏Omegab- decay, it is a consequence of the interplay of various contributing factors. Firstly, the Ωb−superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏Omegab- decay is predominantly governed by the external W-emission mechanism. This amplitude enjoys a Nc=3subscript𝑁𝑐3Nc=3 enhancement, see Eq. , with respect to the internal W-emission amplitude in Ξb0superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0Xib0 decay, Eq. . In addition, the latter one is suppressed by a 1/6161/sqrt6 factor. When these amplitudes are squared to compute the spectrum, the Ωb−superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏Omegab- decay ends up with a magnitude approximately 50 times larger, solely due to numerical flavor and color coefficients. In addition, we see in Fig. that the factor G3​T31≡GD¯0​Ωc​tD¯0​Ωc,ηc​Ξ0subscript𝐺3subscript𝑇31subscript𝐺superscript¯𝐷0subscriptΩ𝑐subscript𝑡superscript¯𝐷0subscriptΩ𝑐subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0G3T31equiv GbarD0Omegac,tbarD0Omegac,etacXi0 in Eq. is typically about four times larger than the factor G1​T11≡Gηc​Ξ0​tηc​Ξ0,ηc​Ξ0subscript𝐺1subscript𝑇11subscript𝐺subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0subscript𝑡subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0G1T11equiv GetacXi0,tetacXi0,etacXi0 in Eq. , which contributes an extra factor of 16 in the distribution in favor also of the Ωb−superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏Omegab- decay. Combining this with the previously mentioned factor of 50 results in an 800-fold enhancement of the Ωb−superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏Omegab- spectrum compared to that of the Ξb0superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0Xib0. On the other hand, the reason for the strong dip seen in the Ξb0superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0Xib0 distribution is the interference between the FSI, Fig. (b), and the tree level process, Fig. (a). Mathematically, the tree level is accounted for by the 1 term in Eq. , which interferes with the G1​T11subscript𝐺1subscript𝑇11G1T11 term from the FSI in that equation. It can be seen in Fig. that at the resonant position, the real part of the G1​T11subscript𝐺1subscript𝑇11G1T11 term coincidentally takes a value very close to −11-1, leading to a destructive interference with the +11+1 term of the tree level mechanism. This dip phenomenon is a well-known feature in scattering theory, typically arising from strong interference between a resonance and a non-resonant background. In fact, if the tree-level contribution was absent, the spectrum for Ξb0superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0Xib0 decay would closely resemble that of Ωb−superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏Omegab- decay, except for the numerical factor 800800800 explained above. Summarizing the previous discussion, we can conclude that the ηc​Ξ0subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0etacXi0 spectrum in the Ωb−→K−​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscript𝐾subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Omegab-to K-etacXi0 exhibits a strength approximately 800 times larger than that in Ξb0→η​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0𝜂subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Xib0toetaetacXi0. Additionally, the Pc​s​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠𝑠Pcss state considered in the present work would appear as a Breit-Wigner-like shape in the former decay, while in the latter, it would manifest as a significant dip. The decay modes under study are chosen as those that can provide the best access to the Pc​s​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠𝑠Pcss states. Another important factor is whether or not these decay processes can be reconstructed and analysed at current or future facilities. The requirement to study beauty baryon decays limits this discussion, at present, to the current and future upgrades of the LHCb experiment, where the proton-proton collisions provide access to all beauty hadrons. Using the Run1 and Run2 datasets, several decay modes of the Ξb(0,−)superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0Xib(0,-) and Ωb−superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏Omegab- baryons have been observedcitebib.bib65, bib.bib64, bib.bib63, bib.bib62, bib.bib61, bib.bib60. However, the sample sizes from Run 1 and Run 2 are unlikely to be sufficient to observe the decays in question and certainly not large enough to enable amplitude analyses, where 𝒪​𝒪1000O candidates are required. These observations are of processes that are well-suited to the strengths of the LHCb experiment, reconstruction of charged tracks of hadrons and muons. We now consider the relative strengths and weaknesses for the decay processes considered here to be detected and reconstructed by the LHCb experiment. • Ξb0→η​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0𝜂subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Xib0toetaetacXi0: This process will be challenging to reconstruct at LHCb due to the presence of neutral particles in the decays of the η𝜂eta (e. g. γ​γ𝛾𝛾gammagamma or π+​π−​π0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋superscript𝜋0pi+pi-pi0) and Ξ0(→Λ0​π0)annotatedsuperscriptΞ0→absentsuperscriptΛ0superscript𝜋0Xi0(toLambda0pi0) mesons. The relatively long-lived Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0 baryon introduces a secondary challenge because it will often decay downstream of the LHCb vertex locator. Such candidates are reconstructed using the downstream tracking detectors only with reduced efficiency. Finally, the ηcsubscript𝜂𝑐etac meson appears in all three final states and can be reconstructed in hadronic final states such as p​p¯𝑝¯𝑝pbarp. • Ωb−→K−​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscript𝐾subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Omegab-to K-etacXi0: Compared to the Ξb0superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0Xib0 decay mode above, the yield of Ωb−superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏Omegab- baryon decays will be suppressed by the ratio of production fractions in the p​p𝑝𝑝pp collisions at the LHC. However, swapping the η𝜂eta meson for a charged kaon brings a large improvement in the reconstruction efficiency and will likely mean that this process is more promising to study first. • Ωb−→KS0​ηc​Ξ−→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞOmegab-to KS0etacXi-: While we have studied the decay Ωb−→K−​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscript𝐾subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Omegab-to K-etacXi0, the Ωb−→K¯0​ηc​Ξ−→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscript¯𝐾0subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞOmegab-tobarK0etacXi- mode, to be seen experimentally in Ωb−→KS0​ηc​Ξ−→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞOmegab-to KS0etacXi-, which has the same strength in our approach due to isospin symmetry, seems the most promising channel to study at the LHCb experiment. Reconstructing this final state requires no neutral particles because the KS0→π+​π−→superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋KS0topi+pi- and Ξ−→Λ0​π−→superscriptΞsuperscriptΛ0superscript𝜋Xi-toLambda0pi- decay modes can be utilised. Similar to the Λ0superscriptΛ0Lambda0 baryon, the KS0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0KS0 meson reconstruction efficiency is lower than for a charged kaon given the two-track final state and long lifetime that means many candidates will decay downstream of the LHCb vertex detector. Nevertheless, considering all of the particles involved, this process should be the most straightforward to study using LHCb data samples. It should also be noted that the branching fractions of all three decay modes are unknown, but given the quark-level processes involved, it is reasonable to expect they are all of similar magnitude and will not be significantly suppressed.
content_sections.IV Conclusions: In light of previous theoretical predictions of several pentaquark states Pc​s​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠𝑠Pcss, with global flavor c¯​c​s​s​n¯𝑐𝑐𝑠𝑠𝑛barccssn, based on formalisms implementing unitary in coupled channels, we have studied the feasibility of seeing the anticipated I​(JP)=12​(12−)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃12superscript12I(JP)=frac12(frac12-) pentaquark in the ηc​Ξ0subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0etacXi0 spectrum in the decays Ξb0→η​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0𝜂subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Xib0toetaetacXi0 and Ωb−→K−​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscript𝐾subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Omegab-to K-etacXi0. The theoretical model employed to generate the pentaquark is based on techniques similar to the chiral unitary approach, aiming to implement unitarity in coupled channels into the meson-baryon scattering amplitudes. The only input of the unitarization procedure is the tree level pseudoscalar-baryon potentials obtained from vector meson exchange mechanisms with Lagrangians derived from appropriate extensions of the local hidden gauge approach to the heavy quark sector. The pentaquark states emerge dynamically as poles in unphysical Riemann sheets of the unitarized pseudoscalar-baryon amplitudes, without the necessity of including them as explicit degrees of freedom. In the present work, the pentaquarks are generated in the Ξb0superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0Xib0 and Ωb−superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏Omegab- decays by implementing the ηc​Ξ0subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0etacXi0 and D¯0​Ωcsuperscript¯𝐷0subscriptΩ𝑐barD0Omegac final state interaction, considering all the possible pseudoscalar-baryon coupled channels significant to generate the pentaquark according to the model. To evaluate the tree level Ξb0superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0Xib0 and Ωb−superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏Omegab- decay amplitudes, necessary before implementing the final state interaction, we identify the dominant mechanisms at the quark level. Subsequently, we implement the hadronization, after the weak transition, incorporating the generation of q¯​q¯𝑞𝑞barqq pairs within the philosophy of the P03superscriptsubscript𝑃033P0 model. The different channels are related by flavor symmetry considerations at the quark level. We provide, up to a global factor, the ηc​Ξ0subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0etacXi0 invariant mass distributions for the Ξb0→η​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0𝜂subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Xib0toetaetacXi0 and Ωb−→K−​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscript𝐾subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Omegab-to K-etacXi0 decays which manifest a large difference in both strength and shape between the two decays. The strength notably favors the Ωb−superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏Omegab- decay by about a factor 800, primarily influenced by flavor and color numerical coefficients in the elementary amplitude. Furthermore, the spectrum for the Ξb0superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0Xib0 decay manifests the resonance as a distinctive dip in its shape, resulting from the interference between non-resonant tree level production and the resonant final state interaction responsible for the pentaquark generation. On the other hand, the Ωb−superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏Omegab- decay leads to a very clear peak resembling a Breit-Wigner distribution. Therefore, for both theoretical and experimental reasons, we recommend searches using the Run3 and Run4 datasets from LHCb to focus initially on searching for Ωb−→KS0​ηc​Ξ−→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞOmegab-to KS0etacXi- decays and studying the different contributing amplitudes to search for the Pc​s​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠𝑠Pcss states. Following this, the focus should first switch to study Ωb−→K−​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏superscript𝐾subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Omegab-to K-etacXi0 decays and then finally to the Ξb0→η​ηc​Ξ0→superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏0𝜂subscript𝜂𝑐superscriptΞ0Xib0toetaetacXi0 process, possibly requiring data samples from the proposed LHCb Upgrade 2 projectcitebib.bib66.

all_papers.title: Heavy flavor molecular states with strangeness
all_papers.abstract:
We proposed a unified framework to describe the interactions of the observed
$T_{cc}$, $P_c$, and $P_{cs}$ within a quark level interaction in our previous
work. In this work, we generalize our framework to the loosely bound hadronic
molecules composed of heavy flavor di-hadrons with strangeness. We predict the
possible $D^{(*)}D^{(*)}_s$ molecular states in the SU(3) limit with the masses
of the $P_c$ states as the inputs. We also investigate the baryon-meson and
baryon-baryon systems and consider the SU(3) breaking effect in their flavor
wave functions. We generalize our isospin criterion of the formation of heavy
flavor di-hadron molecules to the $U/V$ spin case. For a specific heavy flavor
meson-meson, baryon-meson, or baryon-baryon system, the interactions for the
states with the same flavor and spin matrix elements can be related by a
generalized flavor-spin symmetry.

qualitative: The paper proposes a unified quark-level framework to describe loosely bound hadronic molecules composed of heavy flavor di-hadrons with strangeness, extending previous work on Tcc, Pc, and Pcs states.

The interactions in heavy flavor di-hadron systems are primarily mediated by light flavor meson exchange, with the strength determined by the flavor and spin structures of the light degrees of freedom.
A hydrogen-like picture is adopted, where the binding mechanism of exotic states like Tcc, Pc, and Pcs is explained through molecular interactions between heavy di-hadrons.
The framework uses a quark-level Lagrangian to parameterize effective potentials in terms of light quark-quark coupling constants and flavor-spin matrix elements.
The interactions between different heavy flavor di-hadron systems (meson-meson, baryon-meson, baryon-baryon) are related via flavor-spin symmetry, allowing a unified description.
SU(3) flavor symmetry is applied in the limit to predict D(*)D(*)_s molecular states, with masses of the Pc states used as input.
SU(3) breaking effects are considered in the flavor wave functions of baryon-meson and baryon-baryon systems.
The isospin criterion for molecular state formation is generalized to the U/V spin case, extending the symmetry to include strange quarks.

A generalized flavor-spin symmetry is established: for a given heavy flavor system (meson-meson, baryon-meson, baryon-baryon), interactions of states with identical flavor and spin matrix elements are related by this symmetry.
The framework enables a systematic analysis of molecular multiplets across different di-hadron systems, including those with strangeness.
quantitative: The Tcc, Pc, and Pcs states are observed below their respective di-hadron thresholds by several to several tens of MeV, supporting the molecular interpretation.
The effective potentials are parameterized using light quark-quark coupling constants and flavor-spin matrix elements derived from the light degrees of freedom.
The masses of the Pc states are used as input to predict the possible D(*)D(*)_s molecular states in the SU(3) limit.
The paper does not provide specific numerical values for predicted masses of D(*)D(*)_s states or coupling constants, but emphasizes the predictive power of the symmetry framework.
The framework allows for the calculation of binding energies and state properties based on the flavor-spin structure, though specific numbers are not given in the provided text.
